title: High-sensitivity transverse load and liquid level fiber optic sensor based on a Lyot filter with a bend-induced linear birefringence
authors: Ivan Armas-Rivera, Nestor Eduardo Cruz-Ojeda, Manuel Durán-Sánchez, Ponciano Rodríguez-Montero, David Sánchez-De-la-Llave, Baldemar Ibarra-Escamilla
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: We present a numerical and experimental demonstration of a high-sensitivity fiber optic transverse load and liquid level sensor based on bend-induced linear birefringence in a single-mode fiber. The design of the sensor, together with numerical analysis and experimental demonstration, are reported. The proposed sensor consists of a double-pass Lyot filter, consisting of a single-mode fiber segment coiled around a 1.8 cm radius cylinder and a sensitive section of 7.5 cm single-mode bare fiber placed between two thin glass sheets whose birefringence is load-dependent. Numerical simulations show that when the birefringent axes of the coiled fiber and the sensitive section are orthogonal, the central wavelength of minimum transmission shifts toward shorter wavelengths as the load on the sensitive section increases. Sensitivity also improves as the coiled fiber length decreases. Experimentally, coiled fibers of 10, 8, 6, and 4 m were tested for transverse load sensing, yielding sensitivities of -6.41, -8.35, -10.9, and -17.51 nm/N, respectively. For liquid level sensing, we use a 4 m coiled fiber and a 64 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> sensing area providing a sensitivity of -1.3 nm/mm. The maximum resolution obtained were 0.017 N and 0.23 mm for transverse load and liquid level sensors, respectively. Numerical simulations based on the Jones matrix formulation validated these experimental results.

title: Did I Vet You Before? Assessing the Chrome Web Store Vetting Process through Browser Extension Similarity
authors: José Miguel Moreno, Narseo Vallina-Rodriguez, Juan Tapiador
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Web browsers, particularly Google Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers, have grown in popularity over the past decade, with browser extensions becoming an integral part of their ecosystem. These extensions can customize and enhance the user experience, providing functionality that ranges from ad blockers to, more recently, AI assistants. Given the ever-increasing importance of web browsers, distribution marketplaces for extensions play a key role in keeping users safe by vetting submissions that display abusive or malicious behavior. In this paper, we characterize the prevalence of malware and other infringing extensions in the Chrome Web Store (CWS), the largest distribution platform for this type of software. To do so, we introduce SIMEXT, a novel methodology for detecting similarly behaving extensions that leverages static and dynamic analysis, Natural Language Processing (NLP) and vector embeddings. Our study reveals significant gaps in the CWS vetting process, as 86% of infringing extensions are extremely similar to previously vetted items, and these extensions take months or even years to be removed. By characterizing the top kinds of infringing extension, we find that 83% are New Tab Extensions (NTEs) and raise some concerns about the consistency of the vetting labels assigned by CWS analysts.

title: Emergence of Adaptive Collective Evasion Through Density-Based Interactions in Swarm Robotics_supp1-3675932.mp4
authors: Xingguang Peng
publish: N/A
abstract: Collective evasion is critical in swarm robotics, enabling groups of simple robots to effectively elude external threats. Previous work on anti-predator behaviors relied on rigid evasion patterns coupled with velocity alignment, which lack adaptability when confronted with sustained and varied predatory attacks and further degrade anti-predation performance. Achieving adaptive collective evasion without predefined evasion rules and explicit velocity alignment is underexplored in swarm robotics. Here, we propose a density-based swarm model for adaptive collective evasion driven by field contextual sensing derived from positional information. By leveraging instantaneous density gradients and temporal fluctuations in density field, our approach enables the emergence of the fountain maneuver, wherein the group exhibits rapid spreading under predation followed by regrouping behind the threat. Compared to coordinated turning and rigid fountain maneuvers, our approach demonstrates better anti-predation performance through more dynamic and longer sustained fountain maneuvers under continuous attacks in both simulations and Human-In-The-Loop predation experiments with real robots, highlighting the practical advantages of adaptive collective evasion in swarm robotics.

title: Robust Federated Learning With Double Denoising_supp1-3673539.pdf
authors: Siguang Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Federated learning (FL), as a representative distributed learning paradigm, has achieved remarkable success. However, most existing FL studies assume that each client holds correctly labeled data, whereas in reality, noisy labels are ubiquitous on the client side. To mitigate the adverse impact of noisy labels on FL performance, we propose a robust FL method with double denoising. Specifically, we first perform primary denoising based on the idea of cross-prediction, where two global models are trained and mutually used on each client to identify and filter out mislabeled samples. Next, after fine-tuning the models, we develop secondary denoising by detecting and removing residual noisy samples through clustering based on loss values. In addition, we design a noise-tolerant local training strategy that dynamically assesses the influence of noisy data on local updates and applies differentiated update rules to prevent overfitting. Finally, experimental results on three benchmark datasets, including the real-world noisy dataset Clothing1M, demonstrate that our method effectively removes label noise, delivering improved performance of the global model.

title: Symmetry-Optimized Image Guidance and Biomechanics-Driven Force Measurement for Robotic Ultrasound: A Control Framework Enhancing Sonographic Quality
authors: Yizhao Qian, Jiayuan Luo, Yameng Zhang, Kai Long, Yixuan Yuan, Max Q.-H. Meng, Li Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Robotic ultrasound systems (RUSS) face significant challenges in maintaining optimal image quality and contact force across complex, deformable anatomical structures. This paper proposes a control framework integrating symmetry-optimized image guidance with biomechanics-driven force measurement. We introduce a Column Similarity (CS) method for rapid in-plane tracking and a Young-Laplace (Y-L) model to predict surface curvature changes for out-of-plane adaptation. To ensure clinical safety during autonomous operation, a dual-layer protection mechanism combining global force thresholds with adaptive image-gain limits is integrated. To validate the efficiency of the proposed visual algorithm, the in-plane module was tested on an embedded platform (NVIDIA Jetson), achieving a processing rate of 66 Hz. Extensive in-vivo experiments were conducted on highly heterogeneous anatomical structures, including the curvature-rich supraclavicular region and the deformable abdominal aorta. Results demonstrate that the proposed framework elevates a composite Image Quality Score (IQS) by 27.2% compared to conventional methods, with the gap between proposed method and human expert within 5%. Furthermore, the autonomous system completes in-vivo abdominal aorta volumetric scans in 49 s, achieving vessel reconstruction quality comparable to manual scanning by expert sonographers (Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) 0.85, Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD) 2.9 mm). The findings confirm the system’s capability for high-precision, autonomous sonography in diverse clinical scenarios.

title: A Novel Design of Low-PAPR OFDM-DCSK Waveform with Riemann Matrix-Based G-SLM for ISAC
authors: Cheng Peng, Yanhua Tan, Dingfei Ma, Zhaojie Yang, Chi Zhang, Yi Fang
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This letter introduces an orthogonal frequency division multiplexing differential chaos shift keying (OFDM-DCSK) waveform for communication-centric integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), which simultaneously enables reliable noncoherent communication and robust sensing capabilities. To further address the high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) issue of OFDM-based waveforms, a Riemann matrix–based group selected mapping (G-SLM) scheme is incorporated to reduce PAPR under ISAC sensing constraints. The proposed G-SLM achieves significant PAPR improvement compared with conventional SLM methods while requiring no side information at the receiver. Closed-form expressions are derived for the ambiguity function and bit error rate (BER) in the proposed systems with and without G-SLM. Both analytical and simulation results verify that the OFDM-DCSK waveform integrated with G-SLM maintains communication reliability and sensing accuracy compared to the conventional OFDM-DCSK waveform, while reducing the PAPR with only slight main lobes broadening and modest side lobes elevation. These features make the proposed scheme particularly suitable for ISAC applications in complex wireless environments under low and moderate mobility conditions.

title: TSF-Mlstm: A Spatiotemporal Feature-Enhanced Monitoring Method for Coalmine Pressure Prediction
authors: Jian Cheng, Linhao Nie, Si-Yu Chen, Yakun Wang, Zhaoming Yang, Yi Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate and reliable monitoring of coalmine pressure is critical for ensuring safe underground operations and preventing catastrophic roof collapse. However, coalmine pressure signals are inherently complex and highly variable, often affected by geological heterogeneity, mining disturbances, and sensor noise, leading to significant challenges in acquiring stable and interpretable measurements. In this study, we present a novel measurement enhancement framework for coalmine pressure monitoring, which augments traditional hydraulic support sensors with intelligent signal modeling and spatiotemporal feature alignment mechanisms. Unlike conventional approaches that focus solely on raw sensor outputs, our method acts as a data-driven post-measurement refinement module, designed to mitigate noise interference, model temporal-spatial dependencies, and provide real-time prediction feedback for early warning systems. The proposed framework integrates a multidimensional measurement modeling strategy, in which a Mamba-based long-sequence module is fused with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to form a hybrid signal processing pipeline. This architecture enables the system to learn deep temporal-spatial patterns embedded in noisy and nonstationary measurement sequences, thus enhancing data stability, reliability, and interpretability. Rather than replacing traditional sensors, our method complements and extends their functions by enabling intelligent post-measurement analysis and predictive forecasting, thereby reinforcing the practical utility of existing instrumentation in harsh underground environments. Experimental validation on real-world coalmine hydraulic support monitoring datasets shows that the proposed framework consistently outperforms traditional measurementdriven forecasting baselines across various prediction horizons. Specifically, the proposed TSF-Mlstm achieves relative MAE improvements of 8.19%, 9.67%, 8.00%, 10.60%, 12.52%, and 12.05% over the baseline methods. In addition, compared with three recent hybrid Mamba–LSTM variants, it further reduces the average MAE by 8.22%, 11.55%, and 14.55%, demonstrating its stronger long-range forecasting stability. These quantitative improvements demonstrate the superior predictive stability of our system under complex and noisy underground conditions. The proposed work thus contributes a viable direction toward intelligent coalmine measurement enhancement and supports early risk detection through advanced sensor data modeling.

title: Research on Deep Learning-Based Optimization Strategy for Power System Scheduling
authors: Haoxun Liu, Ziyan Li, Yuzhen Dang, Fuliang Ma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: This paper proposes a deep learning-based optimization strategy for power system scheduling, aiming to enhance load forecasting accuracy, optimize scheduling decisions, and reduce operational costs. A combination of LSTM and CNN deep learning models is used for load forecasting, and multi-source data (such as load, weather, and renewable energy) is used for training. The experimental results show that the deep learning hybrid model outperforms traditional methods in load forecasting, data processing efficiency, demand response management, and scheduling cost optimization. This study provides an innovative optimization solution for power system scheduling in the era of smart grids, with significant application value for ensuring the stable, safe, and economic operation of power systems.

title: A Novel Method of Predicting the Output Characteristic Curves of Trench-Gate IGBTs with CNN
authors: Xiaoxiao Shi, Jingcheng Tang, Zhihang Ma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: Trench-gate Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs) are widely used in power conversion systems, electric vehicles, and renewable energy inverters. However, the strong nonlinear coupling between device geometry and electrical behavior makes accurate performance prediction difficult. To overcome this limitation, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is developed to predict the output characteristic curves of trench-gate IGBTs from simulation data. Using six structural and doping parameters as feature inputs together with voltage sampling points as conditional inputs, the CNN achieves a mean squared error (MSE) of 1.19, a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.464, and a coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex>) of 0.049 on the test set. Considering all metrics, the CNN exhibits balanced and stable performance compared with others.

title: Genetic Algorithm-Tuned CNN–SVM Hybrid Model for Early and Accurate Detection of Breast Cancer
authors: Shaik Akbar, E. Vijayakumar, Soni Singh, Nihar Ranjan Behera, S. Padma Devi, Priyadarshini S
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Breast cancer remains one of the leading causes of mortality among women worldwide, necessitating early and precise detection methods. Traditional models often struggle with imbalanced data, overfitting, and limited generalization. To address these challenges, this study proposes a Genetic Algorithm-Tuned CNN–SVM Hybrid Model that integrates the deep feature learning ability of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with the classification power of Support Vector Machines (SVM), while using Genetic Algorithms (GA) for optimal hyperparameter tuning. The GA effectively enhances convergence and minimizes computational overhead, enabling adaptive learning across diverse breast cancer imaging datasets. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance compared to existing models, achieving Accuracy of 98.7%, Precision of 98.4%, Recall of 98.6%, and F1-score of 98.5%. The proposed hybrid model not only ensures reliable cancer detection but also offers scalability and adaptability, making it a promising tool for clinical diagnostic systems and personalized healthcare decision support.

title: Integrating Ontology with Abstract Behavioural Specification for Automated Business Logic in Software Product Lines
authors: Venkata Madhu Prateek Reddy Kambala
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Software Product Lines (SPL) offer a promising approach to accelerate development processes while enhancing product quality and reducing production costs. Central to SPL is the feature diagram, which delineates the relationships between features within a domain, capturing both commonalities and variabilities. Despite its advantages, the implementation of SPL in information system development remains insufficiently explored. This paper investigates the integration of ontology within the SPL framework, particularly through the lens of executable modeling languages like Abstract Behavioural Specification (ABS). We introduce the ABS Microservices Framework, which extends ABS capabilities to accommodate variabilities in microservices-based architectures. Our proposed adaptor effectively integrates ABS microservices with ontology-based information systems, enabling the automation of business logic generation. By leveraging ontology, we ensure that the system maintains a semantically structured foundation, allowing for automatic updates of the necessary business logics. This integration not only enhances the adaptability and efficiency of information systems but also positions ontology as a critical component in the evolution of SPL methodologies.

title: ShieldNetMapper: A Predictive Model for Real-Time Network Threat Detection and Response
authors: S.Thangamayan, Nandhu.B, Nithyashri.N.B, Gohulatharani. R, Hema Shree. S, Shri.K
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: In the evolving landscape of cyber threats, real-time and accurate threat detection is paramount to securing digital infrastructure. This study presents ShieldNetMapper, a predictive deep learning approach to real-time network threat detection and automated response. The model combines a 1D-CNN 1D Convolutional Neural Networks for spatial features extraction architecture with Bi-LSTM Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory for temporal pattern recognition and an attention mechanism to focus on dynamic threats. Utilizing labeled UNSW-NB15 dataset that includes various patterns of attack using packet-level traffic data and enriched log sources (firewall logs, IDS/IPS alerts, and NetFlow statistics), the model is trained. Preprocessing includes session reconstruction and categorical encoding and numerical scaling and threat enrichment is performed using contextual graph mapping and threat intelligence feeds. Empirical testing shows that such an approach is superior to both a traditional approach and state-of-the-art models achieving 97.48% accuracy, 96.71% F1-score, and 98.23% AUC-ROC, significantly better in detection precision and recall. The framework is implemented with Kafka-based real-time inference and SOAR-backed threat response automation. The results validate ShieldNetMapper as a reliable solution for proactive network defence with explainable outputs, rapid inference and low false positive rates, therefore opening the door for deployment in enterprise and critical infrastructure systems.

title: Simulation of Cumulative Deformation of Power Transformer Windings Under Reclosing Short-Circuit Shock
authors: Rui Xu, Jiawei Yi, Xiaolu Lin, Wenhu Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This paper presents a systematic study on the cumulative deformation of power transformer windings under shortcircuit conditions, including multiple reclosing events. A coupled field-circuit finite element model of a three-phase dual-winding transformer was developed to simulate transient short-circuit currents and the resulting electromagnetic forces. The elasticplastic model of copper windings was used to predict cumulative deformation under repeated short-circuit impacts. Simulation results show that three-phase symmetrical short-circuit currents are the highest, and repeated short-circuit shocks significantly increase plastic deformation in the windings. These findings offer valuable insights for transformer design, short-circuit withstand assessment, and protection strategy development.

title: DistFNE: A Distributed-Memory Algorithm for Force-Directed Node Embedding
authors: Isuru Ranawaka, Ariful Azad
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: We develop a distributed-memory algorithm to embed nodes of a graph into a low-dimensional vector space. Our distributed algorithm, called DistFNE, is based on a force-directed layout that maximizes attraction among neighboring vertices and repulsion among distant ones. DistFNE utilizes large minibatches in stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to scale node embeddings to thousands of processors without compromising the quality of the embeddings. DistFNE optimizes memory usage and inter-process communication through a configurable push-pull strategy. We employ asynchronous MPI communication to overlap communication and computation. These combined techniques made DistFNE scalable to 32,768 cores of supercomputers to generate embeddings of graphs with billions of edges.

title: A Co-Accelerated Framework for Integrated Power-Compute Scheduling in Multi-Data-Center Systems
authors: Yahan Dong, Xu Wang, Jun Ke, Payman Dehghanian, Zhaohao Ding
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: To boost the market participation and scheduling efficiency of dispersed data centers, we propose a Data Center Aggregator (DCA) that unifies multiple small sites under one operator. We develop an integrated model in which workload dynamics drive a DVFS-based power mapping, augmented by PV–ESS regulation of supply, to design a flexible scheduling scheme. The problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization model: an upper-level DRL agent selects foresighted actions shaping future energy states, while a lower-level mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solver makes short-term, cost-optimal decisions. This coordination links long-horizon planning with real-time operation. Simulations show the efficacy of the proposed approach in reducing total costs and improving server utilization and energy efficiency, outperforming traditional optimization and DRL methods with a full action space.

title: Joint Type Diagnosis and Severity Assessment of Partial Discharge in Gas-Insulated Switchgear via Multi-Task Learning
authors: Xichao Tang, Chunyan Zhang, Jianlei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: Partial discharge (PD) is a key indicator of insulation health in gas-insulated switchgear (GIS). It is widely used for condition monitoring in high-voltage power systems. However, the diverse intensities and underlying fault mechanisms of PD often result in complex and variable signal patterns. Traditional single-task learning approaches struggle to capture this complexity and generalize across multiple diagnostic objectives. To address these challenges, we propose PD-MTNet, a multi-task learning framework for simultaneous PD type diagnosis and severity assessment. The framework adopts an attention-enhanced lightweight backbone to extract robust shared representations, tailored to the multi-scale and sparse nature of PRPD data. By highlighting salient discharge regions and suppressing background responses, the backbone effectively captures physically meaningful features across phase-amplitude scales. A multi-path hybrid module is then employed to decouple task-specific features according to their diagnostic mechanisms, facilitating more discriminative representations for each task. This design, together with the shared feature learning strategy, enhances intra-class compactness and inter-class separability. This design improves robustness to ambiguous boundaries and noisy labels, resulting in more stable and physically consistent diagnostic performance. Experimental results demonstrate that PD-MTNet achieves classification accuracies of 97.92% for PD type diagnosis and 95.19% for severity assessment, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability in complex PD diagnostic scenarios.

title: SAM-Based Multi-Scale Fine-Tuning with Inter-layer Difference Guidance for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Leiquan Wang, Yu Meng, Chunbo Luo, Mingming Xu, Chunlei Wu, Zhongwei Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Visual Foundation Models (VFMs) have attracted growing interest in recent years. However, a significant domain gap between natural and remote sensing imagery—particularly in terms of scale variation—remains a core challenge in remote sensing change detection (CD). In addition, many existing methods rely on single-level difference features and lack the ability to propagate change representations across network layers. To address these limitations, we propose IDRG-mSAM, a SAM-based multi-scale fine-tuning framework with Inter-layer Difference Representation and Guidance, for remote sensing CD. Our approach first leverages the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as a general-purpose backbone, utilizing its strong capacity for dense prediction tasks. To bridge the domain gap and capture multi-scale context, we introduce a Multi-Scale Adapter, which enhances feature expressiveness by modeling contextual dependencies at various spatial resolutions. Moreover, we design an Inter-layer Difference Representation and Guidance Module (IDRGM) to facilitate progressive interaction of bi-temporal features. This module propagates difference cues across network depths, enhancing multi-level difference representation and improving the quality of bi-temporal image features. Extensive experiments on three widely used benchmark datasets, LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD and CLCD, demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our method, achieving consistently superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

title: LLM-Enhanced Failure Localization in Microservices: Integrating Multi-Modal Data and Expert Interpretation
authors: Zhenyu Zhong, Ruowei Fu, Minghua Ma, Shenglin Zhang, Yongqian Sun, Chetan Bansal, Dan Pei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Failure localization in microservice environments is increasingly challenging. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in software engineering tasks, existing approaches struggle to effectively integrate multi-modal telemetry data (e.g., log, metric and trace) and provide interpretable results. This paper presents LocaleXpert, a novel failure localization system that combines specialized LLM-based agents with traditional AIOps methods to diagnose issues in microservice environments. LocaleXpert introduces three key innovations: (1) a modular pipeline that transforms metrics, logs, and traces into natural language descriptions that LLMs can effectively process, (2) specialized expert agents that analyze each data type and collaborate to identify root causes, and (3) an interpretation mechanism that produces clear, actionable explanations of its reasoning process. Evaluation results show that LocaleXpert significantly outperforming baseline approaches in both accuracy and interpretability. The system has been successfully deployed in Microsoft's AIOpsLab benchmark, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Beyond Warning Lists: How to Uncover Patterns in Python Static Code Warnings Using Association Rule Mining
authors: Gudrun Schappacher-Tilp, Jan Kornberger, Nicoletta Kaehling, Kevin Beichler, Egon Teiniker
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: Static analysis tools such as Pylint generate hundreds or thousands of warnings in Python projects. While each warning is useful in isolation, it is far less clear how these warnings relate to one another or what broader patterns they form across a codebase. This article presents a practical method for uncovering such structures in Pylint outputs using association rule mining. By treating each source file as a transaction and warning codes as items, we identify recurring cooccurrence patterns, directional relationships, and warning “hubs” that consistently appear across multiple repositories. We demonstrate how association-rule mining exposes relationships among warnings that are not visible in flat lists, enabling deeper analysis of static code quality data. Using examples from real-world Python projects, we highlight how this approach enriches the interpretation of static analysis results for developers, technical leads, and educators.

title: High-Performance SOT-MRAM with 52 ppm Raw Bit Error Rate and 1 ppm Write Error Rate via Novel Writing Scheme on 300 mm Wafers
authors: Yi Xu, Chao Wang, Dinggui Zeng, Yang Gao, Zhenghui Ji, Yaoyu Gu, Xin Cao, Zhou Li, Shasha Wang, Ting Fu, Tiejun Zhou, Yihui Sun, Qijun Guo, Shikun He, Guchang Han, Bo Liu
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Back-hopping type soft errors and etching-induced shunting failure are among the primary failure mechanisms preventing spin-orbit torque magnetic random access memory (SOT-MRAM) technology from commercialization. In this work, a novel writing scheme is proposed, which effectively suppresses back-hopping type soft failures, achieving a writing error rate below 1 ppm and a significantly widened write margin for enhanced reliability. Meanwhile, by inserting a buffer layer between the free layer and the SOT layer, we demonstrated significantly reduced shunting failure, yield improvement, and a raw bit error rate down to 52 ppm on 300 mm wafers. Additionally, a high-yield magnetic tunnel junction with a tunnel magnetoresistance (TMR) of 134% is demonstrated through material and stack optimization. These progresses pave the way for the development of high-density SOT-MRAM chips with Mbit~Gbit capacities.

title: Women-in-IES Luncheon at IECON 2025 in Madrid [Women in IES News]
authors: Jing Zhou
publish: IEEE Industrial Electronics Magazine
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Non Multiplexed SQUID Readout Electronics for Large Detector Arrays
authors: Manuel Gonzalez, Damien Prêle, Bao Ton, Galen O'Neil, Avirup Roy, Kazuhiro Sakai, Simon Bandler
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Large arrays of sensors operating at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{m}\mathrm{K}$</tex-math></inline-formula> temperatures are desirable for applications including X-ray satellites missions. Proposed designs for these arrays often rely on multiplexing methods which read out many sensors while requiring less than one additional wire for every additional sensor. Here, we consider the possibility of non-multiplexed readout of a 10,000 pixel sensor array, where each pixel has an individual DC SQUID read out with an individual analog feedback circuit. We focus on the total power dissipation of a non-multiplexed readout. We present an ASIC-compatible SQUID feedback circuit meeting proposed noise and bandwidth requirements that dissipates only <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10 \,\mathrm{m}\mathrm{W}$</tex-math></inline-formula> per channel, or a total of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100 \,\mathrm{W}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for 10,000 channels. We show that total power consumption cannot be reduced by operating this circuit at the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$75 \,\mathrm{K}$</tex-math></inline-formula> temperature stage of a cryostat. We predict the power load from 60,000 wires running to the base temperature of a cryostat, and show that with resistances compatible with the feedback circuit and feasible with current commercial capabilities that the power loads are below the cooling capacity of a cryostat proposed for an X-ray satellite mission.

title: Development of a Sensor Array and Data Visualisation Platform to Detect Active Steel Corrosion
authors: I. Carotti, J.A. Covington, D. Billson, D. Hutchins
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Corrosion is a major issue across many industries, requiring continuous monitoring, prevention, and repair to avert catastrophic failures. This study investigated the development of a portable sensor array capable of detecting airborne corrosion by-products, enabling simple early-stage corrosion detection. An experimental setup was designed to look at the volatile compounds outputs of mild steel in a corrosive environment in a sealed PTFE bottle. A second sensor array was deployed with mild steel under non-corrosive conditions over the same one-week period for comparison. Data were analysed using principal component analysis (PCA) for visualisation, with the longer-term aim of using the collected dataset to train machine-learning models for future deployment. The sensors were selected for their ability to detect volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and some gases, both of which may be associated with corroding steel. Clear separation was observed between the day-by-day progression of corrosion and the non-rusting samples, with particularly strong discrimination from the VOC sensors.

title: Effective Activated Area and Power Scaling Laws of XL-RIS: How Large RIS do We Need?
authors: Ke Zhao, Zhiqun Song, Yong Li, Xingjian Li, Lizhe Liu, Bin Wang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) represents a promising technology due to its ability to create a controllable wireless propagation environment. However, this advantage can only be realized when the passive RIS is of a sufficiently large size, for which the conventional uniform plane wave (UPW)-based far-field model may become invalid. In this paper, we consider the directional reflecting element and establish an extremely large-scale RIS (XL-RIS) signal transmission model that incorporates distance/angle correlation based on the non-uniform spherical wave (NUSW). Based on the proposed model, we first analyze the optimal rotation angle of the RIS in both far-field and near-field scenarios, concluding that optimal system performance can be achieved when the angle of arrival (AoA) and angle of departure (AoD) are equal through the rotation of the RIS. Then, we introduce the concept of the effective activated area and analyze the number of effective activated elements of the XL-RIS under narrow beam incidence, and the relationship between transmission distance and the optimal number of reflecting elements is obtained.We also analyze the asymptotic performance and convergence characteristics of the system. Theoretical results indicate that the relationship between the quadratic of the XL-RIS element number and the received signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is nonlinear in the near-field. Numerical results validate our analysis and demonstrate the necessity of an appropriate XL-RIS transmission model within the effective activated area.

title: High-Frequency Ultrasound Micro-Transducer for Skin Imaging Based on PMN-0.30PT Single Crystal
authors: Yili Hu, Xiaobing Li, Zhaoping Yang, Xingfei Chen, Xi Tang, Honglin Zhu, Jun Chen, Jie Jiao, Di Lin, Shengdong Nie, Changjiang Zhou
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Pb(Mg<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/3</sub>Nb<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2/3</sub>)O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3-x</sub>PbTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> (PMN-PT) single crystals, characterized by an ultrahigh piezoelectric response, are well suited for transducers used in medical ultrasound imaging. A high-frequency ultrasound transducer with a center frequency above 45 MHz was designed and fabricated based on a high-performance PMN-0.30PT single crystal. A bandwidth of 58.3% @−6dB was measured at ultra-high frequency after the size effect had been overcome in the micro-transducer. An axial resolution of 36 μm was achieved in B-mode imaging of a tungsten-wire phantom, and animal-skin imaging further confirmed the transducer capability. These results indicate that the PMN-0.30PT single crystal is a promising material for high-frequency medical ultrasound imaging applications.

title: Towards Adaptive User Interface Design Strategies for Older Adults: A Thematic Analysis_supp1-3675691.pdf

title: Analysis and Modeling of SNR Degradation for Adjacent Signals Under High-Power Interference in YIG Frequency-Selective Limiters
authors: Xinan Fan, Qinghui Yang, Yanhui Fan, Feng Wang, Shanshan Du, Ming Wang, Huaiwu Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This study investigates the impact of a high-power interference on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of an adjacent useful signal when both are fed into an yttrium iron garnet frequency-selective limiter (YIG FSL) in a white noise environment. By building a dedicated test platform for systematic measurements, it is experimentally found that the signal SNR can be weakly optimized (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 1$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB and weakened with increasing frequency deviation) when the interference power is slightly higher than the limiting threshold, while it leads to deterioration of the SNR when the interference power is sufficiently high. Furthermore, we develop a theoretical model to explain the phenomenon and finally reveal a clear dependence between SNR enhancement or deterioration and interference strength and frequency offset.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Lab-Scale Type-IV WECS With Sliding Mode Controller by Emulating Wind Turbine
authors: Abhishek Kumar, Prashant Surana, Anubrata Dey
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a laboratory prototype wind turbine emulator for a Type-IV Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS). This setup employs a DC motor coupled with a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG), interfaced to the grid through a three level Neutral Point Clamped (NPC) back-to-back converter. Optimal Torque Control (OTC) combined with a Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Controller (ST-SMC) is employed for machine-side current regulation to ensure maximum power extraction. On the grid side, a robust ST-SMC-based DC-link voltage controller is designed for effective control of the Grid-Side Converter (GSC). In addition, a novel ST-SMC-based neutral-point balancing strategy is proposed for the NPC converter, offering faster convergence and stronger disturbance rejection than conventional methods. Experimental results demonstrate that the ST-SMC limits DC-link overshoot to 25% during reference step changes (compared to 80% with PI control) and to 11% under wind variations (compared to 25% with PI). The neutral-point balancing scheme achieves a convergence time of 260 ms, significantly outperforming existing approaches. Furthermore, when applied jointly for DC-link regulation and neutral-point balancing, the proposed method yields the lowest total harmonic distortion (THD) of 3.8%, confirming its superior performance over traditional PI control.

title: A Rapid Restoration Strategy for Distribution Networks Considering the Optimization of Mobile Energy Storage Pre-Deployment
authors: Bingqian Chen, Naixuan Zhu, Nuoling Sun, Hao Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Against the backdrop of global climate change, the intensity and frequency of extreme weather events are showing an increasing trend, which can easily trigger large-scale blackouts in distribution networks. There is an urgent need to enhance their rapid recovery capability after disasters. Focusing on large-scale distribution network outage scenarios caused by extreme weather, this paper adopts a stochastic programming method to account for the uncertainty of line faults under extreme weather and coordinates various distributed resources to restore critical loads. For typical line fault scenarios, a stochastic programming optimization model is constructed to incorporate multiple potential line fault scenarios and their impacts into a unified analytical framework. The model aims to minimize the system's load loss and operating costs by optimizing the pre-allocation locations and quantities of mobile energy storage, thereby efficiently coordinating distributed resources to achieve rapid power supply restoration for critical loads. Firstly, addressing the uncertainty of fault scenarios under extreme weather, a fault scenario model based on stochastic programming is established to fully adapt to the complex characteristics of distribution network disaster damage scenarios. Secondly, focusing on the dual-objective optimization of load loss and operating costs, the precise configuration of mobile energy storage pre-allocation strengthens the power supply matching efficiency between distributed resources and critical loads. Finally, simulation analysis conducted on the IEEE 33-node distribution network verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results show that the disaster-scenario-adapted mobile energy storage pre-allocation strategy can lead to a significant decreasing trend in both the overall load loss level and operating costs of the distribution network, effectively enhancing the distribution network's resilience to extreme weather and providing support for optimal disaster prevention resource allocation and rapid distribution network restoration.

title: A Cross-Voltage Feedforward Compensation Method for Improving the Transient Stability of PLL-Synchronized Converters
authors: Jingbo Dong, Jiawei Zhao, Leming Zhou, Tinglong Zhou, Kexin Wei
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: To enhance the transient stability of grid-connected voltage source converters (VSCs), this paper establishes an analytical model that treats the VSC-PLL system as analogous to the rotor motion of a synchronous generator (SG) during grid voltage sags. The model reveals the dominant influence of the equivalent “mechanical torque” and equivalent “damping” on the transient stability region. Building upon this analysis, a cross-voltage feedforward compensation (CVFC) method is proposed. This method derives a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$q$</tex>-axis voltage feedforward compensation term from the transient deviation of the point of common coupling (PCC) d-axis voltage. The objective is to rapidly reduce the equivalent “mechanical torque” and enhance the equivalent “damping”, thereby suppressing phase-angle oscillations, enlarging the stability margin, and improving transient stability. Finally, comparative simulation results are presented to verify the validity of the theoretical analysis and the effectiveness of the proposed strategy in enhancing the transient stability of the VSC-PLL system.

title: Small Signal Stability Analysis of VSG and Synchronous Condenser Parallel System
authors: Xining Liu, Haibo Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Considering stability problem brought by the new energy, synchronous condenser is usually installed on the side of new energy power stations to improve voltage maintenance capability. However, when new energy station controlled by VSG is connected in parallel with synchronous condenser, stability will be reduced under small disturbance. In this paper, the authors establish different small-signal models of parallel system of VSG and synchronous condenser, and conclude that the paralleling VSG and synchronous condenser reduces system stability. In addition, the authors explore the effect of the virtual inductance on the stability by characteristic roots analysis, and conclude that increasing the virtual inductance appropriately is beneficial to the stability of the parallel system, but if it is increased too much, the stability will be reduced or even lead to instability. The simulation verifies the correctness of the conclusion.

title: An Accelerator for Low-Computational Overhead Privacy-Preserving GNN Inference
authors: Heonhui Jung, Whoi Ree Ha, Kevin Nam, Youyeon Joo, Lucas Oros, Yunheung Paek
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly used in domains such as finance and bioinformatics, where both node features and edge structures can contain sensitive information. While Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) offers a promising solution for privacy-preserving GNN inference, existing approaches such as PPGNN rely on costly Homomorphic Rotation and MUX operations for operand obfuscation, resulting in significant computational overhead. In this work, we propose a new obfuscation method that leverages the probabilistic nature of FHE to duplicate ciphertexts at the client side, thereby eliminating the need for runtime selection logic. To support this method efficiently, we design a pipelined hardware accelerator with a simplified CKKS datapath and parallel TFHE execution, avoiding the complexity of rotation-heavy designs. Despite reduced ciphertext reuse, our architecture mitigates memory pressure through buffer-aware PBS unit design. Experimental results demonstrate up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.8 \times$</tex> speedup and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.69 \times$</tex> energy efficiency improvement over PPGNN, while also outperforming existing multi-scheme accelerators such as Trinity and UFC even when applying the same obfuscation strategy. Our approach offers a practical and scalable solution for efficient, privacy-preserving GNN inference.

title: Predicting Executability and Performance of CNN Kernels on Tenstorrent Hardware Using Machine Learning
authors: Param Gandhi, Sharvil Potdar, Nayan Gogari, Gargi Alavani Prabhu, Sankar Manoj, Santonu Sarkar
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Maximizing the performance of deep learning models on AI accelerators like Tenstorrent Wormhole requires precise control over hardware resources such as compute cores and onchip memory. Operations such as convolution expose a range of tunable parameters, such as parallelization strategies, buffer sizes, compute datatypes, and memory hierarchies (SRAM vs. DRAM) that involve trade-offs between performance, memory usage, and numerical accuracy. Tenstorrent's TT-NN API reimplements PyTorch's conv2d while exposing all these lowlevel controls, allowing fine-grained control, but presenting a steep learning curve for users familiar with PyTorch's high-level abstractions. We propose a predictive software layer that facilitates the execution of conv2d on Tenstorrent hardware by modeling the relationship between input tensors, configuration parameters, and execution outcomes. We train two machine learning models: one predicts execution success with 99.82% accuracy, and the other estimates run-time performance with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}$</tex> score of 0.9984. These models enable automated hardware-aware tuning (TuneNet) of the TT-NN configuration space, reducing trial and error, avoiding out-of-memory errors, and improving performance. TuneNetselected configurations reduce conv2d latency by 13.29% compared to TT-NN defaults on Tenstorrent Wormhole N150s, while incurring minimal computational overhead (9s). This work introduces the first hardware-aware autotuning pipeline for Tenstorrent accelerators, significantly reducing tuning overhead while improving performance.

title: Differential Evolution-Based Parameter Estimation for Infrared Interferometric Thickness Measurement
authors: Siyan Jin, Ye Li, Meifeng Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: The performance and yield of high-power silicon carbide (SiC) devices are critically dependent on precise control of epitaxial film thickness, driving the need for accurate and nondestructive metrology. To address the limitations of conventional infrared interferometry—namely, sensitivity to noise and the propensity of parameter inversion to settle in local optima—this study proposes a hybrid framework that integrates physical modeling with intelligent optimization. Dual-beam interference modeling, coupled with Sellmeier dispersion modeling (i.e., wavelength-dependent refractive index fitting using the Sellmeier equation), forms the physical basis. This is enhanced by segmented Savitzky-Golay preprocessing for noise suppression and a differential evolution algorithm for the global optimization of key parameters, including thickness and refractive indices. Experimental results validated the framework's nanometer-level accuracy, strong robustness, and excellent repeatability. This approach provides a reliable pathway for high-precision thickness measurement of SiC epitaxial films, facilitating the industrial adoption of infrared interferometry in advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Optimization of Diamond Nitrogen-Vacancy Center Magnetic Measurement System Sensitivity Through Synergistic Laser and Microwave Power Control
authors: Yaoyao Deng, Dongmei Liu, Zhen Cheng, Haoliang Hu, Xiaofei Li, Li Yuan, Yiwen Liang, Junchang Huang, Qi Nie, Yuzhuo Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: The sensitivity limit of diamond NV center magnetometry using continuous-wave ODMR (CW-ODMR) relies on three spectral parameters: full width at half maximum (FWHM), contrast, and the fluorescence detection rate. These parameters are coregulated by microwave and laser power, which requires combined optimization. We built a diamond NV center magnetic measurement platform and developed a dual-parameter regulation model. Through orthogonal experiments and Lorentzian spectral fitting, we systematically analyzed the correlation characteristics between the power parameter combinations and system sensitivity. Under the optimized parameter combination for a microwave power of 1 dBm and laser power of 82.14 mW, the system achieved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.30 \text{nT} / \text{Hz}^{1 / 2}$</tex> sensitivity, which approached the theoretical limit. With frequency-modulation demodulation, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.35 \text{nT} / \text{Hz}^{1 / 2}$</tex> sensitivity was practically realized. This work offers practical guidelines for power optimization in NV center magnetic measurement systems.

title: LLM-Based Hate Speech Detection in Malayalam-English Code-Mixed Memes
authors: Dhanya L K, Kannan Balakrishnan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: It’s becoming more important to find hate speech on social media as damaging content becomes more common. Detecting hate speech in code-mixed and low-resource languages like Malayalam-English is still hard, even if English and other resource-rich languages have made a lot of progress. When it comes to multimodal content like memes, the job gets increasingly harder because translation errors, spelling issues, or subtle insults might hide inappropriate sentiments. In this study, we introduce a training-free approach for identifying hate speech in Malayalam-English code-mixed memes by leveraging the zero-shot and few-shot capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). For memes, our method comprises a pretreatment pipeline that is aware of code mixing and uses optical character recognition (OCR), transliteration, and token-level language identification to get the text right. We test multimodal LLMs like CLIP, BLIP-2, and LLaVA by using descriptive, contextual, and instruction-tuned prompts in both zero-shot and few-shot scenarios. In Contextual Few-Shot prompting, LLaVA has the best accuracy at 83.0%, beating BLIP-2 (81.0%) and CLIP (79.0%). The lowest scores for Zero-Shot Descriptive prompting were CLIP (64.0%), BLIP2 (65.0%), and LLaVA (66.0%). These findings indicate that multimodal LLMs, when integrated with a robust preprocessing pipeline, may proficiently execute hate speech detection in multilingual, low-resource, and multimodal environments without the necessity of supervised training.

title: Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Transformer (Psttransformer) for Short-Term Solar Irradiance Forecasting
authors: Maryam Saeed, Jiaqiang Yang, Muhammad Farhan Khan, Bingqiang Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Accurate solar irradiance forecasting is critical for reliable renewable energy integration, yet traditional machine learning models struggle to capture temporal dynamics and purely data-driven deep learning approaches often lack physical consistency. This study proposes the Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Transformer (PST-Transformer), which incorporates solar geometry and cloud cover as physicsinformed features to guide model learning. Comparative evaluation against both machine learning and deep learning benchmarks demonstrates that physics-guided hybridization substantially improves forecasting accuracy: CNN-LSTM achieved a 52.6% reduction in MAE, while Transformer-LSTM gained an additional 7.3% improvement when physics-based inputs were included. The proposed PST-Transformer achieved state-of-the-art performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MAE} = 0.0216,\ \text{RMSE} = 0.04199, \mathrm{R}^{2} = 0.9571$</tex>). These findings highlight that combining physical constraints with deep learning not only enhances robustness under variable weather conditions but also provides a practical pathway for stable large-scale solar integration.

title: AI-Driven Helmet Detection in Construction Zones: A YOLOv8X Approach for Sustainable Safety Monitoring
authors: M. Chithik Raja, Mohamed Musallam Al-Mahr, Wasim Haidar, Suresh Palarimath
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The enforcement of personal protective equipment (PPE) compliance, particularly helmet usage, is a critical determinant of occupational safety in high-risk environments such as construction sites. This paper presents a robust, deep learning-driven framework for real-time helmet detection leveraging the YOLOv8X architecture—an advanced variant of the You Only Look Once (YOLO) family. The proposed system autonomously identifies workers and classifies their helmet-wearing status by detecting "head" and "helmet" instances using high-resolution industrial imagery. A curated dataset of 5,000 annotated images, enriched with diverse construction, manufacturing, and mining scenarios, was employed for training and evaluation. The YOLOv8X model achieves a mean Average Precision of 93.3%, with helmet AP of 93.2% and head AP of 93.55%, outperforming YOLOv3-v6, SSD, and Faster R-CNN. With an inference speed of 37.2 FPS and training efficiency of 2.045 hours, the model demonstrates a superior accuracy-latency trade-off suitable for edge deployment. A SHAP-based interpretability analysis confirms the model’s reliance on semantically meaningful features. Furthermore, we propose a privacy-preserving inference pipeline with on-device processing and facial anonymization. This work contributes a scalable, real-time safety monitoring solution aligned with smart city and Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) offering a transformative approach to proactive risk mitigation in industrial environments.

title: Intelligent Federated Learning Framework for IIoT with Soft Actor Critic Based Client Optimization
authors: L. Natrayan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems generate heterogeneous and sensitive data that require secure and adaptive learning frameworks. Federated Learning (FL) provides decentralized training while preserving data privacy, and the Federated Moving Average (FedMA) approach improves model generalization on non-IID data. However, FedMA lacks adaptiveness, which is critical for dynamic IIoT environments. This paper proposes a novel framework that integrates Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning with FedMA to enable adaptive client selection and efficient gradient aggregation. The actor–critic architecture optimizes client participation policies based on rewards, improving fairness and convergence. Experiments conducted on diverse Kaggle datasets—including vibration signals, production quality, and logistics tracking—demonstrated 94.74% accuracy, a Jain’s fairness index of 0.91, and significant gains in client selection efficiency compared to baseline FedMA. The results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed SAC–FedMA framework in achieving adaptive, fair, and secure learning for IIoT applications.

title: Context Aware LLM Optimization Using Domain-Specific Fine-Tuning
authors: S Geetha, Divij P, Pranav K M, Abhishek Paul P
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This paper presents an efficient attitude to fine-tune any Large Language Model (LLM) using Quantized Low Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) technique. QLoRA provides a coherent approach that not only shrinks computational resources usage but also maintains complete or full 16 – bit fine-tuning task performance. QLoRA incorporates several advancements to minimize memory usage while maintaining performance: (a) 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4), an optimized data type designed for normally distributed weights, (b) Double Quantization, which lowers memory consumption by further compressing quantization constants, and (c) Paged Optimizers, which help control memory spikes efficiently. Various LLMs have been fine-tuned using this approach. The results show that QLoRA fine-tuning on these models using clean datasets yields better results than the base models.

title: Machine Learning-Assisted Parkinson’s Disease Detection Through Voice Feature Analysis
authors: Dhanyasri Akella, Padmalaya Nayak, Pagidimarri Jasmitha, Archana Mullapudi, Raghavaram Namburu, Veena Trivedi
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a long-standing brain disorder that profoundly impacts how people move and speak. Preliminary diagnosis is essential to help doctors treat the disease better and improve patients’ quality of life. Among the earliest indicators of PD is voice impairment, positioning voice analysis as a non-invasive, promising diagnostic approach. This study investigates the early detection of PD through Machine Learning techniques applied to voice data from the Multi-Dimensional Voice Program (MDVP) dataset. The dataset comprises a range of vocal parameters—such as shimmer, jitter, and harmonic-to-noise ratios—that capture subtle variations in vocal quality associated with PD. Many AI models, like Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), have demonstrated high accuracy in symptom classification and predicting disease progression. In this work, we have employed various machine learning classifiers, namely SVM Classifier, Random Forest (RF), k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), and XGB, to distinguish between healthy individuals and people affected by PD. Moreover, we have employed feature selection techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and SelectKBest, to enhance model performance and identify the most relevant features. Our experimental results show that RF with the SelectKBest feature selection method outperforms other ML models, achieving an accuracy of 94.87%.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Forecasting Models for Groundwater Parameters with a Hybrid Approach
authors: Vaishnav Bhor, Aakanksha Bhusewar, Anushka Kale, Om Bhavsar, Dishali Bhoir, Jyoti Kanjalakar, Pramod Kanjalakar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Groundwater level forecasting is critical for sustainable water resource management, especially in regions dependent on seasonal rainfall and environmental fluctuations. This project presents a hybrid forecasting system combining Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models, to predict groundwater levels accurately for Aroma Solutions. The SARIMA model captures trend and seasonal patterns in water level time series, while the RNN corrects residual non-linear components. The hybrid approach significantly reduces prediction errors, achieving a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.04, outperforming individual models. A Streamlit-based user interface facilitates real-time, user-driven forecasting and scenario analysis, enhanced with explainability features for stakeholder trust. This work demonstrates the potential of hybrid statistical and deep learning models for environmental time series forecasting and practical water resource management applications.

title: A Novel Method for Discriminating Ultrasonic Signal Paths to Enhance Partial Discharge Localization in Transformers_supp1-3675575.docx
authors: rui hu
publish: N/A
abstract: When using ultrasonic detection to locate partial discharge points, some sensors receive ultrasonic signals that are affected by the internal structure of the transformer, undergoing multiple refractions, reflections, diffractions, and velocity changes during propagation. Under these conditions, the time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA) positioning equations cannot accurately describe the relationship between time delays and the location of the discharge source, leading to positioning results that deviate from the actual discharge point. To address this issue, this paper proposes a method for analyzing and identifying the propagation paths of ultrasonic signals, excluding sensors influenced by the internal structure of the transformer to improve positioning accuracy. First, the impact of the transformer's internal structure on ultrasonic signal waveforms is analyzed, and the characteristic differences between direct and non-direct waves are clarified. Then, four time-domain and frequency-domain feature parameters are extracted to describe the waveform differences between the two, and a support vector machine (SVM) is used for classification. Finally, multiple ultrasonic sensors are deployed on a 110 kV transformer experimental platform for signal acquisition and PD localization. Experimental results show that the SVM classifier achieves recognition rates of 88.3% and 84.6% for direct and non-direct waves, respectively. Using direct waves for localization reduces the positioning error and its standard deviation by 68.57 cm and 29.37 cm, respectively.

title: Comparison Studies of Protective (EG) Conductor Sizing for Low Voltage Systems
authors: Haijun Liu, Anthony Farias
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Low voltage system protective (equipment grounding) conductor sizing can be based on thermal capability calculations. The thermal capability method is based on the Joule's integral curve, where I<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>t remains constant. Some international and national standards also provide a table to allow users simply to select the Protective Conductor (PE) size based on the phase (line) conductor size. IEC 60364-5-52, BS 7671, NF C15-100 and other standards all provide the same table. This paper provides comparison studies for protective conductor sizes based on standard table and thermal calculation. The studies consider different scenarios such as phase conductor sized based on short circuit conditions only, phase conductor sized based on loading conditions while meeting short circuit conditions. Short circuit current variations and fault clearing time variations are analyzed. Protective conductor and phase conductor of different material types, and different protective devices are also studied. From the studies, a conclusion can be made that in some cases the table method may provide protective conductor sizes that do not meet the short-circuit thermal capability requirements.

title: Consumer-Electronics-Oriented Safe Interaction-Aware Motion Planning for Ramp Driving with Perception Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Zhen Tian, Zhihao Lin, Chunhong Yuan, Sunil Prajapat, Jing Yang, Por Lip Yee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Autonomous ramp driving is challenging due to complex interactions between autonomous vehicles (AVs) and human-driven vehicles (HDVs) with diverse driving styles, as well as strict geometric constraints imposed by ramp terminations. For consumer electronic vehicles (CEVs), these challenges are further amplified by limited computational resources and real-time decision requirements. This paper proposes a consum-erelectronics-oriented cooperative motion planning framework for autonomous ramp driving that integrates driving style awareness, perception uncertainty quantification, and interaction-aware decision-making. Heterogeneous HDV behaviors are modeled using data-driven driving style classification, while prediction uncertainty is captured via error ellipses. A game-theoretic formulation is employed to select lane-changing time points, which are then translated into executable trajectories through lightweight planning and control modules. Simulation results based on the NGSIM dataset and diverse ramp traffic scenarios demonstrate collision-free operation with improved merge efficiency and velocity smoothness under varying traffic densities. Furthermore, a dedicated consumer electronics platform evaluation shows that the proposed method achieves sub-millisecond runtime, minimal memory usage, and low energy consumption, outperforming several recent 2025 state-of-the-art planners, including mean-field-game-based MPC, Monte Carlo decision processes, and risk-field-based game-theoretic approaches. These results indicate that the proposed framework is well suited for safe and efficient ramp driving on consumer-grade autonomous vehicle platforms.

title: Large AI Model-Enabled Secure Communications in Low-Altitude Wireless Networks: Concepts, Perspectives and Case Study
authors: Chuang Zhang, Geng Sun, Yijing Lin, Weijie Yuan, Sinem Coleri, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs) have the potential to revolutionize communications by supporting a range of applications, including urban parcel delivery, aerial inspections and air taxis. However, compared with traditional wireless networks, LAWNs face unique security challenges due to low-altitude operations, frequent mobility and reliance on unlicensed spectrum, making it more vulnerable to some malicious attacks. In this article, we investigate some large artificial intelligence model (LAM)-enabled solutions for secure communications in LAWNs. Specifically, we first explore the amplified security risks and important limitations of traditional AI methods in LAWNs. Then, we introduce the basic concepts of LAMs and delve into the role of LAMs in addressing these challenges. To demonstrate the practical benefits of LAMs for secure communications in LAWNs, we propose a novel LAM-based optimization framework. This framework uses chain-of-thought-enabled large language models (LLMs) to enhance state features derived from handcrafted representations and design intrinsic rewards based on these enhanced features. This approach improves reinforcement learning performance for secure communication tasks. Through a typical case study, simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. Finally, we outline future directions for integrating LAMs into secure LAWN applications.

title: Learning Woody Clearing with Loss Alignment for Zero-Shot Regrowth and Woody Segmentation
authors: Kal Backman, Jared Wood, Adam Roff
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Detecting woody clearing is vital for managing biodiversity. Deep learning models can detect change in woody vegetation from bitemporal remote sensing imagery, however generated products may not meet end-user specifications due to unaligned loss definitions. Further limitations of deep learning models are the reliance on large datasets which can be difficult to attain for spatially rare and ambiguous events such as regrowth detection. In this work we train a model to detect woody change using bitemporal Sentinel-2 imagery consisting of 7 years’ worth of annual imagery across the state of New South Wales, Australia. To align the objective of the model with end-user metrics, we introduce the loss scaling coefficient α which transforms the objective to optimize for specific Fβ scores. Introducing α was found to increase precision by 1.85× or recall by 1.12×. We propose input imagery augmentation and generation techniques that allow the woody change detection model to zero-shot transfer to regrowth and woody segmentation tasks. For woody segmentation, image generation techniques using activation maximization with low α values for stability and image generation techniques derived from handcrafted features utilizing a mosaic of clearing patches and artificial trees for contextual grounding were found to outperform prior woody segmentation works of the study area, reducing the overall error by up to 18.2%. For zero-shot woody regrowth, creating pseudo-post and prior images resulted in the model achieving an F1 score of 0.845, creating a foundation for future regrowth detection work.
