title: GOT-JEPA: Generic Object Tracking with Model Adaptation and Occlusion Handling using Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture_supp1-3675005.mp4
authors: Yen-Yu Lin
publish: N/A
abstract: The human visual system tracks objects by integrating current observations with previously observed information, adapting to target and scene changes, and reasoning about occlusion at fine granularity. In contrast, recent generic object trackers are often optimized for training targets, which limits robustness and generalization in unseen scenarios, and their occlusion reasoning remains coarse, lacking detailed modeling of occlusion patterns. To address these limitations in generalization and occlusion perception, we propose GOT-JEPA, a model-predictive pretraining framework that extends JEPA from predicting image features to predicting tracking models. Given identical historical information, a teacher predictor generates pseudo-tracking models from a clean current frame, and a student predictor learns to predict the same pseudo-tracking models from a corrupted version of the current frame. This design provides stable pseudo supervision and explicitly trains the predictor to produce reliable tracking models under occlusions, distractors, and other adverse observations, improving generalization to dynamic environments. Building on GOT-JEPA, we further propose OccuSolver to enhance occlusion perception for object tracking. OccuSolver adapts a point-centric point tracker for object-aware visibility estimation and detailed occlusion-pattern capture. Conditioned on object priors iteratively generated by the tracker, OccuSolver incrementally refines visibility states, strengthens occlusion handling, and produces higher-quality reference labels that progressively improve subsequent model predictions. Extensive evaluations on seven benchmarks show that our method effectively enhances tracker generalization and robustness. The code will be available at https://github.com/chenshihfang/GOT.

title: Smile on the Face, Sadness in the Eyes: Bridging the Emotion Gap with a Multimodal Dataset of Eye and Facial Behaviors_supp1-3673532.pdf
authors: Yuanyuan Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Emotion Recognition (ER) is the process of analyzing and identifying human emotions from sensing data. Currently, the field heavily relies on facial expression recognition (FER) because visual channel conveys rich emotional cues. However, facial expressions are often used as social tools rather than manifestations of genuine inner emotions. To understand and bridge this gap between FER and ER, we introduce eye behaviors as an important emotional cue and construct an Eye-behavior-aided Multimodal Emotion Recognition (EMER) dataset. To collect data with genuine emotions, spontaneous emotion induction paradigm is exploited with stimulus material, during which non-invasive eye behavior data, like eye movement sequences and eye fixation maps, is captured together with facial expression videos. To better illustrate the gap between ER and FER, multi-view emotion labels for mutimodal ER and FER are separately annotated. Furthermore, based on the new dataset, we design a simple yet effective Eye-behavior-aided MER Transformer (EMERT) that enhances ER by bridging the emotion gap. EMERT leverages modality-adversarial feature decoupling and a multitask Transformer to model eye behaviors as a strong complement to facial expressions. In the experiment, we introduce seven multimodal benchmark protocols for a variety of comprehensive evaluations of the EMER dataset. The results show that the EMERT outperforms other state-of-the-art multimodal methods by a great margin, revealing the importance of modeling eye behaviors for robust ER. To sum up, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the importance of eye behaviors in ER, advancing the study on addressing the gap between FER and ER for more robust ER performance.

title: Computational Studies on Dynamic Multi-objective Optimization with Feature Representation Transfer
authors: Gan Ruan, Hao Tong, Jialin Liu, Xin Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Transfer learning-based dynamic multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (Tr-DMOEAs) represent one of the earliest feature-representation-transfer optimization approaches in dynamic multi-objective optimization. Our previous investigations into Tr-DMOEAs revealed transfer effectiveness on problems with drastic shifts in Pareto-optimal sets (PSs), and demonstrated the superiority of a linear kernel over the original Gaussian kernel. This paper conducts comprehensive comparative studies on knowledge transfer in Tr-DMOEA variants with a linear kernel. We firstly investigate how transfer works in Tr- DMOEA with a linear kernel. We then propose six new Tr- DMOEA variants with linear feature representations to study the impact of different components of knowledge transfer in Tr- DMOEA. Among them, two variants have different approaches to learning linear feature representations, and four variants use different source knowledge selection methods. We show that all these six variants have little impact on knowledge transfer in Tr-DMOEA (i.e., little impact on the dynamic optimization). Considering the computational cost of feature representation learning and its little impact on knowledge transfer in Tr- DMOEA, we then propose three other new Tr-DMOEA variants without learning feature representations to further investigate their impact on knowledge transfer. The three variants have different methods of selecting source knowledge, which are experimentally shown to have few impact on knowledge transfer in Tr-DMOEA either. Finally, we demonstrate the superiority of Tr-DMOEA variants without feature representation over those with it regarding solution quality and transfer efficiency. Our studies show that feature representation is not essential in transfer learning based dynamic optimization.

title: Traceable calibration of energy meters with digital IEC 61850-9-2 inputs
authors: Ernest Houtzager, Pieter Van Der Kamp, Gert Rietveld
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: A test setup is developed for the traceable calibration of energy meters with digital IEC 61850-9-2 sampled value inputs. The development of the setup is driven by the increased use of digital instrumentation in electrical distribution substations. During calibration, the meter under test is subjected to a stream of synthetically generated voltage and current samples simulating a polyphase grid with different grid conditions. Reading the energy indicated by the meter before and after the test gives the accumulated energy, and an indication of the meter error is achieved by comparing this value with a reference value based on the applied voltage and current samples. Although the inner workings of the meter are unknown, several tests have been designed to determine errors that could occur due to the digital nature of the meter. These include inadequate synchronization and the effect of harmonics. Small but non-zero errors in energy reading were found in one of the meters during tests that followed the spirit of the tests defined in the IEC 62053-22 standard on ‘Static meters of AC active energy’.

title: CSQNet: A Query-Driven Channel-Spatial Attention Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Xiaoyang Yuan, Yiding Wang, Chunling Yang, Yan Zhang, Mingwei Zhang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Infrared small target detection constitutes (IRSTD) a critical capability in remote surveillance and tracking systems, serving essential roles in applications such as early warning, reconnaissance, and target monitoring. However, existing IRSTD methods primarily rely on attribute-specific feature modulation (i.e., spatial and channel features modulate their own responses) or feature-driven attention in transformer-style designs, computed directly from input features, lacking explicit query-guided mechanisms to coordinate spatial and channel information, which limits robust small target representation. To this end, we propose CSQNet, a query-driven channel-spatial attention network that leverages learnable queries to explicitly guide feature selection and attention generation. At its core, the Residual Channel-Spatial Query (RCSQ) Attention mechanism constructs decoupled yet complementary pathways along channel and spatial dimensions, enabling cross-dimensional interaction for enhanced target discrimination. Based on RCSQ, a query-guided encoder-decoder framework is designed, in which the Progressive Query-Guided Encoder progressively refines feature representations, and the Residual Query-Aware Decoder aggregates multi-scale query-enhanced features for precise target reconstruction. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that CSQNet achieves superior segmentation accuracy and robustness in cluttered infrared scenes.

title: Investigation of SiO 2 :HfO 2 Composition Effects on ALD HfSiO x as Gate Dielectric on β -Ga 2 O 3 (001)
authors: Xin Zhai, Zhuoqun Wen, Pengcheng Lu, Kai Sun, Elaheh Ahmadi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: This study investigated the dielectric properties of HfSiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> thin films deposited on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>, with a focus on the effects of SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>:HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> composition ratio and post-deposition annealing (PDA) environment using deep ultraviolet (DUV)-assisted capacitance–voltage (C–V) and current–voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$I$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> measurements. Initial results showed no clear trend between composition and performance, attributed to HfSiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>/Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> interfacial layer formation. To minimize interfacial layer effects, a thicker initial SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> layer was introduced, enabling a clear correlation between composition ratio and dielectric behavior. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) further confirmed a well-defined interface between the dielectric and the Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> substrate. Oxygen PDA improved dielectric constant (~10.6), and significantly reduced interface trap density (~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 6.1\times 10^{12}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> eV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>) compared to air annealing (~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 11.9\times 10^{12}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> eV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>).

title: CT Saturation Compensation Using a Physics-Informed FCN-LSTM Network
authors: Haoheqi Shi, Zhongping Liu, Ruiyu Zhang, Jun Han, Yu Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The effectiveness of power system fault analysis, protection and fault location is reliant on the current measurements provided by current transformers (CTs). However, CT saturation during faults distorts the secondary current waveform. Existing reconstruction methods, including approximate model-based and signal-fitting techniques, often struggle with the nonlinearity of saturation and are constrained by dependencies on precise parameters or the identification of a relatively long unsaturated regions. While data-driven methods offer a powerful alternative, the lack of interpretability remains a concern. This paper proposes a physics-informed data-driven model to bridge this gap. A physics-informed fully convolutional network and long short-term memory (FCN-LSTM) model is established. The physical knowledge is integrated at multiple modules: a dual-channel input incorporating saturation status, an output target of predicting the compensation current, and a adaptively weighted loss function that prioritizes accuracy in saturated regions. During simulation, the proposed method achieves smaller mean absolute error (MAE), root mean square error (RMSE), standard deviation (Std) and maximum (Max) error compared to existing methods. The results verify that the proposed method is not only accurate but also more interpretable, offering a robust and effective solution for CT saturation compensation.

title: Multi-Stage Coordinated Voltage Control and Harmonic Suppression Reserve in Distribution Network
authors: Ben Liu, Bo Wang, Zhichao Jiang, Wei Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: To address the issues of voltage fluctuations and harmonic pollution in distribution networks caused by a high penetration of distributed energy resources and new loads, this paper proposes a multi-stage coordinated voltage control and harmonic suppression reserve method. In the day-ahead stage, on-load tap changer settings and feeder switch states are determined to achieve the optimal root-node voltage and network reconfiguration for the entire day. Then, during the day, the active power scheduling of battery energy storage systems and interruptible loads, along with the reactive power support of photovoltaic inverters, static var generators (SVGs) and capacitor banks, is coordinately optimized in each scheduling period. Meanwhile, the capability reserve of SVGs for harmonic suppression is integrated into the intraday model. The proposed multi-stage coordinated method is validated using a modified 33 bus system. The results show significant improvements for economic benefits and power quality.

title: Generator Stator Partial Discharge Pattern Recognition Based on MSRCR and Visual Transformer
authors: Peng Ling, Zhuohui Wu, Xiaosheng Peng, Qing Li, Hao Wu, Xiangdong Liu, Jun Jiang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Online partial discharge (PD) monitoring is widely regarded as an effective technical measure for ensuring the stable operation of large generator insulation systems. To further improve the accuracy of generator partial discharge pattern recognition, a recognition method integrating image enhancement with the Visual Transformer (ViT) is proposed. First, the Multi-Scale Retinex Enhancement Algorithm (MSRCR) is employed to enhance the image features of partial discharge spectra. This step creates enhanced partial discharge feature spectra based on Retinex theory, effectively improving the distinctiveness of spectral features. Subsequently, a Visual Transformer deep learning network is introduced to achieve efficient feature extraction and pattern classification from the enhanced feature spectra. Finally, systematic multi-parameter and multi-model performance evaluations are conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves an identification accuracy of 98.5%, which enables the precise recognition of generator partial discharge feature spectra.

title: Cylindrical PV for Urban Spaces: Field Measurements and Optimal Fixed Installation
authors: Kento Honma, Shinji Yokogawa, Shuzi Hayase, Fumiki Tamura, Masaki Nakamura
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Global efforts toward carbon neutrality highlight the need for advanced renewable energy solutions. Cylindrical solar cells (Cylindrical PV) offer a complementary technology for locations where conventional flat panels are difficult to install, featuring angle-independent light collection and improved heat dissipation. However, optimal installation configurations for maximizing energy yield and stability remain underexplored, with most studies relying on simulations rather than field measurements. This research identifies optimal installation conditions through field measurements and predictive modeling using actual cylindrical PV modules. Power, irradiance, and surface temperature data were collected at various orientations and tilt angles. A regression model was constructed to simulate annual power generation under clear-sky conditions. The model achieved high accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}=0.88$</tex>) in predicting power output. Results show that a south-facing module with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex> tilt maximizes annual output while maintaining stable performance across seasons. Based on the model and meteorological data, optimal installation guidelines were proposed for wall-mounted and rooftop applications. These findings provide foundational insights for cylindrical PV deployment in building-integrated photovoltaic systems, contributing to efficient solar energy utilization in urban environments.

title: Multi-Omics Fusion for Personalized Pancreatic Cancer Survival Prediction Using Hierarchical Deep Neural Networks and Transformer Models
authors: Kavitha A, Girisha J K, Archana V
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Pancreatic cancer belongs to the number of the most aggressive types of malignancies having poor prognosis, delayed diagnosis and limited treatment options. The biological heterogeneity and complexity of the disease cannot be understood using the standard methods of diagnosis and prediction. In solving these problems, this study proposes a multi-omics integrative methodology, which entails integration of genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, methylomics, and radiomics with the most recent deep learning frameworks, i.e., Hierarchical Deep Neural Networks (HDNNs), and transformer-based survival prediction model. The strategy begins with the purchase of the multi-omics data in databases such as TCGA and ICGC along with radiomic features on CT and MRI scans. Transformers, graph neural networks, autoencoders, and Swin Transformers are modality-specific encoders and used to obtain informative representations following selective preprocessing. The combining of the embeddings is enhanced by a cross-modality transformer, which is effective to capture the inter-omic relationships in an adaptive manner. The joint features are inputted into an HDNN to acquire local mutations to global tumor patterns. Longitudinal disease progression is also captured with a time-conscious transformer and the model is trained using multi-task learning and assessed using concordance index, Brier score, Kaplan-Meier analysis and ROC. It already has the tools of interpretability (SHAP, integrated gradients) and can be implemented as a HIPAA-compliant clinical platform.

title: Optimized Multi-Cancer Classification with Gene Expression data: Integrating GWO and Deep Learning
authors: M. Dhatri Reddy, M. Aravind Kumar, P. Sushanth, M. Ramachandro Majji, M. A. Jabbar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Early diagnosis of cancer is crucial for improving treatment outcomes, but multi-class cancer classification remains challenging due to high-dimensional gene expression data and class imbalance. This study focuses on four clinically significant cancers—Breast, Colon, Kidney, and Ovary. This paper proposes a hybrid framework integrating Random Oversampling (ROS) for class balancing, Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) for feature selection, and deep learning for classification. GWO was selected for its superior balance between exploration and exploitation compared to other metaheuristics, enabling effective feature subset reduction. Unlike prior works focusing on single cancers, the proposed model addresses multi-cancer classification with systematic benchmarking against PSO, GA, PCA, SVM, and Random Forest. The approach achieved 96.01% accuracy, a 5.2% improvement over baselines, with enhanced sensitivity and recall for minority cancer classes. This highlights the clinical relevance and generalizability of the proposed method, demonstrating the potential of optimization-driven deep learning for scalable multi-cancer diagnosis from high-dimensional gene expression data.

title: Energy Consumption Prediction in Wireless Sensor Networks Using Swarm Intelligence Algorithms
authors: Bonda Tanmayi, Divyasri C, Syama S, Kruthika U
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks(WSN) are becoming more crucial in contemporary applications and thus energy efficiency is crucial for extended system durability. This work focuses on improving the accuracy of energy consumption prediction in WSNs using nature-inspired optimization strategies. It explores the application of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Salp Swarm Optimization (SSO) and Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA) to fine-tune the weights of a linear regression model for enhanced forecasting performance. Through the reduction of prediction errors, such algorithms will have the capability of delivering a reliable solution for the estimation of energy consumption in sensor nodes. Comparative studies feature each approach’s convergence rate, predictability, and computational cost. Results confirm the ability of PSO to outperform conventional optimization significantly in the regression training, particularly in challenging and dynamic WSN scenarios. This work identifies bio-inspired algorithms as promising tools for developing intelligent and energy-efficient models for WSNs. This approach contributes to the advancement of more sustainable and optimized network infrastructures through the application of predictive analysis driven by nature-inspired optimization techniques.

title: Design and analysis of a Slotted Vivaldi Antenna for Microwave Imaging Applications
authors: Vijaya Geetha R, Harsha S, Naveen Kumar N, Shashank C
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: A microstrip line-feed ultra-wideband (UWB) slotted compact Vivaldi antenna is crafted based on a 50×50×1.5 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> FR4 substrate, enabling seamless integration with microwave imaging systems. Its compact form, efficiency, and adaptability make it versatile across various applications. Operating at 12GHz, it demonstrates superior proficiency in ultrawideband communication. Vivaldi Antennas are pivotal in microwave imaging, advancing fields like medical imaging, security screening, and satellite imaging. The design progresses through four stages: from a basic antenna to one incorporating resonant cavities, then integrating slots, and finally synthesizing advancements [8]. This study thoroughly analyzes slotted Vivaldi antennas, focusing on key parameters: Reflection Coefficient for impedance match, VSWR for power mismatch, Gain for signal strength, Directivity for signal concentration, and Radiation Efficiency for power utilization.

title: Enhanced Credit Card Fraud Detection Using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks with Real-Time Alert Systems
authors: Mala K, Sahana K L, Harshitha C A, Monika K S, Niveditha K, R E Nithyashree
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The proliferation of digital financial transactions has intensified the need for robust credit card fraud detection mechanisms. This study proposes a novel approach utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) integrated with real-time alert systems to enhance fraud detection capabilities. The methodology addresses class imbalance through Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and incorporates dropout regularization to mitigate overfitting. The system automatically generates alerts upon detecting suspicious transactions, enabling immediate cardholder verification. Experimental validation on a dataset containing 284,807 transactions demonstrated training accuracy exceeding 90%, though validation performance exhibited fluctuations indicating sensitivity to data characteristics. While achieving perfect recall (100%), the model exhibited challenges in precision (50%), highlighting the need for architectural refinements before practical deployment.

title: Active Inference for Sustainable Computing Continuum Systems
authors: Juan Luis Herrera, Boris Sedlak, Schahram Dustdar
publish: IEEE Internet Computing
abstract: The computing continuum (CC) is expected to improve the quality of service of distributed applications. However, adding more devices to current cloud datacenters to implement the CC can increase the energy-related CO2 emissions of the paradigm. Sustainability requires intelligently managing applications through the CC, considering both the energy consumption and carbon intensity of energy sources. Moreover, the CC provider should perform this management, given their control of devices and networks. This work provides an overview on achieving sustainability in the CC and the associated challenges, proposing an architecture that leverages active inference to learn, manage, and reconfigure the system to achieve a low carbon footprint while meeting the service level objectives of applications.

title: Integrated Active Filtering Function MMC Harmonic Suppression Control and Power Range Analysis
authors: Shaopeng Zhang, Wang Xiang, Junjie Lin, Yu Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of power electronic devices in power systems, high-voltage grids will face operational risks of exceeding the limits for 5th and 7th background harmonics. By leveraging the topological isomorphism between flexible HVDC converters and multi-level active power filters (ML-APF), the control strategy of traditional converter stations can be modified. This approach not only maintains the basic functions of AC/DC power conversion and transmission but also enables active suppression of 5th and 7th grid harmonics. To address this challenge, this paper first extends the classical Decoupled Double Synchronous Reference Frame (DDSRF) to the Decoupled Multiple Synchronous Reference Frame (DMSRF). Compared to harmonic detection based on only Multiple Synchronous Reference Frames (MSRF), this approach achieves superior performance. Then the paper further modifies the control strategy of Modular Multilevel Converters (MMC) by incorporating 5th/7th harmonic modulation ratios to actively suppress harmonic currents. Additionally, this paper provides a conservative estimation of the MMC power operating range in harmonic-polluted systems, offering guidance for the capacity design in practical engineering applications. Finally, simulation results demonstrate that the proposed harmonic detection strategy balances steady-state accuracy and dynamic performance, while effectively suppressing 5th and 7th harmonic currents.

title: Electricity-Carbon Coupling Based Substation Planning Method Considering Multi-Uncertainty
authors: Feng Zhao, Tiancheng Shi, Zhuxing Shao
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: To address the uncertainties introduced by high penetration of distributed renewable energy such as wind and photovoltaic (PV) generation in distribution network planning, this paper proposes a configuration method for substation siting and sizing that considers multiple uncertainties for distributed generation and electricity-carbon coupling. Firstly, wind and solar output probability models are constructed, and large-scale scenario reduction is performed to obtain typical output scenarios. Secondly, carbon factors are considered in the optimization of substation planning, achieving balance between substation investment economic environmental performance. Then, a global optimization algorithm is designed to determine the optimal substation location and capacity configuration. Case analysis on the IEEE 40-node system verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating that the electricity-carbon coupling model can provide a more comprehensive planning basis for substations in distribution networks in the future smart grid.

title: A Novel Power Grid Resilience Enhancement Method Integrating the Pangu-Weather Model and Active Defence Strategies
authors: Jianjun Deng, Long Wang, Yu Li, Huaqiang Chen, Wenhu Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Frequent occurrences of extreme natural disasters such as typhoons pose severe challenges to the secure and stable operation of power systems. This paper proposes a method that integrates the Pangu-Weather model with active defense strategies to enhance the resilience of power grids against disasters. By performing hourly predictions of 10 m wind fields with the Pangu-Weather model and locating typhoon centers through minimum wind speeds, high-accuracy typhoon path forecasting is achieved. Simulation studies on an improved IEEE-33 bus system are conducted by incorporating active load shedding and postdisaster restoration strategies. Results show that the prediction error of typhoon paths is within 3%, providing effective scenario inputs for active load shedding during typhoon events and significantly improving the overall resilience of power systems.

title: One-Step Image-Function Generation via Consistency Training
authors: Ke Liu, Feng Liu, Jingjun Gu, Shuyi Zhang, Zhihua Wang, Jiajun Bu, Bo Han, Haishuai Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Diffusion models achieve remarkable image generation quality but suffer from slow inference due to hundreds of denoising steps. Consistency models alleviate this and enable one-step sampling by directly mapping noise to images with a U-Net generator, but they remain unstable and resource-hungry under small-batch training and can only generate images at fixed resolutions. These limitations motivate us to explore more efficient and flexible architectures. To this end, we propose a novel framework for efficient one-step flexible-resolution image generation. First, we represent images as continuous image functions based on implicit neural representations (INRs), which decouple image resolution from the total amount of the generated parameters and can be differentiably rendered at arbitrary resolutions. Then, we design a Transformer-based generator that directly outputs such continuous functions, providing an end-to-end architecture tailored for consistency training and stabilizing training under limited resources. To further improve convergence, we introduce an image reconstruction warm-up task that strengthens the function prediction module. Extensive experiments on one-step image generation demonstrate that our method significantly improves the stability and performance of consistency models under low-resource settings, while also enabling efficient any-resolution sampling.

title: 3D printed lamellar Bragg gratings on optical fiber end-facets for remote environmental sensing
authors: Ayan Mondal, Priya Dominic, Halima El Aadad, Hicham El Hamzaoui, Marc Douay, Yves Quiquempois
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The fabrication of optical components on the top of optical fiber facets using two-photon 3D printing has opened a new path for the development of next generation optical fiber sensors. However, printing complex structures with high resolution at optical fiber tips has still remained a challenge because of the lower axial resolution owing to the ellipsoidal voxel dimensions. To overcome this issue, we have 3D printed lamellar Bragg gratings (LBGs) on optical fiber facets in the XY plane along with a 45° angled prism on top of the fiber core, allowing to couple light into the gratings. The prism and the design of the gratings were optimized with finite-difference-time-domain (FDTD) simulations to get high reflectance in the operating spectral range corresponding to 6th order of reflection. This compact extrinsic LBGs were 3D printed on single mode fibers (SMF-LBGs) for pressure, humidity and temperature sensing applications. Pressure sensing measurements were performed with these SMF-LBGs. They show a pressure sensitivity of 278 pm/bar in the range 1-50 bars. The humidity sensing has been performed in a relative humidity range of 10-90 RH% and a sensitivity of 65.4 pm/RH% was obtained. The temperature response shows a linear spectral shift with a sensitivity of 27.1 pm/°C in the 20-60°C range.

title: Diktopos: A two-stage framework for joint container-based microservice placement and distributed volume allocation on cloud-edge networks
authors: Mohammed Dhiya Eddine Gouaouri, Sihem Ouahouah, Miloud Bagaa, Messaoud Ahmed Ouameur, Massicotte Daniel, Adlen Ksentini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cloud Computing
abstract: The Cloud-Edge collaborative computing enables the deployment of latency-sensitive and data-intensive applications closer to end users. However, it introduces significant challenges for microservice placement, due to resource heterogeneity, limited edge capacity, and the need to satisfy storage requirements using aggregated resources across multiple nodes. To address these issues, we propose Diktopos, a topology-aware, two-stage scheduling framework that jointly optimizes microservice placement and distributed storage volume allocation in cloud-edge networks. The joint optimization problem is decomposed into two subproblems: (i) microservice placement and (ii) distributed volume allocation, with the objective of minimizing computation, communication, energy, and storage costs. At its core, Diktopos employs a low-complexity, rank-based heuristic that ensures scalable and accurate placement across heterogeneous edge nodes. Simulation results show that our method achieves near-optimal placement decisions (within 1.67% of the optimal solution), and converges up to 5× faster than state-of-the-art approaches in large-scale deployments. Real-world experiments in Kubernetes environments demonstrate up to 53% latency reduction compared to the default scheduler, and up to 23% improvement over other baselines, confirming Diktopos' effectiveness in dynamic, resource-constrained edge scenarios.

title: Polarization-Diversity Single-Layer Silicon Optical Switch with Series Phase Shifters
authors: Ningyu Zhang, Tianqi Song, Jing Wang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: This study demonstrates a novel polarization-insensitive optical switch (PI-OS) based on 220 nm single-layer silicon-on-insulator (SOI) waveguides. By using a polarization-diversity scheme and a specially designed phase shifter (PS), the fundamental electric (TE0) and fundamental magnetic (TM0) modes acquire the same phase shift when a certain power is applied to the PS, thereby achieving PI operation. Compared with other 220 nm single-layer approaches by using slot waveguides or square waveguides, this method offers better performances on insertion loss (IL), extinction ratio (ER) and polarization-dependent loss (PDL) with simple fabrication process. At 1550 nm wavelength, this PI-OS shows ER greater than 23.8 dB and IL below 2.3 dB for the TE0 mode. In addition, ER is higher than 26.7 dB and IL is below 1.1 dB for the TM0 mode. The PDL is less than 1.3 dB.

title: Research on Micro-Contact System Visualization and Non-Destructive Contact Area Extraction Based on Tomography
authors: Wenhua Li, Tianyu Du, Yi Su, Zheng Han, Deyuan Lin, Zhengyuan Zhao, Yun Wang, Zhuoqian Zhang, Bowen Chi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: Electrical contact is a critical phenomenon, and the contact state of the contacts directly determines the stability of system operation. Traditional methods of analyzing contact performance by disassembling components suffer from structural damage and limitations in accuracy. This paper proposes a non-destructive analysis method for contact regions based on computed tomography (CT) technology. Taking relay contacts as the subject of study, the method reconstructs three-dimensional digital models through CT scanning and establishes a voxel-based visualization of the microscopic contact system based on differences in material electron density. Furthermore, an adaptive surface-voxel dual-domain mapping coupling method is proposed to obtain a set of candidate points in the proximity region, and a Gaussian mixture model is utilized to determine segmentation thresholds. Finally, by combining a statistically driven mechanism for region selection and fusion precise extraction and quantitative characterization of the contact region are achieved. The results indicate that the obtained contact projection area shows a significant correlation with contact resistance, providing non-destructive and efficient technical support for the analysis of contact state evolution and full-lifecycle health management.

title: Effects of Magnetocardiography Array Errors on Cardiac Source Imaging
authors: Min Zhang, Yanmei Wang, Peilun Li, Yadan Zhang, Yangyang Cui, Min Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Magnetocardiography (MCG) source imaging allows high-precision three-dimensional localisation of regions with abnormal cardiac activity, playing a crucial role in disease diagnosis. In practical measurements, however, four key types of error in sensor arrays, i.e., crosstalk, gain error, angular error, and position error, can distort magnetic signals or the lead field matrix, thereby compromising imaging performance. To date, their impact on MCG source imaging has not been systematically assessed. In this study, we establish an evaluation framework and systematically investigate the effects of these errors for the first time. A comprehensive evaluation is conducted across three key aspects: reconstruction accuracy, spatial resolution, and the localisation accuracy of correlated sources. Our results indicate that all four error types degrade imaging performance to varying degrees. The detrimental influence of errors on reconstruction accuracy and spatial resolution becomes more severe as the signal-to-noise ratio increases. For the localisation of correlated sources, increases in the number of sources and the inter-source coupling strength further exacerbate the interference caused by array errors. Based on the configuration in this work, we derive conservative safe operating limits: crosstalk <1%, gain error <3%, angular error <1°, and position error <2 mm. Therefore, precise calibration of sensor parameters before MCG measurements is essential to ensure imaging performance. This study highlights that systematically evaluating array errors is critical for designing calibration strategies and supporting the clinical application of MCG systems.

title: CNNGP: Multi-tree Genetic Programming Integrating Pre-trained CNN Features for Fine-Grained Image Classification_supp2-3675672.pdf
authors: Ying Bi
publish: N/A
abstract: Fine-grained image classification (FGIC) remains a challenging task due to subtle inter-class differences and significant intra-class variations, particularly under limited training data. While genetic programming (GP) offers promising adaptability, the performance of existing GP-based methods is limited due to the reliance on traditional image descriptors for feature extraction/learning. To address this limitation, this paper proposes CNNGP, i.e., a novel multi-tree GP approach that utilizes the rich representations of pre-trained convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for FGIC. In CNNGP’s multi-tree representation, two trees are used to explore multi-level feature maps from pre-trained CNNs to generate saliency masks that highlight informative regions, and one tree is used to integrate them to construct higher-level and more powerful features for classification. Experiments on four commonly used FGIC datasets demonstrate that CNNGP can achieve superior (or at least competitive) performance to popular deep neural network methods and the recent GP-based method. Further analysis verifies the benefit of using dual saliency trees and visualizes how CNNGP captures complementary visual patterns that enhance feature learning. It also examines convolutional layer selection to reveal dataset-specific feature usage, demonstrating CNNGP’s adaptive feature learning capability.

title: A Review of Probabilistic Power Flow Calculation Methods
authors: Jifu Song, Zhengshuo Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Large-scale renewable energy integration amplifies the stochasticity in power system operations. Methods for probabilistic power flow (PPF) calculation are capable of revealing the impact of uncertainties on system behavior more comprehensively than deterministic approaches and have thus attracted extensive research attention. This paper provides a review of research on PPF calculation methods, summarizing advances from three aspects: stochastic variable modeling, solution methodologies, and application scenarios. Furthermore, it outlines promising directions for the further development of PPF methodologies.

title: Assessing Processor Allocation Strategies for Online Scheduling of Moldable Task Graphs
authors: Mary Jeevana Pudota, Krishna Chaitanya Reddy Chitta, Sai Rithvik Gundla, Hongyang Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Scheduling a graph of moldable tasks, where each task can be executed by a varying number of processors with execution time depending on the processor allocation, represents a fundamental problem in high-performance computing (HPC). The online version of the scheduling problem introduces an additional constraint: each task is only discovered when all its predecessors have been completed. A key challenge for this online problem lies in making processor allocation decisions without complete knowledge of the future tasks or dependencies. This uncertainty can lead to inefficient resource utilization and increased overall completion time, or makespan. Recent studies have provided theoretical analysis (i.e., derived competitive ratios) for certain processor allocation algorithms. However, the algorithms' practical performance remains under-explored, and their reliance on fixed parameter settings may not consistently yield optimal performance across varying workloads. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of three processor allocation strategies by empirically assessing their performance under widely used speedup models and diverse graph structures. These algorithms are integrated into a List scheduling framework that greedily schedules ready tasks based on the current processor availability. We perform systematic tuning of the algorithms' parameters and report the best observed makespan together with the corresponding parameter settings. Our findings highlight the critical role of parameter tuning in obtaining optimal makespan performance, regardless of the differences in allocation strategies. The insights gained in this study can guide the deployment of these algorithms in practical runtime systems.

title: Enhancement of security and Network Lifetime using Enhanced Intrusion-Resistant Algorithm in Wireless Sensor Networks for IoT Applications
authors: Kodumuri Srujana, D. Rajani, Venkata Syamala Raju Talari, Arun M, N.V. Phani Sai Kumar, V. Gokula Krishnan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The fast-growing adoption of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) in such critical applications of the Internet of Things (IoT) not only requires reliable data, but more importantly, robust networks against malicious attacks. Nonetheless, nodes can be attacked frequently, fail often and deplete their energy in a relatively short time resulting in performance deterioration and compromised security. To overcome these deficiencies, the proposed paper introduces the framework of Enhanced Intrusion-Resistant Algorithm (EIRA) which can enhance network lifetime, error rate of intrusion detection and energy efficiency simultaneously. EIRA combines dynamic clustering, node selection based on trust and lightweight anomaly detection to balance secure transmission of data. The algorithm is able to dynamically detect attacker patterns in the network and intercept suspicious nodes while incurring the least overhead possible. Moreover, a fine-grained energy-efficient routing is applied to limit retransmitted packets and to distribute energy consumed sensor nodes uniformly. Simulation results indicate that EIRA is more efficient in terms of transferred packets and throughput, energy consumption, and detection accuracy than the other existing methods. The proposed framework results in a 25 per cent increase in the network lifetime and a substantial decrease in the percentage of false positive, as compared to conventional intrusion detection approaches.

title: Transient Stability Analysis of Power Systems Integrated with Grid-Forming Converters Based on Current-Limiting Switching Surfaces
authors: Zhenyu Du, Sichao Wang, Liwen Zheng, Yuexi Yang, Shan Liu, Huarui Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Current limiting strategies are widely adopted in power electronic controls to prevent overcurrent damage to devices, yet they significantly affect the transient stability of grid-forming converters (GFMCs). This paper investigates the domain of attraction (DA) distribution of GFMCs under current saturation conditions. First, we establish that GFMCs typically operate in voltage-control mode (VCM) but switch to currentcontrol mode (CCM) upon triggering current limiting, with the switching surface analytically derived. Second, post-fault trajectory analysis identifies four distinct dynamic behaviors, including undesirable controller lockup in current mode if faults persist. Finally, using the Runge-Kutta method, we analyze the DA distribution in a single-GFMC infinite-bus power system under current saturation constraints. The results offer critical insights into transient stability during current-limiting operation.

title: Current Sharing Control Strategy for LLCC Converter Based on Variable Virtual Impedance
authors: Zhangquanyi Guo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: To prevent control gain failure caused by the competition between the current-sharing loop and voltage loop under load transients, which leads to loss of current-sharing effectiveness, we propose an improved average current-sharing control strategy by introducing variable virtual impedance into the current-sharing loop. This approach enables high-efficiency balanced current output through real-time adjustment of virtual impedance values. Simulation experiments were conducted on a 2000W input-parallel output-parallel (IPOP) LLCC resonant circuit, with comparisons made against traditional control strategies. The results demonstrate that the proposed control strategy can achieve effective system current sharing during both startup and load transient conditions.

title: Unscented Kalman Filter with Generalized Correntropy-Cauchy Fusion Loss for Robust Power System Forecasting-Aided State Estimation
authors: Dongjian Huang, Shiwei Xu, Dongcheng Zhou, Xiang Shen, Wenyuan Wang, Wenxu Yan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: Forecasting-aided state estimation (FASE) plays a vital role in ensuring the stability of power systems, particularly under conditions involving various anomalies such as non-Gaussian measurement noise. This work introduces an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) enhanced with a Generalized Correntropy-Cauchy Fusion Loss (GCCFL) for power system FASE. The key feature of the GCCFLUKF is the replacement of the conventional mean square error loss in the UKF framework with a Generalized Correntropy-Cauchy Fusion Loss. This composite loss integrates the advantages of generalized correntropy, which offers robustness to non-Gaussian disturbances through information-theoretic learning, with the Cauchy loss, which effectively suppresses the influence of outliers. Consequently, the GCCFLUKF can robustly handle a wide range of bad data scenarios while maintaining the UKF's capability to manage strong model nonlinearities. The effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is demonstrated through numerical simulations conducted on the IEEE 14-bus and 30-bus test systems.

title: EnsemBrain: Deep Ensemble Learning with EfficientNet and ResNet for AI-Guided Brain Tumor Diagnosis
authors: Sangeeta Jayebhaye, Aarya Deshpande, Kalpesh Devre, Shubham Devkar, Anurag Deshmukh
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: It is difficult for doctors to diagnose brain tumors. Brain tumors appear significantly differently on MRI scans, making it difficult to detect them early. We designed EnsemBrain, a deep learning model that integrates two neural network architectures— EfficientNetB2 and ResNet50. As collaborators, these two models are well-suited to distinguish between various tumor types (such as gliomas, meningiomas, and pituitary tumors) and also identify normal brain tissue. Our system mitigates the issue of unbalanced data through Focal Loss, and Grad CAM images allow physicians to visualize which areas of the MRI had an impact on the diagnosis. The distinctive aspect of EnsemBrain is that it doesn't just classify tumors but also provides actionable guidance—like whether a patient must see a specialist or take additional scans. We applied our system to a large dataset of brain MRI scans and achieved 98.08% accuracy. It is superior to relying on one model (EfficientNetB2 achieved 95.62%, ResNet50 achieved 94.87%) and surpasses other current methods by 2-3%. The Grad-CAM images were in line with radiologists' results in 96% of instances. Our web-based application is simple for physicians to utilize, offering speedy analysis with easily readable heatmaps to aid enhanced patient care.

title: Emotion-Based Music Recommendation System Using Facial Expression Recognition and Deep Learning
authors: Mala K, Veda H S, Kavana R J, Gagana V S, Pragna S N, Varshitha S
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This paper presents a novel emotion-based music recommendation system that integrates real-time facial expression analysis with personalized music selection using deep learning techniques. The proposed system employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for accurate emotion recognition from facial expressions and automatically suggests music based on the detected emotional state. The system is trained using the FER2013 dataset and utilizes OpenCV for efficient face detection, implemented through a user-friendly graphical interface. Experimental results demonstrate 87.3% accuracy in emotion classification across five fundamental emotions (angry, happy, neutral, sad, surprise) with effective real-time music recommendation capabilities. The system represents a significant advancement in personalized multimedia experiences by seamlessly integrating computer vision, deep learning, and music therapy principles to enhance user emotional well-being through adaptive music selection.

title: ChainAutomate-ERP: A Blockchain-Driven Smart Workflow Framework for Secure and Transparent Enterprise Resource Planning Automation
authors: Murugan Ramu, Sneha T, Hemalakshmi V B, Shwetha S, Rindhya V, Madhu Shree S
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems are pivotal in managing organizational workflows across departments such as finance, human resources, inventory, and procurement. However, traditional ERP systems are prone to inefficiencies including lack of transparency, delayed approvals, data inconsistencies, and audit complexities. This paper introduces ChainAutomate-ERP, a blockchain-powered workflow automation framework that integrates smart contracts, reinforcement learning (A3C) agents, and IPFS-based document storage to enhance ERP operations. The proposed SmartFlowChain-Net architecture ensures tamper-proof workflow execution, decentralized approvals, and AI-driven optimization of task routing and resource utilization. A permissioned blockchain network using Hyperledger Fabric is configured with department-wise node identities and inter-channel communication for secure data exchange. Smart contracts autonomously govern workflows such as invoice processing, purchase orders, and leave approvals, eliminating human error and procedural delays. Reinforcement learning agents trained with historical data significantly improved resource allocation and decision efficiency. The framework was benchmarked against traditional ERP systems across five key performance dimensions. Experimental results demonstrate substantial improvements: average workflow execution time reduced by 58.6%, approval accuracy increased from 88.1% (manual) to 98.2% (blockchain-based), and resource allocation efficiency rose from 69.4% to 91.5%. Moreover, audit time dropped by 63.7%, and integrity check failures per 1000 transactions decreased by over 90%. The findings confirm that ChainAutomate-ERP delivers a secure, transparent, and scalable automation layer for next-generation ERP ecosystems. The integration of AI and blockchain technologies positions the system as a transformative solution for enterprises seeking operational excellence and regulatory compliance.

abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Analysis of a Re-Ignition Fault Occurring in a Filter Circuit Breaker
authors: Hongyuan Li, Ruixiang Zhong, Huangneng Xiao, Hui Jin
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This article focuses on a case of filter circuit breaker re-ignition failure encountered in practical work. Based on the fault recording data and disassembly inspection results, it deeply analyzes the specific causes of re-ignition inside the circuit breaker. At the same time, based on the composition analysis of the ablation material after the failure, it further explores the source of the foreign matter that caused the re-ignition of the circuit breaker. The purpose is to offer valuable insights for subsequent analysis, operational and maintenance management, and repair work related to circuit breaker re-ignition failures. Based on this, suggestions for improving the components of the circuit breaker's arc extinguishing chamber are proposed.

title: Maximizing Green Hydrogen Production from Offshore Wind Without Energy Storage
authors: Elias Nadi, Md Redwan Sammir, Zuyi Li, Jie Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This paper presents a novel control strategy for harnessing renewable energy from offshore wind turbines, focusing on maximizing green hydrogen generation. The major contribution is applying Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) to a high-capacity offshore wind turbine to ensure optimal energy extraction under varying wind conditions. The power extracted is transferred directly to the electrolyzer through a multi modular converter (MMC), a DC link capacitor, and a DC/DC converter without any energy storage systems such as batteries. The proposed system employs Zero d-axis control (ZDC) to maintain stable DC link voltage, MPPT at the Electrolyzer side controller (ESC) for the DC/DC converter to optimize power delivery, and pitch angle control at the turbine side to prevent overshoot and ensure safe operation during high winds. Case studies using the NREL IEA 15 MW reference offshore wind turbine validated the efficiencies of the proposed control strategies across a wide wind speed range. Specifically, MATLAB/Simulink simulations demonstrate the proposed strategy's effectiveness in maintaining DC link voltage stability and maximizing hydrogen production under dynamic conditions. By eliminating reliance on extra energy storage and enabling direct power transfer, this work provides a practical and scalable solution for renewable energy utilization and long-term storage. The proposed approach contributes to industrial decarbonization and broader sustainability goals.

title: Quantifying Capability Boundaries: An Application-Driven Analysis for Large Language Model Selection

title: TDGNet: Two different graph convolutional networks for approximate subgraph matching
authors: Masaki Shirotani, Motoki Amagasaki, Masato Kiyama
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Subgraph matching is a fundamental problem with applications across various domains, involving the identification of a query graph within a larger target graph. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are frequently employed to address this problem. In GNN-based subgraph matching, new node features are generated through convolutional operations that aggregate information from neighboring nodes. This process enables the identification of similar node pairs and facilitates the detection of subgraphs in the target graph that correspond to the query graph.Typically, GNN-based subgraph matching aims to identify subgraphs that perfectly match the query graph, often by using GNNs that share weights between the target and query graphs. However, real-world graph data often contain noise. Consequently, GNNs with shared weights may produce dissimilar node features after convolution, leading to incorrect matching. To address this issue, we propose TDGNet, a model designed for approximate subgraph matching on noisy graphs. TDGNet employs distinct graph convolutional networks for the query and target graphs to ensure the similarity of convolved node features while enhancing robustness to noise. Furthermore, TDGNet introduces a mechanism that concatenates the original node features of the query graph with information aggregated from the target graph via one-way cross-propagation, combined with an edge connectivity loss function, thereby improving the model’s structural understanding of the matched subgraphs. Experimental evaluations on three public datasets with injected noise demonstrate that TDGNet achieves accuracy comparable to or higher than existing learning-based subgraph matching methods. In particular, on the COX2 dataset with 10% noise, TDGNet achieved 91.9% matching accuracy, outperforming the existing method’s 89.9%.

title: Dynamic Compensation of NTC Thermistors Based on LMMOA
authors: Jianfeng Gao, Changming Xue, Longxi Li, Naiguo Han
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In this paper, to address the significant hysteresis in the dynamic response of NTC thermistors due to the influence of protective housings and measurement environments, a Lagged Memory-based Meerkat Optimization Algorithm (LMMOA) is proposed for dynamic response compensation. First, the H-W model of the NTC thermistor is derived, and the hysteresis parameters to be identified are determined. The Meerkat Optimization Algorithm (MOA) is employed to solve for the optimal values of the hysteresis vector. Based on the MOA algorithm, the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is introduced to store the superior and inferior solution sets obtained in each iteration. The short-term memory is used to guide the overall update direction of the lag parameters. A guided spiral global update method and a dimensional reward mechanism are designed to obtain better solutions for each lag factor. A local migration route is set up, and the deviation is redefined to simulate the change of the lag parameters. Meanwhile, a dimensional perturbation strategy is added to retain the optimal solution of each parameter. Finally, solving the H-W inverse model yields the NTC dynamic compensation model, which is applied to NTC dynamic compensation. The results show that after LMMOA compensation, the rise time is improved by approximately 98.3% compared to the original NTC output. LMMOA compensation can effectively improve the dynamic characteristics of NTC and reduce the lag error.

title: Marginal Effect-Driven Participant Selection With Local Differential Privacy for Mobile Crowdsensing
authors: Mengzhe Tian, Yongjiao Sun, Anrui Han, Yishu Wang, Hangxu Ji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: In prior research, spatial coverage has been the primary metric for assessing the quality of mobile network data, with various participant selection strategies developed for data brokers operating within budget constraints. This paper introduces a novel data participant service quality model that prioritizes participant privacy while ensuring creditworthiness and participation willingness. This paper proposes a selection strategy for mobile network data participants that operates under the principles of local differential privacy, designed to maximize the marginal gain under a cost budget constraint. Drawing upon the principle of marginal gains from economics, our strategy advocates for the selection of participants whose marginal gains exceed their associated costs, thereby optimizing the selection process based on relative marginal costs. This paper formalizes this approach as the marginal problem of data participant selection in mobile network data markets, constructing a model and providing theoretical analysis to establish its NP-hard. To address this problem, we develop the Random Response Adaptive Algorithm (RRAA), designed to select participant groups that maximize mobile network data gains. Extensive experiments on both real and simulated datasets demonstrate the algorithm's efficiency and effectiveness. Additionally, to safeguard participant privacy, we propose the Random Response Self-Adaptive Algorithm (RRSAA). Our experimental evaluation on real datasets not only validates the algorithm's privacy-preserving capabilities but also elucidates the impact of privacy preservation parameters on marginal gains.

title: High-Speed Real-Time Acquisition with Staged Gain for Weak Signal Detection in Deep GPR
authors: Jinglin Li, Xianlei Xu, Minghao Sun, Di Zhang, Weiliang Qin, Guangui Zou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Ultra-deep ground-penetrating radar (GPR) serves as a crucial technical tool for detailed exploration of deep geological structures in mines. Conventional equivalent sampling methods suffer from significant errors in capturing deep reflection signals due to propagation attenuation in the medium. While real-time sampling technology substantially enhances data acquisition efficiency, its resolution capability for deep weak signals remains limited by the voltage amplitude of direct waves. This paper presents a segmented-gain high-speed real-time acquisition system designed for capturing deep, weak signals in GPR. By employing a segmented-gain acquisition control method, it effectively collects deep radar echo signals to enhance the resolution capability for deep signal acquisition. Additionally, a segmented data fusion method is proposed. To validate the segmented-gain technique's effectiveness, the gain characteristics of deep weak signals were systematically analyzed using a 100MHz ultra-deep GPR antenna as an example, confirming the radar's detection capabilities. Experimental results demonstrate that deep weak signals acquired with segmented-gain technology exhibit an average gain of 35dB compared to those collected in the no-gain mode. In confined-space air experiments, the proposed method achieved a detection depth of 26.02m with a detection error of 1.08% in gain mode. This compares to a detection depth of 12.24m and an error of 5.56% in no-gain mode, representing a 13.78m increase in detection depth and a 4.48% reduction in error. In engineering applications, the system enables geological stratum detection at depths up to 18m. This technology provides effective technical support for ultra-deep geological precision surveying in mines.

title: Multi-Source Assessment of Machine Learning Models and Atmospheric Correction Products for Chlorophyll- a Retrieval in Meso‑Eutrophic Waters: Application to Hamilton Harbour, an Area of Concern in Lake Ontario, Canada
authors: Ali Reza Shahvaran, Amir Masoud Chegoonian, Homa Kheyrollah Pour, Mir Amir Mohammad Reshadi, Philippe Van Cappellen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Freely available multispectral satellite imagery enables frequent monitoring of lake Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a), but retrieval accuracy depends strongly on both atmospheric correction (AC) and model choice. Using more than 600 pixel–in-situ matchups (2000–2023) from more than 240 Landsat-5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 imagery in western Lake Ontario (±4-day window; 3×3 pixel means), we benchmarked four radiometric products (Level-1, Level-2, DOS, ACOLITE) with four machine learning models (LASSO, MDN, SVR, XGBoost). ACOLITE paired with XGBoost was consistently best on the held-out test sets: RMSLE = 0.34 (L5), 0.43 (L7), 0.56 (L8), and 0.42 (S2), with slopes ~0.7, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.7, respectively. Minimally corrected products (Level-1, DOS1) performed poorly (test RMSLE ≳ 0.6; slopes ≲ 0.1). For Landsat-8, the Provisional Aquatic Reflectance generalized better than LaSRC (test slope ≈ 0.6 vs 0.1; RMSLE 0.59 vs 0.68), but ACOLITE remained strongest overall. Against semi-empirical indices, ML—especially XGBoost—reduced errors and produced fits closer to unity across sensors. We then mapped seasonal Chl-a in Hamilton Harbour (2000–2024) to showcase model application. Monthly means rose from late spring to midsummer (May 13.2 μg L⁻¹; June 11.5; July 19.2; August 20.2), with persistent nearshore hotspots near the Royal Botanical Gardens and Windermere Basin. Mann–Kendall trend analyses during bloom months indicated little monotonic change: mean Sen's slopes were near zero (|mean| ≤ 2×10⁻⁴ μg L⁻¹ day⁻¹) and <15% of pixels showed significant trends per month. Thus, interannual variability dominates over simple linear trends. Overall, harmonizing radiometry with ACOLITE and deploying XGBoost provides a practical, cross-sensor pathway for robust, long-term Chl-a mapping, demonstrated here for a meso-eutrophic embayment of Western Lake Ontario.

title: Distributed task allocation and collaborative obstacle avoidance in multi-AGV systems
authors: Yefeng Liu, Zeyu Zhang, Linze Song, Jingjing Liu, Qichun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: This paper addresses real-time task allocation and path conflict resolution for heterogeneous agents in large-scale automated guided vehicle (AGV) systems within smart manufacturing. To overcome limitations of existing methods in dynamic and high-density environments, we propose a distributed market-based task allocation algorithm integrated with a cooperative multi-AGV obstacle avoidance strategy to ensure coordinated motion planning. A dynamic task buffer pool suppress system oscillations caused by frequent task arrivals. In addition, an incremental path caching mechanism is developed to reduce redundant computations during the 2-opt optimization process. The proposed Two-level priority mechanism effectively resolves multi-AGV collaborative obstacle avoidance and path conflict issues. Compared with existing approaches, the proposed method maintains robust conflict resolution among multiple AGVs while reducing task replanning frequency and computational cost. A standardized task allocation comparison framework is constructed to evaluate system performance under varying task scales. Simulation results based on real manufacturing layouts validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed hybrid algorithm. The results provide practical guidance for efficient task scheduling and collaborative obstacle avoidance in industrial AGV systems.

title: Deterministic Delay-Aware Task Scheduling over In-Network Computing: A Graph Embedding-Based DRL Approach
authors: Jing Gao, Lei Feng, Fanqin Zhou, Mianxiong Dong, Peng Yu, Kaoru Ota, Xuesong Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: As the in-network computing (INC) paradigm evolves, efficient scheduling of dependent tasks within complex network systems becomes increasingly crucial. The network needs to handle high-level resource demands while adhering to strict latency requirements. Deterministic delay constraints are particularly critical in applications that rely on directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). To address this challenge, we first propose a deterministic delay-aware task scheduling optimization problem over INC to maximize resource utilization and ensure task acceptance. We accurately establish the complex deterministic delay constraint through traffic arrival and service curves and utilize network calculus for conversion to facilitate solving. Then, we further transform the task optimization problem into MDP and develop a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm that combines graph neural network (GNN) and delay-aware proximal policy optimization (DPPO) to solve it, called the Deterministic Delay-aware Task Scheduling (DDTS) scheme. It utilizes multilayer GNN to handle task dependencies and applies the DPPO algorithm to introduce deterministic delay penalty factors to evaluate policy operations, achieving optimal task scheduling. The simulation results demonstrate the significant advantages of the DDTS scheme over existing algorithms and task scheduling schemes in terms of task acceptance rate and resource utilization.

title: High Performance Signal Design for Optical OFDM Systems Using Variational Autoencoder
authors: Nam N. Luong, Chuyen T. Nguyen, Thanh V. Pham
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper proposes a design of low peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), low symbol error rate (SER), and high data rate signal for free-space optical communication (FSO) systems employing optical orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems. Specifically, we consider a high-altitude platform (HAP)-to-ground FSO link where the transmitted optical signals are affected by atmospheric attenuation and Gamma-Gamma distributed turbulence. The proposed design leverages a variational autoencoder (VAE) incorporating gradual loss learning to jointly optimize the geometry and probability of the constellation's symbols. This not only enhances mutual information (MI) but also effectively reduces the PAPR while maintaining a low SER for reliable transmission. We evaluate the performance of the proposed VAE-based design by comparing the MI, SER, and PAPR against existing techniques. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves a considerably lower PAPR while maintaining superior SER and MI performance for a wide range of optical signal-to-noise ratios (OSNRs).

title: ArcLight-AttGRU: An Ultra-Lightweight Attention-Gated GRU Framework for Low-Frequency Series Arc Fault Detection
authors: Yannan Guo, Jianling Yao, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Series arc faults are a significant cause of electrical fires and equipment failures in power systems, highlighting the need for robust and efficient detection technologies. However, existing methods often require high-frequency data acquisition or lack the necessary adaptability and transparency for deployment on resource-constrained devices. To address these challenges, we propose ArcLight-AttGRU, an ultra-lightweight and interpretable detection framework specifically designed for terminal measurement applications. Unlike traditional methods that rely on high-frequency sampling (kHz-MHz range), ArcLight-AttGRU achieves accurate arc fault detection using standard grid sampling rates (e.g., 60 Hz), thus reducing both hardware and system complexity. The proposed framework integrates multi-scale motif analysis with a lightweight design, which together facilitate the extraction of key disturbance features from current signals. The model combines depthwise separable convolution, gated recurrent units (GRU), and an attention mechanism for both local and global temporal modeling. Additionally, structured pruning and attribution-based interpretability are incorporated to ensure efficient and transparent deployment on embedded platforms. Extensive experiments conducted on the ArcSafe-60 dataset demonstrate that ArcLight-AttGRU achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy (99.98% pre-pruning, 99.86% post-pruning with a 48% reduction in parameters), while maintaining minimal memory usage and sub-millisecond inference latency. Comparative evaluations further confirm the framework’s superior generalization and robustness across various load and circuit scenarios. The code repository is available at https://github.com/GYMN-C/ArcLight-AttGRU.

title: Resilient Adaptive Hybrid-Triggered Control for Cyber-Physical Direct-Drive-Wheel Systems Under Hybrid Cyberattacks
authors: Wen-Hu Chen, Chuan-Ke Zhang, Kang-Zhi Liu, Yuan-Hang Yang, Yong He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article addresses the problem of resilient <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_\infty$</tex-math></inline-formula> control for direct-drive-wheel (DDW) systems by developing an adaptive hybrid-triggered (AHT) scheme that improves security and communication efficiency against hybrid cyberattacks. First, the attacked DDW system is modeled as a switched cyber-physical system (CPS) to effectively capture the impact of such attacks on signal transmission. Then, an attack-information-dependent AHT scheme is proposed, where triggering instants are determined by system states and attack information, and the adaptive threshold is tuned via a self-adaptive differential evolution (DE) algorithm to balance dynamic performance and communication frequency. By constructing a Lyapunov functional, sufficient conditions are derived to co-design the control gain and AHT parameters, ensuring global exponential stability and guaranteed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_\infty$</tex-math></inline-formula> performance. Finally, the proposed method is validated on examples, showing its efficacy in maintaining system stability while considerably reducing communication overhead.

title: Talk Structurally, Act Hierarchically: A Collaborative Refinement Framework for LLM Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Zhao Wang, Sota Moriyama, Wei-Yao Wang, Briti Gangopadhyay, Shingo Takamatsu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: While recent advancements in Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent (LLM-MA) systems have shown promise, significant challenges remain in managing communication and refinement when agents collaborate on complex tasks. In this paper, we propose Talk Structurally, Act Hierarchically (TalkHier), a novel framework that introduces a structured communication protocol for context-rich exchanges and a hierarchical refinement system to address issues such as incorrect outputs, falsehoods, and biases. TalkHier surpasses various types of SoTA, including inference scaling model (OpenAI-o1), open-source multi-agent models (e.g., AgentVerse), and majority voting strategies on current LLM and single-agent baselines (e.g., ReAct, GPT4o), across diverse tasks, including open-domain question answering, domain-specific selective questioning, and practical advertisement text generation. These results highlight its potential to set a new standard for LLM-MA systems, paving the way for more effective, adaptable, and collaborative multi-agent frameworks. The source code is available at https://github.com/sony/talkhier.
