title: A Novel Data Fusion Method of Redundant Gyroscope Systems with Non-Uniform Accuracy
authors: Can Li, Shishi Li, Wenxin Sun, Zhichao Feng, Yiyang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In the scenario of sensor performance degradation, the predictive management of the inertial navigation system needs to maintain reliable accuracy. However, traditional methods are difficult to effectively handle the problem of data fusion of gyroscope with heterogeneous accuracy and dynamic degradation. This paper proposes a new data fusion method for redundant gyroscope systems based on reinforcement learning. This method adopts the soft actor-critic (SAC) reinforcement learning algorithm, breaking through the limitations of traditional models that rely on pre-defined noise models or system models. It achieves adaptive real-time optimization of fusion weights through dynamic interaction with the environment. Based on the principle of information non-increase, a three-stage data fusion model is constructed to precisely match the entire life cycle state of the gyroscope from mild degradation, moderate degradation to severe degradation. To solve the problem of objective determination of stage boundaries, a intersection point detection network is designed. Through convolution feature extraction, bidirectional GRU temporal modeling and multi-branch fusion architecture with difference perception, the critical transition point of sensor stability mutation is automatically identified. Experiments are verified on the inertial instrument control platform. The results show that the accuracy loss in the mild degradation stage is only 7.67%. The fusion accuracy in the severe degradation stage is improved by more than 16% compared to the weighted least squares method (WLS) and the fruit fly optimization weighted least squares method (FFO-WLS), and the single-step inference time is as low as 0.82ms. It has the characteristics of high accuracy, strong adaptability and efficient execution, providing active maintenance support for predictive management.

title: Sensorless Control of PMSM Based on Complex Coefficient Filter-based Fixed-Time ESO and Novel Cost Function-based PLL
authors: Zhimin Zhang, Yongting Deng, Hongwen Li, Yuxin Kang, Wenjie Li, Haiyang Cao, Gaolin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a novel sensorless control strategy for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) based on complex coefficient filter-based fixed-time extended state observer (CCF-FESO) and novel cost function-based finite-position- set phase-locked loop (NCF-PLL). Firstly, the CCF-FESO is proposed by integrating a complex coefficient filter (CCF) with a fixed-time convergent extended state observer (FESO). This novel approach overcomes the inherent phase lag and poor sinusoidal tracking performance of conventional linear ESO, enabling accurate estimation of back electromotive force (BEMF). Secondly, the NCF-PLL is designed based on a novel Newton-iteration cost function, which removes the dependency on auxiliary algorithms, thereby simplifying the computational structure while enhancing theoretical precision. Finally, experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy achieves a significant improvement in estimation performance, reducing the steady-state position root mean square error (RMSE) by approximately 15.8% and decreasing the maximum dynamic error from 13.1° to below 4.2°, thereby confirming its feasibility and superiority.

title: A Electricity-Carbon Coordinated Operation Method for Energy Storage in Distribution Networks with Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Feng Zhao, Xuli Wang, Xiang Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The integration of high proportions of renewable energy under the dual carbon targets has a significant impact on the operation of distribution networks. In future, the increased uncertainty and electro-carbon coordination in distribution networks might pose new challenges for energy storage regulation. Thus, this paper proposes an electricity-carbon coordinated energy storage opeartion method that considers multiple uncertainties. A deep reinforcement learning-based Markov decision process is adopted to handle real-time fluctuations of generation and load, and a rolling optimizationbased electrcity-carbon coordinated opeartion method is developed considering actual grid trading mechanisms. Simulation results show that the proposed approach enables effective regulation of all components within the distribution network under complex uncertain conditions. Compared with conventional strategies, the proposed method significantly reduces system operation costs and carbon emissions, providing a feasible technical pathway for achieving low-carbon and economic objectives in distribution networks.

title: Impact and Rebound of Droplets with Different Conductivities on Typical Insulating Surfaces Under an Electric Field
authors: Wenxuan Zhou, Longchuan Cong, Quanlong Chen, Xuetong Zhu, Shuoyan Zhang, Fanghui Yin
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Icing and accretion pose serious threats to the longterm operation of power transmission lines. To reveal the coupled effects of electric field, surface material, and droplet properties on icing dynamics, an experimental platform was established. Under a tunable electric field and on superhydrophobic surfaces, high-speed imaging was employed to quantify droplet spreading and rebound coefficients during impact. Comparative experiments between low conductivity and saline droplets show that conductivity and field strength jointly regulate polarization and energy exchange. At low fields, saline droplets exhibit longer contact and rebound times, which decrease more rapidly with increasing field strength; at high fields, their rebound coefficients rise more significantly. These findings provide valuable insights and guidance for optimizing anti-icing surface design and electric-field configuration of transmission lines.

title: Directional Pilot Protection for Active Distribution Networks Based on Sensitivity Zone Partitioning of Directional Elements
authors: Jianhong Huang, Wenwang Hu, Bolong Zhai, Quan Chen, Jiansheng Guo, Yeqing Lin
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Traditional directional elements relying on positive-sequence fault components face reliability challenges due to the nonlinear fault characteristics of IIDGs. By partitioning the directional element's impedance plane into forward sensitivity, reverse sensitivity, and indeterminate zones, faults are discriminated using phase-angle comparisons for sensitivity zones and current magnitude thresholds for indeterminate zones. A differential pilot protection criterion is established, integrating directional judgments from adjacent nodes. Simulations on a 10 kV PSCAD model validate the scheme's effectiveness in distinguishing internal/external faults, addressing synchronization and sensitivity issues in IIDG-rich environments.

title: Theoretical Analysis of GOOSE Communication Security Enhancement Based on SM9 and Simulation Verification on SimuNPS
authors: Bin Xiang, Jinpeng Song, Junpeng Chen, Shenglin Guo
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The GOOSE network at the bay level of smart substations carries critical protection and control commands, and its communication security directly affects the stable operation of the grid. The international standard IEC 62351 recommends using digital signatures to ensure the integrity and authenticity of GOOSE messages; however, traditional PKI/CA-based certificate systems face challenges such as complex key management and difficult certificate updates when applied to a large number of IEDs in the process layer. To address this problem, this paper proposes a GOOSE security enhancement scheme based on the national cryptographic algorithm SM9, an identity-based cryptography technology. This scheme uses the logical identifier (e.g., GoCBReference) of IED devices as the public key, fundamentally eliminating the need for digital certificate management. First, the architecture of the scheme, the key management process, and the communication mechanism for GOOSE message signing and verification are elaborated. Then, the SimuNPS-FlexNet network simulation toolbox, integrated with a real SM9 algorithm library, is used to construct a functional verification environment that includes legitimate devices and malicious attackers. This study demonstrates that SM9 technology provides a feasible and efficient approach to solving the secure communication problem between the process layer and bay level of smart substations.

title: Multi-Scenario Stochastic Optimal Dispatch for Regional Power Grid Based on ETI Comprehensive Evaluation System
authors: Moyan Zhu, Kaiming Zou, Kailong Zhang, Shuai Fan, Renke Huang, Guangyu He
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Typical large-scale regional power grids in China face fundamental challenges in balancing supply security and renewable energy consumption due to the strong stochasticity of wind and solar capacity exceeding one-third of total installations. This paper proposes a multi-scenario stochastic dispatch method based on the Energy Transition Index (ETI). The method employs triangular probability distribution to model uncertainties in renewable output and load forecasts, generating Monte Carlo samples reduced to typical scenarios through K-means clustering. The ETI integrates five weighted sub-indicators: Regional Power Shortage Rate, Peak-Valley Load Difference Ratio, Regional Renewable Curtailment Rate, Regional Renewable Utilization Rate, and normalized cost. The mixed-integer programming model incorporates thermal unit commitment constraints and pumped storage bidirectional regulation. Validation using actual operational data demonstrates significant ETI improvement, with substantially reduced power shortage and renewable curtailment, markedly increased renewable utilization, and solution times meeting day-ahead scheduling requirements. The method effectively resolves the conflict between supply security and renewable consumption under high renewable penetration, providing a practical technical solution for regional grid dispatch.

title: Priority Encoding-Based Genetic Algorithm for Four-Dimensional Vehicle-to-Grid Scheduling Optimization
authors: Pengyu Wang, Xiaolan Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) scheduling involves four-dimensional optimization: vehicle selection, station allocation, pile assignment, and temporal sequencing. Traditional genetic algorithms suffer from combinatorial explosion when directly encoding these decisions, generating massive infeasible solutions. This paper proposes a Priority Encoding-Based Genetic Algorithm (PBGA) that transforms the four-dimensional space into one-dimensional priority sequences, eliminating infeasible solutions through sequential decoding. The algorithm incorporates an adaptive vehicle revenue parameter <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> mechanism for balancing fairness and efficiency. Experimental validation demonstrates PBGA achieves near-optimal quality with significant computational improvements over exhaustive search and consistent superiority over greedy algorithms.

title: Coordinated Operation of Photovoltaic, Energy Storage, and Load for Enhancing Distribution Network Resilience Under Extreme Scenarios
authors: Wenwei Yang, Xiaohua Li, Junqiang Gong, Yun Li, Uzbekov Mirsoli, Olimova Nodira
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the integration of high penetration renewable energy, its randomness and uncertainty can easily lead to voltage and frequency fluctuation at the end of the power grid, protection malfunction, and reduced fault ride-through capability. Therefore, high penetration of renewable energy brings new challenges, risks, and opportunities for enhancing the resilience of distribution networks under extreme scenarios. On this basis, this paper establishes an overall framework for improving distribution network resilience, covering four key aspects: energy storage optimal allocation, operation control and protection, risk regulation, coordinated restoration. Subsequently, considering the phases of pre-disaster prevention, response during disaster, and post-disaster recovery, the paper systematically analyzes technical pathways for enhancing distribution network resilience under extreme scenarios through the coordinated operation of photovoltaic, energy storage, and load resources. Finally, the key steps for implementing the technical pathways and engineering demonstration applications are presented.

title: Non-Linear Influence Analysis for Adaptive Fraud Prevention in FinTech Using Hybrid Learning Models
authors: E. Anant Shankar, Abdul Rishad, Prasanth S.P, Dev Kumar, Vijay Kumar Dwivedi, V. Venkata Ramana
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The fast development of FinTech platforms has made the detection of complex financial fraud increasingly difficult, due to the dynamic nature of fraudulent activities and the complexity of transaction data. Conventional fraud detection systems sometimes neglect to recognise non-linear and temporal correlations essential for spotting such patterns. The proposed study introduces an innovative hybrid learning framework that combines Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) with Bidirectional LSTM to deal with this issue. The suggested system employs non-linear influence analysis through SHAP and ALE methodologies to improve interpretability and decision-making reliability. Data preprocessing includes advanced feature engineering, whereas the model's adaptive element facilitates retraining in response to real-time drift detection. Experiments utilising publicly accessible fraud datasets illustrate the superiority of the proposed model compared to conventional classifiers such as Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and independent LSTM models. The proposed system attained an accuracy of 98.3%, an F1-score of 0.961, and an AUC-ROC of 0.992, outperforming all baseline models in critical parameters. Its extensive generalisation capacity and adaptability to changing fraud trends make it an appropriate solution for practical FinTech applications. The proposed research advances the area by presenting a scalable, interpretable, and high-performance architecture for fraud detection aimed at enhancing financial security.

title: An Intelligent Framework for Waste Material Classification Using Deep Learning
authors: Vijay A Sangolgi, Mithun B Patil, Gaurav N Biradar, Vishwajeet Madhavrao Waghmare, Onkar Siddheshwar Jamma
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The bane of waste management in modern society has been the increasing pace of urbanization and the various recyclable and non-recyclable materials' growing complexity. Manual waste segregation that is traditionally done by hand is not only inefficient, but it is also error-prone and labor-intensive, and that's why it has become necessary to have intelligent and automated solutions. Essentially, this document presents a novel AI-powered approach to waste classification, which would allow the categorization of common rubbish into different classes, namely plastic, metal, glass, paper, organic, and hazardous waste. We utilized several publicly available datasets, namely Trash Net and Garbage Classification V2, and ran data augmentation experiments. As a result, we implemented, trained, and tested various deep neural networks architectures such as CNN, ResNet, Dense Net, and their combined ones like CNN–SVM and GC Net.The experimental outcomes report the system's ability to correctly classify with the accuracy of more than 95% in the case of fine-tuned DenseNet121 and up to 97.5% when using hybrid ensemble approaches. Besides, the real-time integration with IoT-enabled smart bins and edge deployment emphasize the system's practical feasibility for applications in the smart city. This new mechanism not only instills recycling effectiveness and reduces human intervention, besides this, it can also provide extensive and enduring waste management solutions. Prospects for this work may include the use of transformer-based vision models and the provision of reinforcement learning to cater for the adaptation of the waste stream's dynamics. The graph named "Female Residuals" presents the relation between the height of the women and the corresponding residual numbers, that is the differences between the observed arm length and the linear regression model predicted. The "Female Residuals" graph depicts the connection between female heights and residual values, the residuals being the differences between those observed changed stretches, and predicted by the use of the linear regression model. Now let's talk about the graph named "Female Residuals" which depicts the relationship between the height of female individuals and the residual numbers that refer to the differences between the observed arm length and the predicted arm length obtained from the linear regression model. Each mark on the graph corresponds to the specific height measurement of a particular female individual. Residuals are the differences between the observed arm span and the predicted arm span. The residuals range from around -6 to +6 indicating that the measurements have a large range. The distribution of the points does not highlight any trend, thus it is quite plausible that the regression model functions properly for this population. Several points are located close to zero, suggesting that the observed arm spans are close to the predicted ones, while a few outliers indicate that the deviations from the model predictions are considerable. Thus the plot is generally useful to the determination of the model's accuracy, and it reveals an excellent performance of the subset of female students, although the outliers may require further study to identify individual factors in body proportions.

title: Deep Learning-Assisted Progeria Diagnosis Using GAN-Enhanced Medical Image Augmentation
authors: Raavi Hemalatha, Doondi Kumar Janapala
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Progeria disease is a rare genetic disorder marked by accelerated aging in children, where early detection remains challenging due to limited and imbalanced datasets. This study employed Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based data augmentation using DCGAN, WGAN, and StyleGAN to generate synthetic medical images, which were combined with real datasets for training classifiers. Among the models, Convolutional Neural Network achieved the highest accuracy of 96.22%, outperforming Support Vector Machine and Random Forest. DCGAN produced the most realistic synthetic images with 95.16% discriminator accuracy, while practitioner evaluation confirmed improved diagnostic confidence and efficiency. Overall, GAN-based augmentation enhanced data diversity, reduced overfitting, and strengthened classifier generalization, enabling more robust and reliable early detection of Progeria.

title: Logistic Regression-Based Hearing Test Analysis for Personalized Audiological Assessment
authors: E. Hemavathi, R. Meenakshi, R. Kamaraj, M. Revathi, M. Jagadeeswaran, C Srinivasan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This work provides a logistic regression (LR) method for analyzing hearing test data to facilitate personalized audiological evaluation. Employing the publicly accessible Kaggle dataset, including 5000 records that include age, physical examination scores, and binary hearing test results (pass/fail), constructed a supervised classification model to forecast hearing outcomes. Following conventional preprocessing and data partitioning, the LR model attained a classification accuracy of 99.7% on the test dataset. Age and physical score were identified as robust indicators, with advancing age substantially correlated with a heightened probability of failing the hearing test. The approach provides a personalized potential score for everyone, delivering a comprehensible forecast of auditory performance. This enables the identification of vulnerable people and promotes proactive hearing health measures. Due to its simplicity, openness, and computational speed, the model is well suited for incorporation into telemedicine platforms, mobile screening tools, and extensive public health monitoring. The results indicate that LR, despite its simplicity, may achieve remarkable performance in audiological classification tasks when trained on well-organized health information.

title: Comparative Study of Machine Learning Models and XAI Methods with Statistical Analysis
authors: Rethika K Jegan, Suprajha Baskaran, Sarada Jayan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The practicality of machine learning models in high stakes decision making is still severely limited by the computational and stability constraints of traditional explainability techniques as well as fidelity constraints. The traditional methods like SHAP although they give useful information, are associated with severe trade-offs between the performance at runtime, consistency, and local fidelity that preclude their real-life application. The paper introduces an extended benchmarking framework of explainability algorithms in tabular classification problems, comparing SHAP, Attention-based, and Permutation explanations on 5 crucial dimensions: faithfulness, stability, efficiency, robustness and completeness. The Adult Income dataset was used to validate the framework where a CatBoost classifier reached better predictive performance with 87.6 accuracy, 0.714 F1-score and ROC-AUC of 0.930. The rigorous statistical testing showed the presence of significant performance differences (p < 0.001) with large effect sizes in all measures. The use of attention-based explanations proved to be the best with a high degree of faithfulness (0.812), efficiency (0.9995), and robustness (0.986), and SHAP was the most stable (0.990) and had the same performance in all attempts. The results provide empirically-grounded principles of selection of explainability methods showing that algorithmic compatibility between explanation methods and model structures is the key to the interpretation of explainability.

title: Low-NF and Broadband Cascode CMOS LNA Using a Transmission-Line Transformer
authors: Taerim Noh, Sangjin Yoo, Munsu Jeong, Kyutaek Oh, Geuntae Kim, Ilku Nam, Ockgoo Lee
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a broadband low-noise amplifier (LNA) using a transmission-line transformer (TLT)-based output matching network (OMN) and magnetically coupled inductors for input and noise optimization using 65-nm bulk CMOS technology. The proposed TLT-based OMN extends the bandwidth by providing broadband transimpedance gain, achieving a flat response with a peak of approximately 36.5 dB across the operating band. In the input matching network (IMN), magnetic coupling between two input inductors is introduced to compromise between S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {11}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and noise figure (NF). This coupling improves S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {11}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> by regulating the tradeoff between NF and S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {11}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Measurement results show a 3-dB gain–bandwidth from 16.8 to 28.8 GHz with a 13.1-dB peak gain, −10-dB input return loss bandwidth from 17.3 to 39.4 GHz, 2.07-dB minimum NF, −10.3-dBm peak IP<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {1dB}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and 1.5-dBm peak IIP<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {3}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. The LNA occupies 0.162mm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\mathbf {2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of the core area and consumes 25.2 mW of DC power.

title: Multitasking Optimization to Three-echelons UAVs forms logistic system to cover mobile signals in sprawling mountain roads effectively
authors: Ahmed M. Abed, Ali AlArjani
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The process of addressing customer requests is a strategy designed to fulfil them in time; it must be free of lateness or negligible shortfalls, as these will lead to higher expenses for the business. Consequently, it must carry out a whole aggregate request handling to tackle a determined number of customer requests (Kanban) according to the capacity owned by the communication companies (i.e., scheduling process). Digital twin (DT) has been tailored to simulate the efficiency of hybridization of ARIMA and SR-tree technique in forecasting, handling, and fulfilling the customers' requests (edges) via Three-echelons Flying Logistic System (TEFLS), where echelons consist of multi-suppliers (e.g., Base-Station Antenna Towers), a manufacturer (e.g., UAVs that handle the customer requests), and multi-customers (i.e., who create the requests). The problem lies in covering service in zones that have weakened signals of base-station towers due to sprawling mountain areas. Therefore, using UAVs to be mediators to load the signal from the nearest tower to connect with any requested edge. The DT helps in determining the diameter of UAVs service zones, the capacity of UAVs handling requests in time, and the handling policy that follows Multitasking Echelons (MTE) toward Multitasking optimization (MTO) to be high performing. UAVs receive four types of customer’ requests, each of which has subclasses. First are regular telephony services that embed emergency calls that improved by approximately 63%, while the second service is a download/upload service and includes GPS location search that improved by 72% and therapeutic alerts according to the aforementioned request from the customer to receive the service as contracted with the organised company, which improved by 93%. Finally, there is browsing service and online business that improved by 89%.

title: Wafer-Scale Low-Temperature Measurements of Transition-Edge Sensors During Fabrication
authors: Joel C. Weber, Jason E. Nobles, Robinjeet Singh, Avirup Roy, Kelsey M. Morgan, Nathan J. Ortiz, Jeff Van Lanen, Kyuyoung Bae, Shannon M. Duff, Daniel S. Swetz, Galen C. O'Neil, Joel N. Ullom, Daniel R. Schmidt
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: The development of large-scale, high-resolution transition-edge sensor (TES) arrays requires precise, wafer-scale control of the superconducting critical temperature (Tc). For microcalorimeters, this is often achieved with a superconductor-normal metal bilayer where the thickness ratio between the two materials determines Tc through the proximity effect. Reliably achieving a specific Tc across multiple wafers with good uniformity remains challenging as the fabrication process is highly sensitive to substrate quality, deposition parameters, and deposited thin-film thickness. Missing a Tc target results in an array with degraded or unusable performance and wastes fabrication/measurement resources. To help solve these limitations with TES microcalorimeter fabrication, we have developed a method of measuring 76.2 mm wafers after bilayer deposition to screen for Tc accuracy and uniformity. Four-wire resistance measurements of test structures across the wafer provide rapid feedback early in the fabrication process at temperatures down to 20 mK. We leverage the hybrid additive-subtractive TES process to screen TES arrays with multiple Tc values on a single wafer comprised of a single bilayer. After measurement, the bilayer normal metal thickness can be increased with an additional evaporation step to lower Tc or decreased with an ion mill step to raise Tc as needed. We also present a design to expand measurement capability to 150 mm wafers to screen both larger microcalorimeter arrays as well as NIST cosmic microwave background TES bolometer arrays.

title: Adaptive Slice Handover for Cloud-Native Vehicular Edge Networks
authors: Jiadai Wang, Pengju Li, Yongpeng Shi, Jiajia Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Cloud-native vehicular edge networks enable efficient and scalable service delivery by deploying containerized functions close to vehicles. To accommodate diverse service requirements, edge slices are configured as logically isolated virtual networks with dedicated resources and tailored performance. As vehicles traverse different edge domains and service demands evolve, slice handover becomes essential to ensure service continuity. However, most existing approaches focus mainly on mobility, overlooking the dynamics of service requirements and the heterogeneity of slice types. To address this gap, we propose a deep reinforcement learning-based adaptive slice handover (DASH) scheme for vehicular edge networks, which jointly consider mobility patterns, service dynamics, and slice diversity for optimized decision-making. In addition, a multi-factor scoring function is introduced to assess handover decisions in real time, taking into account resource availability, communication reliability, and decision stability. Simulation results demonstrate that DASH significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of handover success rate and decision effectiveness.

title: 170–400 GHz Ultra-Wideband and Low-Loss Power Splitter Based on Dielectric Silicon Rib Waveguides
authors: Zhu Liang, Feifan Han, Zebin Huang, Zhuoyue Wen, Weichao Li, Xiongbin Yu, Xiaofeng Tao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: In this letter, an ultra-wideband 3-dB power splitter based on all-dielectric high-resistivity silicon rib waveguide is designed, fabricated, and experimentally validated. The device is composed of input–output tapers, adiabatic tapered coupler, and s-bend rib waveguides. Simulation results indicate that the device achieves an ultra-wide 3-dB bandwidth of 462.6 GHz. Experimental characterization confirms that the splitter exhibits an average insertion loss of 0.32 dB and balanced power division with an absolute imbalance of less than 0.6 dB over the 170–400 GHz range, which covers the full WR-4.3 and WR-2.8 bands. In additionally, the proposed 3-dB power splitter device is ideally suited for broadband and low-loss power division and provides a robust and scalable foundation for integrated THz system.

title: A hybrid prognostic method based on CEEMDAN-KPCA and BiLSTM-Transformer for bearing remaining useful life prediction
authors: Guoxu Wang, Siyu Liu, Weidong Jiao, Zhilin Dong, Wanxiu Xu, Jianfeng Sun, Xiaohao Chen, Yonghua Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The prediction of the remaining useful life (RUL) of rolling bearings plays a crucial role in preventing major failures and enabling predictive maintenance. To address bearing fault prediction, a novel hybrid method is proposed. The developed methodology integrates a two-stage prognostic framework. Initially, complete ensemble empirical mode decomposition with adaptive noise adaptively decomposes vibration signals to extract multi-domain features from IMFs, which are transformed into a robust health indicator using a kernel principal component analysis-based approach for nonlinear dimensionality reduction, whereby principal components accounting for 85% of the cumulative variance are retained, with the first principal component selected for precise degradation onset detection using the 3δ rule. Subsequently, the preprocessed sequences are fed into a dual-branch network where BiLSTM captures bidirectional temporal dependencies while Transformer encoder models global contextual patterns through self-attention for RUL prediction. The proposed model is ultimately validated using two publicly available rolling bearing datasets. The analysis results show that our method achieves relatively high prediction accuracy. Compared with other methods, it reduces MAE and RMSE by approximately 10%-30% . Under consistent testing conditions, it outperforms comparative methods, confirming its robust generalization capability across different operational environments and high precision in RUL prediction.

title: Seeing What Few-Shot Learners See: Contrastive Cross-Class Attribution for Explainability
authors: Lingfeng Chen, Panhe Hu, Zhen Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Few-shot learning (FSL) enables deep learning models to generalize to unseen categories with minimal labeled data, making it crucial for data-constrained domains such as healthcare. However, existing FSL models often lack explainability, obscuring the reasoning behind their predictions and limiting trust in their deployment. In this work, we introduce contrastive cross-class attribution (C3A) to enhance explainability in FSL. Specifically, we present C3A for explaining query predictions and its variant (C3A-E) for episodic learning. Unlike prior explainability methods designed for specific architectures, our approach is classifier-agnostic, leveraging the backbone network to ensure broad applicability across diverse FSL paradigms. In addition, we propose contrastive local descriptors that integrate naive-Bayes nearest-neighbor (NBNN) and Fisher score-based attribution to preserve fine-grained details and enhance interclass discrimination. Finally, we comprehensively evaluate our methods across four FSL benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements over state-of-the-art approaches, with absolute gains of 19.72% in insertion-area-under-curve (iAUC) and 25.88% in deletion-area-above-curve (dAAC). Our posthoc design ensures seamless integration with various architectures, including Conv64F, ResNet12, ResNet18, and ViT. The experimental results confirm that C3A and its variant (C3A-E) provide more stable and interpretable explanations compared to existing FSL explainability methods, offering deeper insights into the episodic learning process. We have made our code publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/MountainChenCad/C3A</uri>

title: Learning Optimal Policies With Local Observations for Cooperative Multiagent Reinforcement Learning
authors: He Kong, Qianli Xing, Qi Wang, Hechang Chen, Runliang Niu, Zhiyi Duan, Shiqi Wang, Yi Chang, Irwin King
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The cooperative multiagent reinforcement learning (MARL) has been widely used in many practical applications. Despite its success, a fundamental issue arises in MARL that agents face the dilemma of whether to select the best action to maximize rewards or to acquire more information collectively by exploring the novel states/actions due to partial observability. To solve this issue, existing methods merge exploration and exploitation methods. However, these methods are always suboptimal and may lead to failure in finishing tasks. In this article, we theoretically prove the existence of a latent state that can guarantee the optimal individual and global policies. Moreover, we prove that such a latent state can be approximately obtained by local observations. Based on the analysis, we propose a method named unified MARL (UMARL), which is a weighted value function factorization approach unifying exploitation and exploration in one framework. Specifically, we design the agent representation network (ARN) and individual weighting networks (IWNs) to learn agents’ unified representations and weights of credit. Moreover, a latent state regularizer (LSR) is designed to encourage agents’ representations to approximate the latent state. Extensive experiments show that UMARL can achieve superior performance compared with 12 state-of-the-art methods on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$m$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-step matrix game, level-based foraging (LBF), StarCraft II, and Google research football (GRF). The source code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/CrazyBayes/UMARL</uri>

title: ISPCBPlace: A Gradient-Based Placement Method for Irregular-Shaped PCBs under Complex Design Constraints
authors: Lei Cai, Jixin Zhang, Ke Cheng, Haiyun Li, Zhiwei Ye, Mingyu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The placement of Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs) plays a critical role in modern electronic system design. In contrast to integrated circuit placement, which has been largely automated through advanced algorithms, PCB placement still relies heavily on manual efforts. This is primarily due to the restrictive constraints that devices with vastly different sizes and irregular shapes must be placed within extremely high-density, high-granularity, and limited spaces in the placement process of modern PCBs. Moreover, routing constraints such as the routability of surface-layer traces must be taken into account. These challenges have significantly hindered the advancement of fully automated industrial PCB placement algorithms for many years. To address the challenges, we propose ISPCBPlace, a gradient-based placement method for modern irregular-shaped PCBs under complex design constraints. We formulate the PCB global placement problem as a gradient-based optimization task and propose three key gradient formulation, boundary gradient, overlap gradient (incorporating a centrifugal gradient), and half-perimeter wire-length gradient, to enable effective convergence while satisfying design constraints. Furthermore, we propose a module-aware clustering method to enhance routability of surface-layer traces, and introduce a simulated annealing-based fine-tuning strategy to optimize component orientations and ensure legalization. Experimental results within real-world industrial PCB cases demonstrate that ISPCBPlace outperforms the state-of-the-art methods and even manual designs in terms of wire length, and successfully meeting industrial constraints.

title: Wildfire3D: Three-dimensional Wildfire Scene Reconstruction Using Multiview Aerial Imagery
authors: Maoqin Jia, Xue Rui, Jun Zhang, Weiguo Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Global climate change has increased the frequency of wildfires, making rapid and accurate situational awareness critical for effective firefighting. Although UAV-based imaging enables two-dimensional fire detection, localization, and tracking, the lack of reliable three-dimensional (3D) perception fundamentally limits firefighting precision. Recent advances in real-time 3D reconstruction, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), offer new opportunities for wildfire monitoring; however, their effectiveness depends on reliable Structure-from-Motion (SfM) initialization, which can be challenging in wildfire scenes.Wildfire scenes pose severe challenges to SfM, including repetitive vegetation textures, dynamic flame-induced illumination variations, and frequent occlusions, which jointly cause ambiguous feature correspondences, missing keypoints, and unstable camera pose estimation, ultimately degrading reconstruction quality. In this work, we identify unstable SfM initialization as a key bottleneck that fundamentally limits 3D reconstruction in wildfire environments. To address this issue, we propose Wildfire3D, a wildfire-aware 3D reconstruction framework whose core contribution is a tailored sparse reconstruction strategy that enables robust SfM initialization under severe wildfire-specific visual interference. The proposed framework explicitly incorporates viewpoint-aware image pair selection, complementary feature matching, and spatial consistency–guided optimization to mitigate wildfire-induced SfM failure modes, thereby stabilizing camera pose estimation and reliably supporting downstream 3DGS reconstruction. In addition, we introduce Wildfire3Data, the first open-access multi-view dataset specifically designed for wildfire 3D reconstruction, providing a benchmark for evaluating reconstruction robustness in complex fire scenarios. Extensive experiments on five challenging wildfire scenes demonstrate that Wildfire3D consistently produces stable and accurate 3D reconstructions, outperforming state-of-the-art wild scene reconstruction methods by stabilizing SfM initialization and achieving a 7.3% improvement in PSNR, a 5.5% gain in SSIM, and a 42.8% reduction in LPIPS.

title: Extraction of Shipping Container Defect Dimensions by Video Inferencing via Computer Vision
authors: Yiming Sun, Yihong Wu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: The rapid growth of global trade has intensified the need for reliable and efficient inspection of shipping containers, as surface defects such as dents, rust, and cracks can compromise structural integrity and disrupt logistics operations. Manual inspection remains slow, labor-intensive, and inconsistent, underscoring the demand for automated solutions. This study proposes an end-to-end computer vision pipeline that integrates container detection, surface defect segmentation, and defect size estimation. A YOLOv8n detector was fine-tuned on a selfannotated dataset to accurately identify containers in video streams, while a SegFormer-B2 segmentation model was trained on both synthetic (SeaFront) and real (container-defect) datasets. Results show that although the synthetic dataset achieved strong validation accuracy, its performance degraded significantly in real-world conditions, whereas the real dataset enabled improved segmentation generalization. Post-processing techniques further enhanced mask quality by removing noise and preserving coherent defect regions. Finally, a size estimation module converted binary masks into physical defect areas under a 40 ft container assumption, producing actionable metrics such as defect count, total defective area, and maximum defect size. Experimental evaluation demonstrated that the pipeline achieved high detection accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{mAP} {@} 0.5=0.92)$</tex> and robust segmentation performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mIoU}=0.78$</tex> on real data), validating its effectiveness in realistic port scenarios. The modular design ensures scalability, with potential extensions to multi-class segmentation, calibrated dimension mapping, and integration into smart port infrastructures. This work contributes to bridging the gap between academic advances in computer vision and practical needs in automated logistics inspection.

title: Research on Optimal Operation of a New Power Distribution System Based on Double-Layer Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Jun Ni, Ke Fei, Chao Xu, Peng Hu, Yifang Gao, Ning Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: This paper designs a multi-period bi-level multi-agent reinforcement learning optimization model. Based on traditional optimization devices such as on-load tap changers and capacitor banks, the model can adjust the power flow distribution of the network through the control of distributed power inverters and energy storage, achieving optimal dispatching of active and reactive power in the new distribution system. Considering the characteristics of the hybrid discrete + continuous solution space in the active-reactive power optimization scheduling problem, a Deep Q-network (DQN) agent is established for each discrete device, and a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent is established for continuous devices. The optimization scheduling problem is solved through multi-agent collaboration. The proposed method was validated using the IEEE 33-bus system. The results indicate that the optimization scheduling model based on reinforcement learning proposed in this paper can optimize the system's active and reactive power, mitigate voltage violations, reduce system operating costs, and achieve higher economic benefits.

title: Coordinated Speed Control of Dual Motors for Wire Straightening Mechanism of Overhead Ground Wire Repair Robot
authors: Zhiwei Sun, Cheng Chen, Dongcheng Zhou, Xiang Shen, Wenxu Yan, Wenyuan Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: Aiming at the problem of cooperative speed control of dual motors in the wire stranding mechanism of overhead ground wire repair robots, this paper establishes a dynamic model of two-phase hybrid stepping motors considering unbalanced external load disturbances. A Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Controller is adopted to realize high-precision speed control of the two motors, and the traditional cross-coupling control structure is improved based on fuzzy control. An adaptive synchronization compensator is designed to dynamically compensate the speed error of the two motors, thereby effectively reducing the synchronization error and improving the cooperative control performance and anti-disturbance ability of the system.

title: Zero-Day Attack Detection using Deep Learning + Apache Spark
authors: Neha M Jain, D.V. Ashoka
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: With the rapid evolution of cyber threats, particularly zero-day attacks exploiting unknown vulnerabilities, accurate and interpretable intrusion detection is more critical than ever. This research presents a hybrid framework that combines classical machine learning with explainable AI (XAI) to detect such threats effectively. Using the UNSW-NB15 dataset [3], the system employs a scalable pipeline built on Apache Spark’s PySpark for preprocessing large-scale network logs. Random Forest is used for classification due to its robustness with high-dimensional data, while SHAP [6] provides transparent, model-agnostic interpretability.To enhance detection of structurally complex, unseen attacks, we also explore Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) [5], which learn from graph-based representations of cyber patterns. Stratified sampling ensures a balanced dataset without losing attack diversity. Visualization tools—SHAP plots and an interactive Streamlit dashboard [8]—support analyst-driven insights and real-time inference. The pipeline is designed for scalability and practical deployment, demonstrating high accuracy, strong generalization to zero-day attacks, and enhanced interpretability. This work advances explainable, scalable, and effective cybersecurity solutions for real-world applications.

title: Deep Neural Network–Ant Colony Optimization Hybrid Model for Predictive HRM Analytics in Recruitment and Performance Enhancement
authors: Seema Bhakuni, U. Jawahar Supraveen, Saumya Sharma, Shikha Yadav, Vivekanand Pandey, Tamilarasi Rajamani
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Human Resource Management progressively utilises prediction analytics to enhance hiring quality and employee performance; yet, the complexity and diversity of high-dimensional HR datasets provide hurdles for conventional models, frequently leading to overfitting and decreased interpretability. The proposed study introduces a hybrid model combining Deep Neural Network and Ant Colony Optimisation (DNN–ACO) that simultaneously executes feature selection and hyperparameter optimisation, facilitating precise and resilient recruitment and performance forecasts. ACO assists the DNN in pinpointing the most salient aspects, whereas the deep architecture clarifies complex, nonlinear correlations within recruiting and employee performance data. Experiments utilising recruitment and performance datasets yield exceptional outcomes, with recruitment prediction attaining 96.2% accuracy, 95.5% precision, 96.8% recall, 0.961 F1-score, and 0.984 ROC-AUC, while performance forecasting achieves 94.8% accuracy, 94.1% precision, 95.2% recall, 0.946 F1-score, and 0.934 R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Comparative analyses demonstrate that the hybrid framework surpasses Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM, and Gradient Boosting models in all measures. The suggested method enhances interpretability through feature importance analysis and facilitates ethical HR decision-making, providing effective, high-performance solutions for predictive HR analytics while addressing the shortcomings of traditional approaches.

title: Group Ease: Dynamic Communication Platform for Faculty and Students
authors: Archana Burujwale, Sangita Jaybhaye, Soham Kolhatkar, Prarbdha Isad, Varun Dharwar, Jay Gadre
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Our paper presents a dynamic communication platform designed specifically for educational environments to address the challenges of information overload and disorganized communication between faculty and students. Our proposed system features automatic assignment of students to relevant groups, targeted notifications, and secure role-based login access. The platform incorporates a chatbot for handling routine queries and provides a comprehensive profile section for customized user experiences. Experimental evaluation we made demonstrates significant improvements in information delivery efficiency, with 78% of students reporting better engagement with course material and 82% of faculty noting reduced time spent on repetitive communications. Security tests confirmed robust protection against unauthorized access while maintaining system usability. The results suggest that targeted communication systems can substantially enhance the educational experience by ensuring timely delivery of relevant information.

title: Simulation of Multi-Phase Wireless Power Transformer Using Special Transformer Connections in MATLAB Simulink
authors: B. SomaShekar, Sunil Kumar A V, D. P Somashekar, Jisha L K
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This study examines wireless multiphase power transfer using a simple system running at utility frequency. Significant research has been conducted on wireless power supply to electric motors as a result of the development of Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) technology, especially for applications like articulated arm robots and in-wheel motors. Multiphase WPT systems are required for these systems since they usually use three-phase motors. A static phase transformation system is created to create a multiphase supply from a traditional three-phase grid input. Two approaches are taken into consideration. The Special Transformer Connection technique and the Carrier-Based Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) technique. PWM is more sophisticated even though it has benefits for variable-frequency dual-drive systems. On the other hand, because of its simplicity and dependability, the Special Transformer Connection technique is better suited for applications that need fixed voltage and frequency. With less complexity and more efficiency, this technique successfully transforms a three-phase supply into a five-phase supply. Large capacitors and intricate circuitry are common features of conventional multiphase WPT receivers, which make them hefty. In order to overcome this restriction, the suggested solution uses a special transformer connection method that aims to make the receiver smaller and lighter. With fewer parts, the circuit construction is reduced, allowing for compact and effective multiphase wireless power transfer.

title: Extreme Learning Machine Models for Fault Severity Classification and Anomaly Detection in a Spur Gearbox
authors: Ruben Medina, Mariela Cerrada, René Vinicio Sánchez, Diego Cabrera, Fannia Pacheco
publish: 2025 IEEE Ninth Ecuador Technical Chapters Meeting (ETCM)
abstract: Research on fault severity in gearboxes is a crucial area of study for the successful implementation of predictive maintenance and the optimization of maintenance strategies. In this context, our research compares several extreme learning machines (ELM) for classifying fault severity in two types of faults: pitting and broken teeth. We have included other classical machine learning models in this comparison and also evaluated their capability for anomaly detection by comparing them to classical algorithms such as isolation forest (iForest), one-class SVM (OC-SVM), robust random cut forest (rrcForest), and local outlier factor (LOF). Our study, which utilized nonlinear information entropy (InfoNLEntropy) features and statistical features extracted from vibration signals, demonstrated that ELM models are accurate and highly efficient for classifying fault severity and detecting anomalies in the early stages of fault progress.

title: A Learning based Bidding Strategy for Energy Arbitrage of Battery Energy Storage Systems in Day-Ahead Electricity Market
authors: Hanxi Sun, Yan Xu, Yang Xia, Dunjian Xie, Rui Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Bidding under uncertain electricity prices remains a challenge for battery energy storage systems (BESS). This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning based bidding strategy for battery energy storage systems to arbitrage in day-ahead electricity markets. First of all, a data-driven decision-making framework is designed to maximize the BESS's profit from market participation, which only relies on historical price data without the need for additional forecasting modules. Secondly, a confidence function is developed to evaluate the confidence of the bidding decision, enabling smoother and more adaptive bidding decisions for BESS under market uncertainties. During training, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network is enhanced with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to capture temporal price features, which further improves decision-making. Case studies using three years of Australian market data demonstrate that the proposed method achieves stable convergence, high arbitrage revenue, and safe SoC compliance.

title: A WGAN-GP Based Method for Customer Baseline Load Estimation
authors: Bo Peng, Zhicheng Cai, Xianfu Gong, Yaodong Li, Hui Liao
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Accurate Customer Baseline Load (CBL) estimation is essential for demand response (DR) programs, yet traditional methods like High50f10 and Mid4of6 often fail under high load fluctuations. This paper proposes a Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Networks with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP)-based method to address this limitation. Our model learns the overall load distribution through adversarial training and leverages temporal context around the DR event to reconstruct the baseline, more accurately recovering the actual demand. Evaluated on half-hourly load data from 100 residential customers, the proposed method significantly reduces RMSE and MAE compared to benchmarks. Experiments on aggregated loads of 20 and 50 customers show further error reduction, demonstrating the method's robustness and scalability.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network for Multi-Material Transient Temperature Field Prediction in Dry-Type Transformers
authors: Jiawei Yi, Rui Xu, Wenhu Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Prediction of the transient temperature field in dry-type transformers is crucial for assessing their operational status and ensuring the stability of power systems. Traditional methods, such as finite element analysis, can be computationally intensive and time-consuming and may not be suitable for realtime applications. This paper proposes a novel approach that combines Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) with Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to efficiently predict the transient temperature field in dry-type transformers. The proposed method first employs POD to reduce the dimensionality of the temperature field data. The PINN framework is then utilized to learn the cofficients of the POD basis functions, incorporating the governing physical equations into the training process to ensure physically consistent predictions. The proposed method is validated through numerical simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in accurately predicting the transient temperature field while significantly reducing computation time compared to traditional methods. This approach offers a promising solution for real-time monitoring and assessment of transformer health in power systems.

title: Reversible Data Hiding With Pixel Prediction and Pixel Value Ordering in Industrial Images
authors: Xiaoxi Kong, Wenguang He, Qi Chang, Zhanchuan Cai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Reversible data hiding is essential for data security and integrity in industrial environments. However, existing methods suffer from unstable prediction errors and limited embedding capacity. This article proposes an adaptive reversible data hiding framework for industrial images using enhanced content pixel prediction error. Initially, a refined content pixel selection method is proposed to accurately identify embeddable pixels in industrial images. Then, a distance-based pixel prediction scheme is developed that utilizes high-relevance content pixels for improved prediction accuracy. In addition, a content-adaptive embedding strategy is introduced that prioritizes smooth blocks while employing prediction error pair mapping to simultaneously maximize embedding capacity and minimize distortion. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in embedding capacity, achieving an average of 45 625 bits per image with the capability to embed in complex textured regions. Extensive evaluation on standard benchmarks and two industrial image databases validates its effectiveness for secure industrial data transmission.

title: Event-Triggered Sliding Mode Control for Switched Systems with Reachability-Guaranteed
authors: Yuzhong Wang, Changyun Wen, Xiaolei Li, Hong Sang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: In existing event-triggered sliding mode control (ETSMC) for switched systems, triggering or switching behavior usually causes the system states to deviate from the sliding surface, which may affect both stability and robustness of the system. To deal with this issue, this paper proposes a reachability-guaranteed ETSMC for switched systems without/with communication delay. Firstly, an event-triggered scheme (ETS) is constructed with two error functions integrated to counteract the effects of the triggering and guides the state trajectories onto the sliding surface within finite time. Then, by using time-varying Lyapunov function technology, asymptotic stability of the sliding mode dynamic is established, even in the scenario where all switched modes are unstabilizable. This ensures both reachability and asymptotic stability of the switched system be achieved simultaneously. In addition, Zeno behavior is excluded. Then to deal with the network-induced communication delay and ensure a positive lower bound on the inter-event intervals, the proposed scheme is extended to a periodic event-triggered scenario. Reachability and asymptotic stability can still be guaranteed by using a positive convergent sequence in ETS. Finally, simulation results are presented to verify the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

title: AIRP: Accelerating Multi-tenant Distributed Learning with In-network Resource Pooling
authors: Huifeng Xing, Hao Wang, Yang Chen, Yinfan Hu, Zixuan Chen, Xuandong Liu, Zijian Li, Wanxin Shi, Sen Liu, Yang Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: The increasing popularity of large models and datasets has highlighted the significance of distributed training networks. As gradient synchronization generates substantial traffic, in-network aggregation (INA) has emerged as a solution to offload aggregation onto the switch, alleviating network congestion and accelerating distributed training. However, the limited memory capacity of the INA switch becomes a potential bottleneck as computation shifts into the network, especially in multi-tenant scenarios. To address this bottleneck and enhance network throughput, we propose the Aggregation with Innetwork Resource Pooling (AIRP) framework. Unlike existing approaches that optimize individual switches in a localized manner, AIRP takes a holistic view and efficiently pools switch memory resources across the entire network, allocating them to multiple tenants. Evaluation using the ns-3 simulator and P4 testbed demonstrates that AIRP can accelerate the training of various models, including computer vision and language models. The experimental results show that AIRP outperforms existing INA approaches by up to 7 times in terms of network throughput in multi-tenant scenarios, while also achieving great flexibility and efficiency in deployment.

title: Learning From -Tuple One-vs-All Confidence Comparison Data_supp1-3673280.pdf
authors: Junpeng Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Partial-label learning (PLL) studies classification with ambiguous supervision, where each instance is annotated by a candidate label set that contains the unknown true label. While PLL has been widely investigated, real-world annotation pipelines often provide richer structure than an unordered set: annotators or retrieval systems may implicitly rank candidates, yielding a preferred label that is more likely to be correct, and the mis-selection patterns can be class-dependent rather than uniform. Moreover, practical candidate sets may occasionally miss the true label due to human error or imperfect upstream models. Motivated by these observations, we propose a new weak-supervision paradigm, preferred-label PLL in a one-vs-all view (PLL-OVA), where each training instance is accompanied by a candidate set and a preferred label within the set. We model preferred-label generation through a flexible noisy-channel formulation that subsumes both symmetric and asymmetric mis-selection mechanisms, and we further incorporate the missing-true-label scenario to better reflect realistic annotation noise. Building on this formulation, we develop principled empirical risk minimization (ERM) procedures via risk rewriting, establish identifiability requirements under general channels, and derive practical learning objectives that remain statistically grounded. To improve optimization stability under negative-risk effects inherent to unbiased rewriting, we introduce risk-correction functions (e.g., rectified linear unit (ReLU)/absolute value correction function (ABS)-type corrections) that significantly enhance robustness and empirical performance. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that PLL-OVA consistently improves upon standard PLL baselines, especially when candidate ambiguity is large or mis-selection is nonuniform, validating the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed framework.
