title: JBOC: Just a Bunch of CXL-enabled SSDs for Resource-Efficient LLM Checkpointing
authors: Hakbeom Jang, Younghoon Min, Dongha Yoon, Hane Yie, Hoshik Kim, Jongryool Kim
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: The rapid growth of Large Language Models (LLMs) has pushed training systems to rely on memory sharding and offloading, introducing substantial data movement overhead. This is further exacerbated by frequent checkpointing, where training states must traverse multiple software and hardware layers. Such a process creates a critical performance bottleneck, as traditional host-centric hierarchies struggle to handle the massive data movement required for high-frequency, faulttolerant checkpointing. In this paper, we introduce JBOC, an in-device processing architecture leveraging CXL-enabled SSDs that fuses optimizer updates and checkpointing into a single, unified operation. By offloading massive footprint optimizer updates and executing these fused tasks directly within internal computational units, JBOC achieves highly efficient checkpointing as persistence is handled during the update pipeline. This offloaded approach allows training states to remain within deviceside resources, bypassing the host-side resource hierarchy and effectively isolating the host from heavy storage traffic. We validate JBOC on commercial CXL platforms from Marvell and SK Hynix. Our evaluation achieves up to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.40\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> speedup under per-iteration checkpointing. Compared to ZeRO-Offload, JBOC delivers comparable throughput while checkpointing <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> more frequently. Furthermore, JBOC outperforms both ZeROOffload and ZeRO-Infinity in memory-constrained scenarios, demonstrating its robustness when host resources are limited.

title: A Novel UWB/INS Tight Integration Augmented by an RTK-Dependent Linear UWB Offset Calibration Model and the Denoised Barometer Data
authors: Hanyu Wen, Zhouzheng Gao, Jie Lv, Yanli Zheng, Cheng Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Currently, the Ultra-WideBand (UWB) is treated as a potential precise positioning method in Internet of Things (IoT) applications because of its high accuracy and low latency characteristics in theory. However, in complex environments, the quality of UWB measurements would be polluted significantly by the Uncalibrated Ranging Offset (URO) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) error, which degrades UWB positioning accuracy and reliability especially in the vertical direction. To overcome the impacts of these factors, a tight integration of the UWB and an Inertial Navigation System (INS), which is augmented by a GNSS Real-time Kinematic (RTK)-dependent UWB ranging calibration model and the denoised-barometer data, is proposed in this paper. In such a method, UWB URO is calculated and compensated based on the raw UWB measurements and the geometric distances computed from the high-accuracy RTK/INS-derived coordinates. Then, the denoised barometer data is utilized to form a constraint function for the vertical position, which can limit the position variation caused by the abnormal geometric structure in the vertical direction. All UWB data, INS data, URO data, and barometer data are integrated by an IGG-III-based robust Kalman filter, which can significantly improve positioning accuracy and stability. The experimental results showed that the positioning accuracies of such a novel UWB/INS tight integration in the vertical and horizontal directions are respectively 0.28 m and 0.08 m, providing more than 90% improvement percentages compared to that of the conventional UWB/INS tight integration.

title: Multigroup Energy Representation for Fast Monte Carlo Estimation of Radioisotope Yields from Electron-Beam Spectra
authors: Bruno Silveira Nunes, Nilson Dias Vieira Junior, Alexandre Bonatto, Ricardo Elgul Samad
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science
abstract: Monte Carlo simulations are widely used to estimate radioisotope production through nuclear reactions. However, achieving high accuracy typically requires simulating more than 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8</sup> particles, which is computationally demanding, particularly when many simulations are required, such as in optimization loops. In a previous study, we demonstrated that molybdenum-99 yields from bremsstrahlung-driven photonuclear reactions induced by laser accelerated electron beams can be estimated up to four orders of magnitude faster by employing a multigroup energy representation, in which a limited set of Monte Carlo precomputed group yields is used to reconstruct the total yield for an arbitrary source spectra. The present work expands this approach by showing that the multigroup representation also provides a deeper understanding of the relative contribution of different electron energies to the final isotope yield. Applying the method to 640 different spectra revealed how the choice of group structure—such as the number of groups and their energy widths—affects both accuracy and efficiency. The results show that the proposed approach reproduces full Monte Carlo yield estimates with median errors below 5%, reaching values as low as 0.3%.

title: Semi-Supervised Open-Set Through-Wall User Identification via MIMO Radar Respiratory Signals
authors: Kai Yang, Chong Han, Yichen Xia, Dongsheng Zhu, Jian Guo, Lijuan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: With the rapid evolution of 6G communications and the Internet of Things (IoT), wireless sensing–based identity recognition is emerging as a key enabler for intelligent security and smart environments. However, existing popular mmWave radar–based methods often suffer from severe signal attenuation and unreliable sensing performance in non-line-of-sight (NLOS) environments. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes Resp-TWOSID, a through-wall semi-supervised open-set respiration-based identification framework leveraging low-frequency MIMO radar. By utilizing low-frequency signals, Resp-TWOSID reliably captures fine-grained respiratory patterns through thick masonry walls up to 26 cm, enabling robust user identification. Additionally, conventional identification methods typically rely on fully supervised learning, which demands extensive data collection and labeling, and cannot recognize unknown users during deployment. To address these challenges, Resp-TWOSID introduces the dynamic open-set semi-supervised learning with self-supervised consistency (DOS3L-SSC) algorithm. DOS3L-SSC explicitly models the unknown category as the (C+1)-th class and employs a dynamic first-in-first-out (FIFO) queue to select reliable unknown samples for sustained open-set learning continually. It further enforces joint open-set and closed-set consistency learning to stabilize predictions under data perturbations and introduces a self-supervised feature-consistency loss to enhance representation robustness while efficiently exploiting hard unlabeled samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Resp-TWOSID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving superior accuracy for known-user identification and high-confidence rejection of unknown users. These results highlight its strong potential for deployment in security-critical and privacy-sensitive 6G sensing environments.

title: Sleep Stage Classification Using a Non-contact Capacitive ECG Mattress with Machine Learning Approaches: A Feasible Study
authors: Rui Cui, Jiayi Li, Kaichen Wang, Hongyu Chen, Wei Chen, Chen Chen, Jingchun Luo
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Sleep stage classification is essential for diagnosing sleep disorders. However, the clinical reliance on polysomnography (PSG) faces significant challenges due to its high cost, invasiveness, and limited accessibility. These constraints have driven the growing interest for more cost-effective, user-friendly, and long-term sleep monitoring solutions that can be seamlessly integrated into home environments. This study investigates the feasibility of a novel non-contact capacitive ECG (cECG) mattress system as a unobtrusive alternative for sleep staging. This feasibility study was conducted on 13 young healthy participants (mean age: 25.69 years), with two overnight recordings per subject. Synchronized sleep recordings using both clinical PSG and the proposed cECG system were collected. Heart rate variability (HRV) features were then extracted across time, frequency, and nonlinear domains for further analysis. These features were then utilized to train and compare multiple machine learning models. Accuracy of 90.3% is achieved in two-class staging (wake versus sleep), 78.8% in three-class staging (wake versus NREM sleep versus REM sleep), 64.2% in four-class staging (wake versus light sleep versus deep sleep versus REM sleep), and 59.9% in five-class staging (wake versus N1 versus N2 versus N3 versus REM). Our method is seen to be effective in macro-sleep staging with the non-contact cECG system, particularly in two-, three-, and four-class sleep staging. The machine learning models using features from PSG and unobtrusive cECG demonstrated comparable classification performance, indicating that the cECG mattress system captures sufficient physiological information to support accurate sleep stage classification. This study demonstrates the integration of non-contact capacitive electrocardiogram sensing technology with HRV-driven machine learning models, enabling comfortable, long-term, and relatively accurate home sleep monitoring. This approach offers a viable supplement to traditional sleep monitoring methods.

title: Dead Zone Bilateral Control for High Performance Robotic Teleoperation_supp1-3675931.mp4
authors: Rohan Kota
publish: N/A
abstract: Autonomous robot policies are commonly trained using demonstration data acquired via robotic teleoperation, a process which can be time-intensive and physically demanding for human operators. Bilateral control can speed up robotic teleoperation by allowing the operator to feel the forces experienced by the remote manipulator, but it can also transmit undesirable forces, such as friction and damping, back to the operator. Here, we present dead zone bilateral control, an improvement to the conventional position-position bilateral control scheme that prevents the transmission of unwanted forces during free-space motion. We implement our controller on a custom 2-degree-of-freedom teleoperation device and show that it reduces the energy required for free-space motion by approximately 50%. During a user study in which 16 participants performed a peg rolling task, our controller reduced completion times by an average of 25% compared to the standard position-position bilateral controller. The results suggest that dead zone bilateral control can expedite the collection of teleoperated task demonstrations, allowing researchers to gather larger datasets for training autonomous robot policies.

title: Latency-Constrained Resource Synergization for Mission-Oriented 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Yueshan Lin, Wei Feng, Yunfei Chen, Yongxu Zhu, Ning Ge, Shi Jin
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper investigates latency-constrained resource synergization for mission-oriented non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) in post-disaster emergency scenarios. When terrestrial infrastructures are damaged, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with edge information hubs (EIHs) are deployed to provide temporary coverage and synergize communication and computing resources for rapid situation awareness. We formulate a joint resource configuration and location optimization problem to minimize overall resource costs while guaranteeing stringent latency requirements. Through analytical derivations, we obtain closed-form optimal solutions that reveal the fundamental tradeoff between communication and computing resources, and develop a successive convex approximation method for EIH location optimization. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves approximately 20% cost reduction compared with benchmark approaches, validating its optimality and effectiveness for mission-critical emergency response applications in the sixth-generation (6G) era.

title: Hackathons that Work: Driving Engagement in Corporate Innovation Events
authors: Darja Smite, Nils Brede Moe, Rasmus Ulfsnes, Viktoria Stray, Leif Eriks Opland, Anastasiia Tkalich
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: Internal hackathons are widely used to drive innovation and collaboration in software companies, yet not all succeed. Many fall short due to low employee engagement. Why do some employees dive in, while others hold back? This article explores what drives and hinders engagement based on insights from five companies practicing regular hackathons. Using Social-Cognitive Theory, we show that engagement depends on two key beliefs: one’s ability to contribute (self-efficacy) and the value of participation (outcome expectations). These drivers are not arbitrary; they are predictable, and thus manageable. By understanding the psychology behind engagement, organizers can create hackathons that help more employees to feel confident and inspired to participate. We offer practical guidance to make your next hackathon truly engaging.

title: Real-Time Multimodal Sea-Ice Mapping through Fixed-lag Smoothing and LiDAR–Camera Fusion
authors: Oskar G. Veggeland, Ekaterina Kim, Roger Skjetne
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study presents an open-source pipeline for multimodal sea ice mapping in real time, enabling persistent environmental monitoring from vessels operating in the Arctic. The method builds on a self-contained instrumentation system that integrates an IMU and a GNSS receiver to estimate the platform trajectory using a factor-graph framework based on fixed-lag incremental smoothing. In addition to inertial measurements and GNSS corrections, the estimator applies a custom process factor to constrain the system’s vertical dynamics, derived from the vessel’s rigid-body kinematics. The estimated trajectory is used to construct spatially consistent 3D point clouds from raw LiDAR scans, augmented with LiDAR intensity and RGB color from an optical camera, providing a rich near-field representation of the surrounding sea ice environment. The resulting maps can support situational awareness for safe operations in ice and enable long-term environmental monitoring by quantifying sea ice structure over time. The full framework is released as an open-source C++ library on GitHub, and the complete dataset used in this study is made available through IEEE DataPort to support further work on shipborne sea ice sensing.

title: GPAQuant: Accelerating Personalized Recommendation with Group-Wise and PIM-Aware Quantization
authors: Sitian Chen, Amelie Chi Zhou, Yusen Li, Min Si, Pavan Balaji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Deep Learning Recommendation Models (DLRMs) have gained popularity in recommendation systems due to their effectiveness in handling large-scale recommendation tasks. However, the embedding layers of DLRMs have emerged as a significant performance bottleneck due to their high demands on memory capacity and bandwidth. Existing solutions, such as offloading to CPU memory, are hampered by communication overheads, while many proposed Processing-In-Memory (PIM) architectures are not yet commercially viable, limiting their practical impact. A promising approach, demonstrated by recent work like UpDLRM, is to accelerate these layers using commercial Processing-In-Memory (PIM) hardware. However, this approach does not address the challenge of ever-growing model sizes in industrial-scale systems. To tackle both memory bandwidth and capacity limitations, this paper proposes GPAQuant, a framework that co-designs PIM-based acceleration with model quantization. GPAQuant strategically stores quantized embedding tables (EMTs) in distributed DPU memory and offloads both the memory-intensive lookups and reductions to the DPUs. To address DPU-specific challenges, we propose four key innovations: (1) architecture-aware EMT partitioning that optimizes data placement to minimize inter-DPU communication, (2) frequency-aware workload balancing to evenly distribute access patterns across DPUs, (3) fine-grained EMT rearrangement that reduces quantization errors while preserving spatial locality, and (4) group-wise quantization to balance dequantization overhead and model accuracy. Extensive evaluations across six datasets and four DLRM architectures demonstrate GPAQuant’s superiority: it achieves 4.6× speedup on embedding layers and 3.4× end-to-end inference acceleration compared to state-of-the-art CPU-GPU implementations and PIM baselines, with negligible accuracy loss.

title: Best Practice for Integrating InSAR and GNSS Observations in High-Density GNSS Networks
authors: Eugenia Giorgini, Milan Lazecky, Andrew Hooper, Stefano Gandolfi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar Interferometry (InSAR) is widely used to monitor ground deformation, but its results are relative to a local satellite-based reference system. To enable consistent geodetic interpretation, InSAR velocities must be aligned to a terrestrial reference frame, typically using Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) data. This study aims to identify the most effective referencing strategy to align ascending and descending InSAR datasets to the European Terrestrial Reference Frame (ETRF), using a dense GNSS network in a subsidence-affected area of northern Italy. We tested several referencing approaches by varying: (i) the number and configuration of GNSS stations, (ii) the density of Persistent Scatterers (PS) used to calculate local InSAR velocities, and (iii) the modeling of InSAR-GNSS differences, using either a bias, a plane, or a second-order polynomial surface. Results show that the optimal referencing is achieved by modeling the differences with a planar surface, using all available GNSS stations, and computing InSAR velocities from PS within 100 m of each station. This approach minimized post-referencing residuals more effectively than other methods, including polynomial fitting. Following referencing, InSAR data from both geometries are merged to extract the vertical (Up) and horizontal (East) components of motion, demonstrating alignment with the GNSS-based reference system. These findings highlight how referencing performance depends on both the modeling strategy and GNSS-PS configuration, providing guidance for improving InSAR accuracy in geodetic applications.

title: CUMA Uplink for Interference-Resilient Multi-Cell Networks
authors: Chenguang Rao, Kai-Kit Wong, Yangyang Zhang, Chan-Byoung Chae
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter investigates uplink reception in a multi-cell network with severe inter-cell interference, where each base station (BS) has access only to local channel state information (CSI) of its intra-cell user equipments (UEs). We propose to use the compact ultra-massive antenna array (CUMA) architecture enabled by the fluid antenna system (FAS) that achieves strong interference suppression with significantly reduced hardware and computational complexity compared with conventional linear receivers. By activating a large number of FAS ports favouring the desired signal and coherently combining their outputs, intercell interference (ICI) can be averaged out through port-domain superposition. In the proposed approach, the number of radio-frequency (RF) chains at each BS equals the number of served UEs within the cell, without requiring additional active electronic components. Moreover, we introduce a low-complexity weighted superposition strategy, implemented solely with attenuators, to further enhance the network rate performance while maintaining low hardware overhead. Simulation results under conventional hexagonal cellular layout and the stochastic-geometric model both illustrate that the proposed CUMA uplink yields substantial performance gains over conventional linear receiver schemes.

title: Security and Verifiability in Federated Learning: A Zero-Knowledge Reputation-Based Blockchain Framework
authors: Swetha Ghanta, Ashok Kumar Pradhan, Prasanthi Boyapati, Sujit Biswas, Saraju P Mohanty
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative training without centralizing sensitive data but faces challenges, including client authenticity, verifiable training participation, and secure aggregation. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel framework, Zero-Knowledge Reputation-aware Blockchain Federated Learning (ZK-RBFL), which integrates blockchain, FL, Homomorphic Encryption (HE), and zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP). In the proposed ZK-RBFL framework, initially the clients undergo lightweight token-based authentication and then generate ZKP to provide cryptographic evidence of honest local training participation and reported inference accuracy before contributing their model updates. The model updates are encrypted using the CKKS HE mechanism to prevent any potential model inversion attacks. These encrypted model updates are stored on IPFS, with their corresponding CIDs recorded on the blockchain to ensure immutability. Further, ZK-RBFL enables mutual client verification of ZKPs to reduce server bottlenecks and enhance accountability. To ensure fairness and robustness in a distributed environment, we introduce a democratic blockchain consensus mechanism named Proof of Reputation-Weighted Voting (PoRWV) for block acceptance. Once consensus is reached, the encrypted model updates are aggregated using reputation-weighted averaging. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ZK-RBFL for brain tumor classification using a ZKP-compatible LeNet model for proof generation. Despite model simplicity, the global model achieves 94.22% accuracy. In addition, experiments with malicious clients and formal Scyther security analysis demonstrate that ZK-RBFL ensures both security and performance.

title: Machine Learning–Based Approach for Parkinson’s disease Detection
authors: B. Ramu, K. Naresh Babu, T.V. Chandra Sekhar, A. Naresh, P Chandra Sekhar Reddy
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD), a neurological condition that impairs motor skills, is brought on by the death of dopamine-secreting neurons in the brain. Timely detection and treatment of Parkinson's disease (PD) is essential for effective treatment and controlled disease. This study focuses on the detection of PD using machine learning (ML) algorithms by examining biomedical voice measurements alongside other available data. Several models including Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest (RF), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Neural Networks are tested for their accuracy on the classification task. It is shown that machine learning algorithms are efficacious in classifying patients suffering from PD and those not suffering from it, with SVM outperforming the rest. This work demonstrates the value of applying ML Physics based algorithms for the detection of PD, offering a simple and non-invasive method of diagnosing the disease.

title: Deep Learning-Based Rice Leaf Disease Detection Using Mobilenetv2 For Agricultural Health Monitoring
authors: Deepa Bhadana, Thanjaivadivel M, R. Lakshmana Kumar, T. Kalaikumaran, Thinagaran Perumal, Rajermani Thinakaran
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Rice is a vital crop to millions, chiefly in low-income economies where it is an significant main food for safeguarding food security. Conventional manual diagnosis events are labour-intensive, need authorities, and are not scalable, particularly in the countryside. The proposed task is to fold a rice leaf disease dataset and shape a classification model based on MobileNetV2 to classify efficiently if a disease exists or not. The methodology starts with the gaining of Rice lease diseases Dataset and preprocessing via data augmentation, normalization, and resizing for image training for model training. Feature extraction is existence performed by the Vision Transformer (ViT) model in order to extract local and global image features using self-attention mechanisms. These extracted features are then classified with the MobileNetV2 in this hybrid structure, which upholds a stability between accuracy and cost of computation. The model has experienced further generalization, with an accuracy of 96.8%, precision of 97.1%, recall of 96.5%, F1-score of 96.8%, and ROC AUC of 97.3%, thus exceptional the baseline models. This hybrid model greatly improves the accuracy and speed of rice leaf disease classification, thus if a good tool for helping in early disease recognition and crop group.

title: Rice Leaf Diseases Detection Based on Images Using Machine Learning
authors: Thrimurthulu Vobbilineni, CH U V R S Pradyumna, Reeshma Gidugu, Priyanka Gajapaga, ShivaSai Eppanapelli
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Rice is a global staple crop, but a number of leaf diseases make it difficult for farmers to grow and can significantly reduce both yield and quality. Manual inspection is one of the difficult, time-consuming, and prone to mistakes traditional detection methods. To overcome these issues, our project aims to develop a machine learning-based system that can automatically identify rice leaf diseases from images. The major objective is to create an effective machine learning tool that can quickly and accurately identify diseases from leaf pictures, enabling rapid response and reducing crop losses. The proposed method evaluates the model's accuracy and precision and its efficiency in image classification tasks using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and other classification methods, such the Random-forest classification algorithm. As part of the methodology, a significant amount of images of rice leaves are gathered, pre-processed, and used to train a model. The model's performance will be evaluated using metrics like accuracy and precision, and changes will be made as needed. When the machine learning tool is finished, farmers will be able to upload images and receive immediate diagnosis of diseases due to a simple interface. The expected outcome is a robust machine learning model that can accurately detect diseases of rice leaves. By being integrated into agricultural management systems or utilized as a mobile application, this technology can enhance crop health monitoring and improve global food security.

title: Context-Driven Performance Modeling for Causal Inference Operators on Neural Processing Units
authors: Neelesh Gupta, Rakshith Jayanth, Dhruv Parikh, Viktor Prasanna
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: The proliferation of large language models has driven demand for long-context inference on resourceconstrained edge platforms. However, deploying these models on Neural Processing Units (NPUs) presents significant challenges due to architectural mismatch: the quadratic complexity of standard attention conflicts with NPU memory and compute patterns. This paper presents a comprehensive performance analysis of causal inference operators on a modern NPU, benchmarking quadratic attention against sub-quadratic alternatives including structured state-space models and causal convolutions. Our analysis reveals a spectrum of critical bottlenecks: quadratic attention becomes severely memory-bound with catastrophic cache inefficiency, while sub-quadratic variants span from computebound on programmable vector cores to memory-bound by data movement. These findings provide essential insights for codesigning hardware-aware models and optimization strategies to enable efficient long-context inference on edge platforms.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Approaches for Early Prediction of Liver Cirrhosis: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Vindhya G B, Shanthala C P
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Early detection of liver cirrhosis remains a paramount challenge in contemporary hepatology, where prompt intervention can substantially modify disease progression and patient prognosis. This comprehensive survey examines state-of-the-art deep learning methodologies for early cirrhosis detection, emphasizing machine learning applications in medical imaging, clinical data analysis, and multi-modal approaches that have emerged from 2021 to 2024. We systematically evaluate current artificial intelligence architectures, performance metrics, and clinical applications through analysis of recent peer-reviewed literature. Our investigation reveals that deep learning approaches achieve diagnostic accuracies between 85% and 96% for liver fibrosis staging while providing non-invasive alternatives to conventional biopsy procedures. Artificial intelligence methodologies enable comprehensive analysis of vast datasets that would be impractical for manual quantification, particularly for unstructured visual and textual data containing valuable disease insights. This survey provides a comprehensive resource for researchers and clinicians developing AI-driven hepatic disease detection solutions, highlighting recent achievements and remaining challenges in clinical translation. The analysis demonstrates that multi-modal integration approaches show superior performance compared to single-modality systems, with attention-based architectures providing enhanced interpretability for clinical adoption.

title: Self-Healing Data Systems: AI-Augmented Root Cause Analysis in Modern Warehouses
authors: Mahendran Chinnaiah, Sashi Kiran Kaata, Rishiraj Kohli
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Contemporary data warehouses are becoming more technical, distributed and more mission critical, but they are also becoming more susceptible to outage and degraded performance due to implicit anomalies. Conventional methods of monitoring and troubleshooting are usually based on manual analysis and rule-based notifications that do not scale in dynamic settings. This paper gives Self-Healing Data Systems that are enabled by AI-enhanced root cause analysis to automatically identify, diagnose, and resolve errors in data warehouse ecosystems. The framework proposed combines anomaly detection, causal inference, and reinforcement learning to observe at scale the large pipelines and to localize the source of faults with extreme precision and activate automatic recovery steps. The system rapidly reduces mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to repair (MTTR) by applying log mining, dependency graph modelling and predictive analytics to ensure continuity in service. Modern cloud-based data warehouse workload evaluation shows that experimental evaluation can substantially increase accuracy of the root cause localization as well as reducing the recovery time up to 45 percent versus the baseline workload approaches. Based on these findings, self-healing with AI is a promising foundational capability to build resilient, adaptive, and intelligent data management infrastructures.

title: A Reducible-Loss-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Automatic Generation Control in Renewable-Integrated Power Systems
authors: Shanke Mou, Nan Yang, Hao Chen, Zhendong Wan, Jinghui Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The large-scale integration of renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar power, introduces strong randomness and intermittency into modern power systems, leading to deteriorated frequency stability and degraded control performance in automatic generation control (AGC). To address these challenges, this paper proposes a reducible-loss-based deep reinforcement learning (RL) approach for AGC in renewable-integrated power systems. The proposed method improves the training efficiency and stability of the traditional RL framework by incorporating a reducible-loss experience replay strategy. This mechanism evaluates the learnability of training samples by calculating the difference between current and target network losses, enabling the algorithm to prioritize samples with higher learning potential while reducing the influence of noisy or low-quality samples. A two-area load frequency control model and a multi-area renewable-integrated AGC system are established to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method under step, random, and white-noise load disturbances. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves faster convergence, smaller frequency deviations, and improved control performance compared with conventional reinforcement learning algorithms, showing strong robustness against renewable fluctuations and stochastic disturbances.

title: Priority-Aware Data Dissemination in VANETs Using Exponential Narwhal Optimization
authors: Sumit Kumar Sinha, Chinmoy Ghorai
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Data dissemination is considered the prominent application in the vehicular network. Prevailing data dissemination methods are usually based on random-access protocols, which mostly cause latency and low throughput. Thus, an innovation hybrid optimization-based approach called Exponential Narwhal Optimization (ExNO) is established for enhancing the data dissemination in Vehicular Ad-Hoc NETwork (VANET). Primarily, a topology for data dissemination in VANET is considered. Further, input messages, including categories, as safety messages, entertainment, traffic updates, general information, and emergency alerts, are considered. Thereafter, a priority score is calculated for each message, and the messages are placed in a priority queue, and the elements are arranged based on the priority through an index. The index block contains the data categories and the corresponding links to the messages placed in the queue. After that, data dissemination is performed in three phases: like route reply phase, the data transmission, and the route discovery phase. Here, the optimal path for data transmission is generated using the proposed ExNO model by considering the various multi-objective routing parameters. Additionally, the presented ExNO approach attained the lowest value of end-to-end delay of 0.538 sec, and the highest value of Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR), throughput of 97.666%, and 97.988 Mbps, respectively.

title: Research on the Grid-Forming Control Strategy for the PCS of Energy Storage System
authors: Zikai Fan, Fei Wang, Sheng Hu, Yifei Fan, Xuesong Jiang, Xiaobo Liu, Jinrui Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Traditional grid-following control is usually used for energy storage system in which phase-lock-loop for grid voltage is necessary, therefore, its control performance highly relies on the phase angle of grid voltage. In order to overcome the problems caused by grid-following control, this paper studied the grid-forming control strategy for energy storage system. Compare to traditional grid-following control with phase-lock-loop(PLL), grid-forming control strategy has the ability of regulating voltage and frequency independently by simulating synchronous generator, furthermore, PLL is not required in such control, therefore, grid-forming control strategy can improve the capability of active and reactive power regulation greatly, especially in weak power grid. In order to validate the effectiveness of grid-forming control strategy, a grid-forming energy storage system was constructed with Matlab-Simulink, the simulation results shows that the grid-forming control strategy can regulate the reactive power of energy storage system into power grid independently, and therefore the capability of regulating active and reactive power is greatly improved.

title: Effects of Thermal Aging on Insulation Performance and Service Life of XLPE Submarine Cables in Hybrid Cable Systems
authors: Shoushui Han, Liang Zou, Zheng Liu, Rongzhao Jia, Zhiyun Han, Qingsong Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This study investigates thermal aging issues in three-core XLPE submarine cables operating under nonstandard conditions (single-core operation) within hybrid cable systems. Through electromagnetic transient simulation and finite element analysis, it was found that this operating mode elevates local cable temperatures from the conventional 89 °C to 94 °C, accelerating thermal aging of the XLPE insulation material. Combined with thermal aging experiments, the impact of abnormal temperature rise on the dielectric properties and electrical conductivity of the insulation was analyzed, and the cable service life was estimated accordingly. Results indicate that under this non-standard operating mode, the service life of the submarine cable decreases by approximately 2.3 years compared to conventional operating conditions. This study provides theoretical basis and data support for optimizing the operation and aging monitoring maintenance of hybrid submarine cable systems.

title: ABPF-ESPRIT Based Approach for Identification of Broadband Oscillation and Harmonic Risks in Novel Power System
authors: Chuang Liu, Yang Zhang, Cong Sun, Heling Yang, Rakhmonov Ikromjon Usmonovich
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The integration of high proportions of renewable energy has led to wide-band oscillations and harmonics caused by power electronic devices, which pose a significant threat to power system stability due to their broad frequency range and fast propagation. Traditional detection methods suffer from limitations such as insufficient frequency resolution and poor dynamic adaptability in wide-band oscillation scenarios. This paper proposes a novel method for identifying wide-band harmonic oscillation risks based on an adaptive band-pass filter and the estimation of signal parameters via rotational invariance techniques. The center frequency and bandwidth of the ABPF are configured based on Fast Fourier Transform to achieve preliminary extraction and separation of signals in various frequency bands. Then, ESPRIT is used for parameter identification to obtain key modal parameters such as frequency, amplitude, and damping factor. Finally, real-time oscillation risk assessment and harmonic over-limit detection are achieved through dynamic secondary data storage. The method is validated using self-constructed signals, wind power sub-synchronous oscillation cases, and photovoltaic wide-band resonance cases built in MATLAB/Simulink. Results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively identifies wide-band oscillation and harmonic risks, providing theoretical and technical support for online monitoring and active defense in power-electricized system.

title: Research on Core Technology and Field Application of Electric Power Generation of Quadruped Robot (Robot Dog)
authors: Fangbo Xing, Chuanwen Sun, Wenxin Feng, Jie Zhang, Hao Liu, Wei Wang, Ruiliang Zhang, Daoyu Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the deep integration of artificial intelligence, mechanical engineering, and sensor technology, quadruped robots (robot dogs) bionic machines renowned for their high mobility and environmental adaptability have found diverse practical applications. This paper analyzes core technologies in robot dog systems, focusing on three key areas: motion control, environmental perception, and autonomous decision-making algorithms. To address the unique challenges of power generation environments, we propose a novel integrated framework that customizes these technologies for electromagnetic interference, high-voltage risks, and dynamic terrains, incorporating adaptive Model Predictive Control (MPC) enhancements and Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)-based sensor fusion tailored for substations. It further explores typical application scenarios in power generation facilities, including intelligent substation equipment inspection, operational risk management, and emergency response support.

title: Distributed Voltage Control in Distribution Networks Based on Error Feedback
authors: Hongbing Wang, Yin Zhang, Haoran Guo, Tangwei Xiao, Xinbo Huo, Yuan Gao
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Aiming at the challenge of balancing convergence speed and solution accuracy in distributed voltage control of distribution networks using momentum extrapolation, this paper proposes an adjustment mechanism based on objective function error feedback. First, building upon initial parameter settings, dual judgment criteria of "objective function fluctuation" and "objective function error" are defined. When these criteria are met, the adjustment mechanism for the extrapolation term coefficient is triggered. Using the minimization of power loss in an IEEE 33-nodes distribution network system under a 30% packet loss rate as the experimental scenario, the effectiveness of the mechanism is verified through comparative experiments with fixedcoefficient schemes. Experimental results show that the proposed error feedback adjustment mechanism improves the algorithm's convergence speed to some extent and reduces the objective function deviation from 0.1 in the fixed-coefficient scheme to below 10-3, achieving a synergistic optimization of rapid convergence and high-precision approximation to the ideal value. This mechanism provides an efficient implementation path for distributed voltage control in distribution networks under packet loss scenarios, particularly suitable for engineering applications such as distribution network voltage control and energy system optimization, where convergence speed and solution quality are critical.

title: Optimization Research on Load-Shedding in the Security and Stability Control Systems Using Branch and Bound Method and Node Importance
authors: Peng Cao, Junshuai Quan, Haofeng Zheng, Bo Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The Security and Stability Control System (SSCS) is critical for ensuring power system integrity. Under external disturbances, Security and Stability Control Equipment (SSCE) deployed at Execution Stations (ESs) act to restore stability, primarily through load shedding. However, conventional Enumeration Methods (EMs) for determining load shedding schemes are computationally intensive, failing to meet the rapid precision requirements. While heuristic algorithms offer faster execution, they often lack sufficient accuracy. To bridge this gap and meet the SSCS's real-time operational demands, this paper proposes a novel load shedding strategy based on node importance assessment. This strategy orchestrates the load shedding sequence, formulates an optimization model, and employs the Branch and Bound Method (BBM) to efficiently solve the relaxed integer programming problem, substantially reducing the search space and accelerating the identification of optimal solutions. Node importance is evaluated comprehensively by integrating analyses from both power level and communication level perspectives. Optimal load shedding strategies are then selected from the feasible solution set based on this importance ranking. Validation on the IEEE 30-node system demonstrates that the proposed node importance-based strategy significantly enhances the computational speed of SSCS control strategies and reliably identifies optimal solutions.

title: Low-Field Ferroelectricity in 10 nm AlBScN Thin Films
authors: Xiaolei Tong, Pedram Yousefian, Ziyi Wang, Meenakshi A. Saravanan, Rajeev Kumar Rai, Giovanni Esteves, Eric A. Stach, Roy H. Olsson
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Ferroelectric aluminum scandium nitride (Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>-<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>Sc<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>N, AlScN) offers CMOS-compatible integration but suffers from high coercive fields and leakage currents that hinder thickness scaling. Further reduction in thickness is essential for low-voltage embedded nonvolatile memory applications. Boron incorporation into AlScN (AlBScN) suppresses leakage current in films down to 40 nm, yet its ferroelectric characteristics in ultrathin films remain unexplored. This letter demonstrates robust ferroelectric switching in 10 nm sputtered AlBScN capacitors with a low coercive field and approximately two orders of magnitude lower leakage than AlScN. Notably, The coercive field (E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub>) extracted from capacitance-voltage measurements was 2.3 MV/cm, and the onset of symmetric polarization reversal was observed near 4.6 MV/cm in positive-up-negative-down (PUND) measurements using 2 μs pulses. Moreover, the breakdown field (E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BD</sub>) was determined to be 10.0 MV/cm for 10 μm diameter capacitors, suggesting a substantial margin between E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BD</sub> and E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> for reliable operation. These findings demonstrate AlBScN as a promising candidate for CMOS back-end-of-line (BEOL) compatible ferroelectric applications with improved energy consumption and reduced leakage current.

title: Semantic Chain-of-Trust: Autonomous Trust Orchestration for Collaborator Selection via Hypergraph-Aided Agentic AI
authors: Botao Zhu, Xianbin Wang, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: The effective completion of tasks in collaborative systems hinges on task-specific trust evaluations of potential devices for distributed collaboration. Due to independent operation of devices involved, dynamic evolution of their mutual relationships, and complex situation-related impact on trust evaluation, effectively assessing devices' trust for collaborator selection is challenging. To overcome this challenge, we propose a semantic chain-of-trust model implemented with agentic AI and hypergraphs for achieving effective collaborator selection. We first introduce a concept of semantic trust, specifically designed to assess collaborators along multiple semantic dimensions for a more accurate representation of their trustworthiness. To facilitate intelligent evaluation, an agentic AI system is deployed on each device, empowering it to autonomously perform necessary operations, including device state detection, trust-related data collection, semantic extraction, task-specific resource evaluation, to derive a semantic trust representation for each collaborator. In addition, each device leverages a hypergraph to dynamically manage potential collaborators according to different levels of semantic trust, enabling fast one-hop collaborator selection. Furthermore, adjacent trusted devices autonomously form a chain through the hypergraph structure, supporting multi-hop collaborator selection. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed semantic chain-of-trust achieves 100% accuracy in trust evaluation based on historical collaborations, enabling intelligent, resource-efficient, and precise collaborator selection.

title: A Rod-Driven Minimally Invasive Bioprinting Continuum Robotic System for Repairing Knee Joint Cartilage Defects_supp3-3670164.mp4
authors: Qian Tang
publish: N/A
abstract: Robot-assisted minimally invasive bioprinting for knee cartilage repair remains a significant clinical challenge. Ensuring bioprinting quality requires the robotic tip to stay perpendicular to the working surface. Rigid robots are limited in controlling tip orientation in the joint cavity, whereas continuum robots, with enhanced flexibility, provide promising solutions. In this article, a minimally invasive bioprinting continuum robot (BCR) system (BCRS) is proposed. First, a novel four-rod-driven double-layer rigid–flexible-coupled BCR is designed and fabricated, featuring enhanced dexterity to accommodate the spatial characteristics of the knee joint cavity. Second, a Lagrangian-based kinetostatic model of the BCR is derived using the geometrically nonlinear Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. Based on this model, an Eulerian analytical moment–equilibrium formulation is established to verify structural safety, and an open-loop feedforward controller is developed to precisely regulate the BCR tip pose. Finally, path-tracking and bioprinting experiments are performed, with results showing a root-mean-square error of 1.89 mm in position and 2.50<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> in orientation. Minimally invasive perpendicular bioprinting within the knee joint cavity is achieved using the proposed BCRS, thereby reducing undesirable molding effects caused by bioink fluidity and providing an effective solution for cartilage repair.

title: Process-Coupled Minimum R on,sp and Its Experiment in 1200-V Si-Based Semi-SJ Device
authors: Rongyao Ma, Ping Li, Hao Zhong, Teng Liu, Wentong Zhang, Zhaoji Li, Bo Zhang, Shengdong Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: The minimum specific <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {ON}\mathrm {,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> coupled with the process for a 1200-V silicon-based semi-superjunction (semi-SJ) device is experimentally achieved. Based on the design formulas derived from our prior research, the key device parameters are preliminarily established. Subsequently, the fabrication process for the semi-SJ device is devised utilizing the multiple epitaxy technology. Aglobal normalization optimization (GNO) is introduced to quantify the influence of the crucial thermal process in the SJ region under a complex temperature field. Consequently, the optimized SJ doping profile for attaining the minimum <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {ON}\mathrm {,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is obtained. The measured results demonstrate that a low <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {ON}\mathrm {,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 39.02m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega \cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is achieved, accompanied by ahigh breakdown voltage <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {B}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 1220V, which is approximately an order of magnitude lower than that of the “silicon limit.” The GNO effectively bridges the disparity between the process-coupled minimum <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {ON}\mathrm {,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and the device theoretical minimum <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {ON}\mathrm {,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.

title: Accuracy Analysis for Bistatic Localization Using Delay and Doppler Factor with Underwater Multipath Effect
authors: Chenglin Zhang, Shaofeng Zhang, Li Yu, Haigang Zhao, Chuanming Zhu
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: For underwater bistatic target localization based on the parameters of the delay and the Doppler factor, the accurate covariance matrix of the joint delay-Doppler estimation is very important. In this letter, the effects of ambient noise, multipath and correlation between the delay and the Doppler factor on covariance matrix calculation is conducted for the wideband underwater acoustic (UWA) channel. Subsequently, we propose a correlation-considered covariance matrix calculation algorithm to evaluate the bistatic localization performance by deriving the theoretical Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) for the estimations of the delay and the Doppler factor. Simulation results verify that the proposed correlation-considered covariance matrix calculation algorithm can acquire the accurate covariance matrix to evaluate the bistatic localization performance.

title: Online Optimization of Video Capturing and Backhaul Scheduling for Multi-UAV Surveillance
authors: Cheng Ma, Zewei Jing, Jinglei Li, Qinghai Yang, Meng Qin, Nan Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The multi-Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle (UAV) networks have been widely studied due to their advantages in regional video surveillance. Specifically, an architecture of multi-UAV network is widely employed for seamless video capturing and backhaul transmission. The multi-UAV surveillance network can dynamically control its video capturing process and resolution selection, allowing for a trade-off between video quality and seamlessness, quantified by the Length of Inter-frame (LoI-F). Each UAV's video capturing, resolution selection, and backhaul scheduling are jointly optimized to maximize the network's average video quality while adhering to a maximum average LoI-F constraint per UAV, ensuring seamless video capturing. An online control algorithm leveraging the Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty method is proposed to address this joint online optimization problem. The network operations based on real-time UAV states are adapted online by the algorithm to enable efficient resource allocation. An optimality analysis of the algorithm is provided, demonstrating the effectiveness of it in achieving the desired goals. Furthermore, the proposed algorithm is extended to a two-timescale variant, specifically targeting the reduction of inter-frame jitter, a critical metric for video smoothness. The optimality loss of this two-timescale algorithm is proved to be upper bounded while preserving the asymptotic optimality of the online control algorithm. Extensive simulations validate the performance of our proposed online control algorithm, showcasing its ability to enhance average video quality compared to other schemes while enabling seamless video capturing for multi-UAV surveillance networks.

title: AI for AIoT as a Service: AI to Configure Models, Capacities, and Tasks
authors: Ying-Dar Lin, Tin-Han Lin, Didik Sudyana, Yuan-Cheng Lai
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) produces vast amounts of data, and integrating AI improves recognition efficiency, giving rise to AIoT. To reduce development and maintenance costs, service providers now offer AIoT as a Service (AIoTaS). However, AIoTaS faces two key challenges: automating accurate AI model configuration and optimizing resource allocation with fast task assignment. To address these, we propose Auto-AIoTaS, which automates model setup using neural architecture search and Bayesian optimization, and employs reinforcement learning (RL) to manage capacity and task allocation efficiently. Experimental results demonstrate that our automated AI model configuration approach significantly improves classification and regression performance. In the classification task on the CIFAR-100 dataset, the accuracy increases from 68.8% with VGG19 and 72.8% with ResNet18 to 81.1%. Furthermore, employing RL for capacity allocation reduces decision-making time from 113 seconds to 0.1 milliseconds, compared to the traditional Simulated Annealing algorithm. These findings highlight the effectiveness of Auto-AIoTaS in enhancing both model performance and resource efficiency, making it a scalable and adaptive solution for AIoTaS service providers.

title: Enhancing Backscatter Communication via Time-Reversal Filtering: Scheme Design and Performance Analysis
authors: Weijia Lei, Hang Yang, Hong Tang, Hongjiang Lei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Backscatter communication utilizes the ambient electromagnetic wave to achieve communication, and is a green communication technique that is bandwidth- and power-efficient. In broadband scenarios, signals experience frequency-selective fading and significant delay spread. This paper proposes a backscatter communication scheme. This scheme employs time reversal (TR) filtering at the receiver to enhance the backscatter signals while keeping the strength of the interference from the direct-link remains almost unchanged. Consequently, the signal-to- interference ratio improves. After TR filtering, the backscatter signals received by multiple antennas are in phase, allowing the signals to be directly combined. On-off keying is used in backscatter modulation, and an energy detector can be used to detect backscatter symbols. The detection error probability is derived and the optimal detection threshold is provided. The correctness of the theoretical analysis and performance of the proposed scheme are verified through simulation. The results demonstrate that the proposed scheme outperforms the comparison schemes by effectively utilizing signal energy and exploiting the diversity and energy gains provided by multiple paths and multiple receive antennas. There is no additional processing at the backscatter device. At the receiver, an energy detector is used and a TR filter is configured for each antenna. The signal processing complexity is low.

title: Deep Q-Network-Based Power Management for Emission-Aware Optimization in Port Ship Microgrid Systems
authors: Mahmoud Elsisi, Mahmoud N. Ali
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The control and optimization of shipboard microgrid operations in port environments pose increasing challenges due to limited shore power capacity and stringent emissions regulations. This paper presents a power electronics-oriented control strategy that leverages a Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework to achieve emission-aware energy management in ship auxiliary systems. The proposed method enables bidirectional power coordination between shore power infrastructure and the ship's onboard microgrid, targeting Minimum Emission Point Tracking (MEPT). By integrating deep reinforcement learning with power system control, the DQN approach dynamically adapts to varying load profiles and ensures optimal engine dispatch, reducing reliance on high-emission operation modes. Additionally, energy storage units (ESUs) are incorporated to enhance system flexibility, with real-time control of battery state of charge (SoC) and adaptive response to specific fuel consumption (SFC) variations. Various scenarios are conducted, including robustness tests and analysis under varying load profiles, grid availability, stochastic uncertainties, and realistic berthing load conditions, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed DQN achieves a higher reduction in emissions by 6.69% and faster convergence to the optimal operational state compared to advanced reinforcement learning approaches (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Twin Delayed DDPG (TD3), Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)) and traditional rule-based methods. These results highlight the potential of intelligent power electronics control for sustainable and high-efficiency maritime applications.

title: Deep-JGAC: End-to-End Deep Joint Geometry and Attribute Compression for Dense Colored Point Clouds
authors: Yun Zhang, Zhiwei Guo, Zixi Guo, Linwei Zhu, C.-C. Jay Kuo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Colored point cloud becomes a fundamental representation in the realm of 3D vision. Effective Point Cloud Compression (PCC) is urgently needed due to the huge amount of data. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end Deep Joint Geometry and Attribute Compression (Deep-JGAC) method for dense colored point clouds. First, we propose a flexible Deep-JGAC framework, where the geometry and attribute encoders are compatible with either learning or non-learning encoders. Second, we propose an end-to-end deep residual self-attention-based geometry encoder to improve geometry coding efficiency, where a Hybrid Residual Self-attention Module (HRSM) is proposed to enhance geometry representation by considering its geometrical importance. Third, to solve the mismatch between the point cloud geometry and attribute caused by the geometry compression distortion, we propose an optimized re-colorization module to attach attribute to the geometrically distorted point cloud for attribute coding, which lowers the computational complexity. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that, in terms of the geometry quality metric D1-PSNR, the proposed Deep-JGAC achieves average Bjøntegaard Delta Bit Rate (BDBR) of -82.96%, -44.63%, -36.46%, -41.72%, and -31.16% compared to the G-PCC (Octree), G-PCC (Trisoup), V-PCC, GRASP, and PCGCv2, respectively. For the perceptual joint quality metric MS-GraphSIM, Deep-JGAC achieves an average BDBR of - 48.72%, -57.14%, -14.67% and -13.37% against G-PCC(Octree), IT-DL-PCC, V-PCC, and DeepPCC, respectively. In addition, the costs of encoding/decoding time are reduced by 32.8%/30.8%, 80.1%/81.8%, 97.2%/35.7%, 98.4%/92.3%, and 96.4%/99.6% on average compared to G-PCC (Octree), G-PCC (Trisoup), V-PCC, IT-DL-PCC and DeepPCC. The code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/SYSU-Video/Deep-JGAC.

title: Qualification Testing Of The 110 Kv Superlink To Iec 63075
authors: O. Holte, C. Thidemann, M. Pitzer, J. Kunert, B. Kozaris, D Willén
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: The SuperLink project aims to develop an attractive cable solution to transport 500 MW of power at the rated 110 kV level in an urban environment. The goals are low losses, reliable electrical insulation and a small physical footprint. The testing standard, IEC 63075, was issued in 2019 and acts as a reference for the testing procedures required to qualify this cable solution for operation in the electrical grid. This article presents our results and procedures for the HV type test, auxiliary sample testing of the conductor and cryostat, the mandatory installation testing and routine testing carried out within the SuperLink Demonstration project. The end result is a 150 m demo system operating for 6 months in a 380kV/110kV substation of the SWM grid in Munich, Germany. In the context of the IEC 63075 test standard, this demonstrator serves as a qualification test to qualify the used materials, processes and designs for further use in commercial applications

title: Escaping swamps through complex space for real canonical polyadic decomposition
authors: C. Vermeylen, N. Vervliet, L. De Lathauwer
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: The canonical polyadic decomposition (CPD) can be used as a low-rank representation of tensors appearing in various applications. However, obtaining a CPD can lead to a difficult optimization problem when, e.g., the rank is higher than some of the tensor dimensions. During the optimization, a degenerate subtensor can then be formed, i.e., an ill-conditioned tensor that tries to approximate a tensor of higher rank. This can lead to regions in the optimization landscape of very slow convergence, informally known as ‘swamps’. An efficient optimization framework is proposed to detect the degenerate terms and let them become complex using a parameterization of the factor vectors in their real and imaginary parts. This gives more freedom to the decomposition during convergence. The eventual solution remains real-valued because of the uniqueness of the CPD. We illustrate the approach for difficult tensors that appear in underdetermined independent component analysis, showing significantly improved accuracy and computational efficiency.
