title: Underwater Image Enhancement Via Dual-Path Frequency-Domain Fusion Network
authors: Yupeng Ma, Qiuling Yang, Rongxin Zhu, Yifei Li, Magd M. Abdel Wahab
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Underwater images typically suffer from color distortion and reduced clarity due to wavelength-dependent light absorption and scattering. Many existing learning-based methods learn a direct pixel-domain mapping from degraded images to enhanced results, while seldom exploiting explicit frequency-domain priors that are important for high-quality restoration. To address this, we propose DPF-Net, a Dual-Path Frequency-domain Fusion Network. Specifically, we first decompose the input into low- and high-frequency components via the discrete wavelet transform (DWT). We then design a low-frequency branch integrating Low-Frequency Guided Attention (LFGA) and the Color Feature Extraction Module (CFEM), and a high-frequency branch integrating High-Frequency Aware Modulation (HFAM) and the Detail Feature Extraction Module (DFEM), to capture frequency-specific characteristics. During upsampling, we introduce a WaveUp module consisting of the Dual-path Up-Interaction Module (DUIM) and a Multi-kernel Convolution-based Fusion Network (MCFN) to enable effective cross-frequency feature interaction and fusion, producing high-quality enhanced images. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that DPF-Net achieves superior qualitative and quantitative performance compared with state-of-the-art methods, and real-world tests further verify its practical potential. Code is available at https://github.com/YuMa-star-lab/DPF-Net.

title: Autonomous Algorithm Synthesis via Large Language Models for Multi-Hop Routing in IoT Networks
authors: Tran Cong Dao, Nguyen Cong Luong, Xingwang Li, Thien Huynh-The, Dusit Niyato, Dong In Kim
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Multi-hop routing optimization in IoT networks involves complex energy–latency trade-offs that challenge traditional algorithm design approaches. This paper presents a systematic framework for Large Language Model (LLM)-assisted algorithm synthesis for multi-hop routing in digital twin (DT)-enabled IoT systems. The proposed three-phase methodology enables LLMs to support algorithmic design by analyzing problem structures, synthesizing hybrid optimization strategies, and iteratively refining routing heuristics under energy, reliability, and latency constraints. The framework generates multiple specialized algorithms, including Advanced Smart Constraint (ASC) for adaptive construction, Energy-Focused Iterative (EFI) refinement, and problem-specific large neighborhood search operators. Extensive experiments on synthetic IoT networks with K = 5–50 devices demonstrate consistent energy reductions of approximately 45% over the random baseline, with statistical significance (p < 0.05) and very large effect sizes (Cohen’s d ≈ 2.5–3.2). Algorithm specialization emerges naturally across network scales, with EFI performing best on small networks and ASC dominating medium to larger configurations. These results indicate that LLM-assisted algorithm synthesis provides a practical and scalable approach for constrained routing optimization in DT-enabled IoT systems.

title: Enhanced DC Series Arc-Fault Detection in LVDC Systems with Capacitor-Assisted Impedance Shaping
authors: Chun-Sung Kim, Hyun-Jun Choi, Taesic Kim, Mina Kim, Wan Kim, Hwa-Pyeong Park
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: A DC grid can achieve high power conversion efficiency by reducing conversion stages between DC energy resources and loads. However, as system complexity increases, DC distribution systems face series arc faults due to damaged power cables and loose connectors, which can induce a fire hazard. Unlike AC grids, DC grids lack natural current and voltage zero crossings, which complicates arc-fault detection. This paper investigates series arc fault detection in LVDC systems with auxiliary DC-link/feeder capacitance for impedance shaping. The proposed capacitor-assisted impedance shaping increases the magnitude of arc-induced current variation and strengthens the frequency-spectrum change of the line current used by the FFT-based detector, thereby improving DC series arc-fault detectability. The operational principle is analyzed to derive capacitance-based impedance-shaping design guidelines for such LVDC systems. Also, a hybrid detector combines time-domain and frequency-domain analyses to detect DC series arc faults, which utilizes the arc characteristics of DC and AC components. Experimental validation of the proposed structure and detection algorithm is performed on a 30-kW laboratory LVDC testbed.

title: Physics-Constrained Adapter-Tuning of Meteorological Foundation Models for Global SST Forecasting
authors: Tianbao Li, Yuting Su, Dan Song, Wenhui Li, Lanjun Wang, Zhiqiang Wei, An-An Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Sea surface temperature (SST) forecasting is fundamental to understanding ocean-atmosphere interactions and maintaining marine ecosystem stability. SST exhibits complex spatiotemporal dynamics characterized by two inherent challenges: global-scale models often fail to resolve local variability, while the gradual nature of global changes obscures short-term temporal fluctuations. Existing approaches exhibit limited capability in simultaneously resolving fine-scale local features and capturing dynamic spatiotemporal evolution patterns. To address these dual challenges in SST forecasting, we propose a novel global daily prediction framework with a 2-day temporal resolution, combining weather foundation model adapter-tuning with physics-informed optimization. The architecture employs a hybrid 3D-ConvNext design that hierarchically integrates volumetric convolutional representations for multi-scale spatiotemporal learning. For temporal modeling, we implement a physics-constrained optimization strategy featuring differential gradient regularization for slow variations and spectral-domain enhancement for transient fluctuations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in global SST forecasting, while showing particular improvements in resolving challenging local dynamics across diverse marine regimes.

title: Improving 33kV Power System Network Using Newton-Raphson Method with STATCOM/DSTATCOM Device in Bayelsa State, Nigeria
authors: Braide Lucky, Lawrence Ekeng
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The demand for clean and reliable electrical energy must be safe at a minimum cost due to the growing energy demand by consumers. As a result, overstressing and overloading of the network, injection substations, transformers, and feeders are done. Hence, sufficient energy cannot be dispatched to meet the ever-increasing load, resulting in undervoltage and intermittent power supply. To overcome these problems, the Newton-Raphson network analysis method in MATLAB 2020 programming language, incorporating STATCOM/DSTATCOM devices, has been utilised to reduce power loss and improve voltage profiles. Sensitivity analyses were performed by simulating voltage loss and voltage profiles of bus voltages of a 33 kV power system network operating at a peak load of 4.46MW with and without the STATCOM/DSTATCOM. Data gathered from the feeder in Tungbo of Bayelsa State under a regulatory scheme limit of ± 5% were taken into account for the power flow study of the distribution feeder using actual and reactive loads of 0.8 and 0.6 of the transformer rating, respectively. From the analysis, 21 Buses were found to be operating below 5% of the rated voltage, but four of these buses, 10, 16, 26 and 30 used for the case studies had the following voltage values, viz, Bus 10= 29563.44 V, Bus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16=29780.18 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex>, Bus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$26=28678.33 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex>, and Bus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30=28812.16 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex>. After the application of DSTATCOM, there were improvements in voltage values on the buses (Bus 10= from 29563.44 V to 33000 V, Bus 16 from 29780.18 V to 33000 V, Bus 26 from 28678.33 V to 33000 V and Bus 30 from 28812.16 V to 33000 V). This confirms an improved voltage with the application of the DSTATCOM device, and the voltage is within accepted <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 5 \%$</tex> of the rated voltage. The results showed that voltage sags were caused by faults and due to the sudden switching of loads in the distribution system. This could be mitigated by inserting a STATCOM/ DSTATCOM device in the affected network. However, the cost of installation of the DSTATCOM device achieved 35% economic benefit significantly in the trade-off between capital investment and gains from reduced or minimised power for improved power quality.

title: Optimal Capacity Planning and Dispatching of Energy Storage Systems in Generation-Load Scenarios
authors: Kai Zhu, Weibang Zhang, Wenhao Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the development of the power system, the installed capacity of renewable energy continues to increase, and various renewable energy sources have become essential measures for electricity consumers to reduce electricity costs. Industrial and commercial users have evolved from traditional single-load consumers to today's integrated generation-load Scenarios. However, distributed photovoltaic (PV) exhibit volatility and intermittency. Given that some distribution networks are relatively weak and cannot handle excessive reverse power flow from PV systems, Energy Storage Systems (ESSs) have become the core solution for balancing supply and demand conflicts and reducing electricity costs. Based on it, this paper proposes an ESS capacity planning method that considers generation-load and electricity price settlement mechanisms. Furthermore, based on the ESS power-capacity ratio determined by the planning approach, an economic dispatch model for ESS operation with conditional constraints is constructed. Using the Big-M method, the model is transformed into a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem for solution. Finally, a practical case study validates the effectiveness of the proposed capacity planning and dispatch strategy.

title: Frequency-Domain Dielectric Spectra Characteristics of Oil-Paper Insulation Under Accelerated Thermal Aging with Different Oilpaper Combinations
authors: Rongfu Zhong, Wanwei Wang, Jiaju Lin, Qiye Du, Congcong Liu, Jiahui Yang, Junjie Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of three different types of insulating paper combined with two types of insulating oil on the dielectric spectra of oil-paper samples, with a particular focus on the effects of thermal aging on the dielectric properties of oil-paper insulation. Dielectric spectrum measurements were carried out on oil-paper samples with various material combinations and different levels of thermal aging. Detailed data of the real and imaginary parts of the complex capacitance, as well as dielectric loss as a function of frequency, were obtained. The experimental results demonstrate that thermal aging has a significant impact on the polarization characteristics and dielectric loss of oil-paper insulation. In particular, in the low-frequency region, both the real and imaginary parts of the complex capacitance show remarkable increases. The intrinsic properties of the paper play a dominant role in determining the degradation rate of dielectric performance, while the quality of the insulating oil not only affects its own dielectric characteristics but also, through interaction with the paper, further amplifies or mitigates the aging effects of the paper.

title: Energy-Aware Runtime Resource Harmonizer for Co-Running Applications
authors: Vanshika Jain, Varun Parashar, Vivek Kumar, Chiranjib Sur
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Nowadays, due to the increasing number of cores and sockets in modern multiprocessor servers, it is essential to coschedule multiple applications simultaneously to maximize system utilization. However, the performance and energy efficiency of coexecuting applications are highly sensitive to thread placement, core allocation, and both core and uncore frequency settings. Existing dynamic resource allocation solutions often rely on model-based approaches, require intrusive modifications to the parallel runtime, or lack a unified framework that can utilize multiple energy-saving mechanisms while supporting different parallel programming models. This paper presents Harmonizer, a novel dynamic resource optimization library for co-running applications on multiprocessor systems to improve overall system throughput and energy efficiency. Harmonizer is oblivious to the parallel programming model and requires no information from prior executions. It periodically profiles each application's CPU, cache, and memory usage by utilizing hardware performance monitoring counters and uses this data to determine optimal thread placement, core allocation, and frequency settings. We evaluate Harmonizer using several co-running mixes of exascale proxy applications on a four-socket, 72-core Intel Cooper Lake processor. Our results show that Harmonizer reduces energy consumption by 8.8% to 35% (20.5% geometric mean) and improves throughput by 4.8% (geometric mean) compared to the default Linux scheduler. Compared to two state-of-the-art approaches, it achieves up to 28.6% energy savings and 15.8% higher throughput (geometric mean).

title: FL-CrossTransNILM: Multi-Load Disaggregation Via Federated Learning and Sparse Communication
authors: Jing Rong, Qiuzhan Zhou, Cong Wang, Huinan Wu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM) aims to disaggregate the power consumption of individual loads from aggregated mains power, playing a critical role in smart grid situational awareness and energy management. To enhance disaggregation accuracy while avoiding the high training cost of separately training multiple load-specific models, we propose a transformer based multi-load disaggregation model named Cross-TransNILM. It employs a shared transformer encoder and load-specific crossattention branches to effectively capture inter-load correlations. Because power data is naturally distributed across many clients and centralized data collection faces privacy regulations, we train CrossTransNILM under a federated learning (FL) framework, referred to as FL-CrossTransNILM, enabling distributed collaborative training without sharing raw data. However, the CrossTransNILM model contains a relatively large number of parameters, leading to a high communication overhead during FL training. To reduce this overhead, we introduce a sparse communication method that uses random Bernoulli masks for selecting parameter updates combined with an error feedback method. Simulation results on two public datasets (REFIT and UK-DALE) show that our method achieves accuracy comparable to centralized training while markedly reducing the communication overhead in the FL setting.

title: A Study on the European Electricity Balancing Market: Mechanism and Modeling
authors: Qichun Shen, Yizhao Liu, Kai Feng, Tianyao Ji, Zhaoxia Jing
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This paper investigates the structure and operational mechanisms of the European electricity balancing market, with a particular focus on the coordinated platforms PICASSO and MARI. While PICASSO manages activation of aFRR through a real-time 4-second optimization cycle, MARI handles mFRR with scheduled and direct activations over a 15minute market time unit. The paper also reviews the regulatory framework for balancing responsibility and settlement, highlighting the role of Balancing Responsible Partys (BRPs) and Balancing Service Providers (BSPs) in maintaining system stability. A simplified optimization model of the PICASSO platform is developed, integrating multiple prioritized objectives such as inelastic demand satisfaction, economic efficiency, fairness, and congestion minimization. Simulation results from a four-area test system demonstrate that enabling imbalance netting significantly reduces total cost and limits activation volumes. The findings support the effectiveness of the PICASSO clearing logic and offer insights into future platform coordination.

title: Medium- to Long-Term Load Forecasting Using Stacking Ensemble Learning Based on a Two-Stage Tree Model
authors: Yanqing Li, Haibo Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: An innovative medium- to long-term load forecasting approach utilizing Stacking ensemble learning is developed for power systems in this study. Initially, considering the non-stationary incremental characteristic of annual electricity consumption, a two-stage tree model training strategy is designed: “linear regression primary prediction” and “tree model residual correction” to mitigate the boundary effect of traditional tree models. By conducting Spearman correlation analysis and error evaluation, the most optimal combination is selected from candidate models such as GRU, XGBoost, and SVR, which are then integrated using the stacking ensemble learning approach. A case study is conducted using historical annual load data and economic data of Jiangxi Province's power grid, validating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Critical Line Identification for New Type Power System in Cascading Failures Based on Multi-Source Information Fusion
authors: Chenkai Song, Lei Qiu, Xianglong Lian
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Global climate change has led to a significant increase in the frequency and intensity of extreme events, while renewable energy generation technologies such as wind and solar power also face challenges related to the randomness of their output. These factors collectively pose severe threats to the safe and stable operation of power systems. Therefore, identifying critical lines and implementing optimization measures are essential for enhancing system resilience. This paper analyzes the evolution process of cascading failures in power systems under extreme events and proposes a critical line identification method based on multi-source information fusion. Firstly, a cascading failure propagation model is established. Secondly, considering both the power flow state and topological characteristics of the grid, the entropy-weighted analytic hierarchy process is employed to assign weights to the Shannon entropy of power flow, structural importance, and load ratio indices, thereby identifying the five most critical lines in the system. Finally, validation analysis conducted on the IEEE 30-node system demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method in identifying critical lines.

title: Building a “Behavior-Policy-Emissions” Dynamic Model for Energy Policy Based on Behavioral Economics Theory
authors: Ye Feng, Shuhong Wu, Yanzuo Chen, Yihong Zhang, Fan Wen, Jingyu Zhou, Yan Chai, Junchao Shi
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Achieving the “dual carbon” goals urgently requires energy policies to shift from a “one-size-fits-all” approach to a “targeted” one. Traditional policy models, based on the “fully rational agent” assumption, ignore behavioral biases of micro-entities and face the dilemma of the “Lucas Critique.” Based on a specialized survey of 1,200 Chinese households and 312 enterprises, this paper identifies key behavioral characteristics such as “loss aversion,” “hyperbolic discounting,” and “social norm following.” It constructs a “Behavior-Policy-Emissions” (B-P-E) dynamic model integrating prospect theory, behavioral decision-making, and social learning theory. This model embeds behavioral parameters into a multi-agent gaming framework, achieving a transition from the “rational agent” to the “behavioral economic agent.” Policy simulations show that behavioral biases significantly distort policy transmission, limiting the effectiveness of single policy instruments (e.g., carbon tax or subsidy). Integrating behavioral insights into policy design (e.g., combining “social norms-economic incentives” or “risk mitigation-signal guidance”) can systematically enhance policy effectiveness by stimulating intrinsic motivation and alleviating psychological barriers. This research provides a micro-foundation and simulation basis for the precise design and modernization of energy policy governance in the Chinese context.

title: Advanced Deep Learning-Driven Safety Assessment and Prediction of Electric Vehicle Optimization
authors: Jay Hemantkumar Shah
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The growing adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) demands advanced methods for assessing safety and optimizing performance. Forecasting EV sales is crucial for all parties involved, including automakers, legislators, and fuel providers. This study suggests a Deep Neural Network (DNN)-based method for forecasting EV range data that includes different brands and driving circumstances. Multiple hidden layers make up the suggested Deep Neural Network (DNN) architecture, which is trained and assessed using common metrics like Coefficient of Determination (R2), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE). With an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 99%, a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 8.10, and a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 9.77, the model demonstrated remarkable prediction accuracy and model fit. The Deep Neural Network (DNN) fared better than models such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Network with Long Short-Term Memory (CNN+LSTM), and Categorical Boosting (CatBoost) on all metrics, according to comparative studies. Scatter plots, prediction curves, and probability density functions are examples of visualization techniques that help confirm the model's dependability and robustness. Results show how successful Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are in predicting the range of electric vehicles (EVs). Larger datasets, real-time data integration, and sophisticated deep learning models will be the main topics of future research to improve prediction performance in practical applications. This research contributes a scalable and accurate solution for Electric Vehicle (EV) optimization.

title: NeuroDrive: An EEG-Based System for Virtual Driving and Robotic Car Navigation
authors: Jyoti Madake, Dhruvesh Kamble, Santosh Kandhare, Kapil Sangameshwar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCIs) have emerged as promising tools for enabling hands-free control of external devices, particularly in assistive technology. This paper presents the development of a low-cost, non-invasive EEG-based mobility control system capable of interpreting four directional commands: Forward, Stop, Left, and Right. EEG signals are acquired using the Bio Amp EXG Pill and streamed via a Raspberry Pi Pico to a host PC. A dual-model machine learning architecture is implemented, with one binary classifier trained to distinguish Forward vs Stop and another for Left vs Right. Real-time classification is performed using features extracted from filtered EEG signals, and predicted commands are transmitted via serial communication to a Maker UNO board, which drives a DC motor-based robotic car. Among the models evaluated, the Random Forest classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 60.28%. The proposed architecture demonstrates real-time response and modularity, offering a practical and scalable solution for EEG-based assistive mobility. Future enhancements include onboard inference, GUI overlays, and expanded command sets.

title: Emergent AI Behaviors in Multi-Modal Fusion Models: Risks, Patterns, and Control
authors: Kshitish Nath, Rishiraj Kohli, Sneh Lata, Sandeep Shrestha
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: the high rates of multi-modal fusion model development (that combine text, vision, audio, and sensory data) have contributed to the occurrence of complicated and unpredictable behaviours of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. Although these emergent activities are at times beneficial, they are a major challenge in-advance in terms of safety, transparency, and alignment to the human agenda. The article here takes a look into the behaviours and the dangers inherent in large-scale multi-modal fusions like emergent behaviours. We examine how interactions between modalities and the mix between latent representation entanglement lead to the unexpected chains of reasoning, goal misalignments and self-rewards. An emergent phenomena taxonomy is suggested including capabilities including zero-shot inference, creative synthesis, and deceptive reason. We also discuss model interpretability, controllability methods and the ineffectiveness of existing guardrails in curbing such actions. Last we hypothesize a system of proactive mitigation of risks involving hybrid monitoring, counterfactual reasoning, and adversarial testing. These results help understand better multi-modal AI systems and iterate on how badly there is a necessity of control protocols guaranteeing robust, ethical and human-aligned deployment.

title: Federated Big Data Processing in Multi-Cloud Architectures
authors: Karthik Linkalapalli, Himanshu Shah, Bala Krishna Rao Guntupalli, Aravind Satyanarayanan, Ponnarasan Krishnan
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The accelerated pace of big data and the resulting spread of distributed applications have led to the requirement of scalable, secure, and efficient data processing platforms in the heterogeneous cloud environment. Federated big data processing on multi-clouds is a promising paradigm that can overcome the problems of data sovereignty, interoperability and performance optimization. This paper proposes a holistic view of how federated processing pipelines can be designed, which would allow multiple cloud service providers to collaborate seamlessly and maintain data locality, compliance, and resiliency. The suggested architecture uses the principles of containerization, orchestration, and federated learning to reduce communication overhead and streamline the use of resources across clouds. In addition, security-enhancing protocols like policy-driven access controls, differentiation privacy, and homomorphic encryption are incorporated to protect sensitive data when processing inter-clouds. Scalability, fault tolerance, query responsiveness, and other experimental results have been shown to greatly improve over traditional single-cloud solutions. The results indicate that federated multiclouds can provide the backbone to the next generation of big data applications in finance, healthcare, and smart cities, where data diversity, security, and compliance are of utmost importance.

title: IoT-Driven Regressive Ensemble LSTM Framework for Scalable Paddy Yield Optimization
authors: P Rohini, S Mohana
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Paddy production needs proper resource management and proper prediction in yield to guarantee sustainable food production. The current smart farming technologies are typically restricted in the area of scalability, real-time decision-making, and heterogeneous sensor information scraping. The current paper would suggest a data-driven approach, otherwise defining a smart farming system which utilises IoT sensors, a predictive modelling framework, alongside optimisation solutions to enhance resource efficiency and productivity of paddy. The framework is based on the Kaggle Smart Agriculture Dataset that offers soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and pH data and uses machine learning and sophisticated data preprocessing and feature engineering methods. It is experimentally proven that the offered methodology gets a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) equal to 3.92, R2 equal to 0.95, and Water-Use Efficiency (WUE) equal to 2.95 kg/m3, which is lower than baseline models such as Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Support Vector Regression. The Scalability analysis represents reduced training (30.2 s) with less prediction latency (9.1 ms). Their findings indicate that the framework is feasible, scalable, and has the capacity to provide real-time actionable information to control the accuracy of paddy farming to allow enhanced yield, cost-effective resource use, and an environmentally sustainable agricultural activity.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of Planar Antenna Arrays Under Element Failures Using Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Shriparna Praveenkumar Hegde, Priyadarshini K Desai
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: At millimeter-wave frequencies, antenna arrays are essential to achieve high directivity and overcome free-space path loss. This work investigates two microstrip patch array geometries — square and rectangular — designed for 24 GHz operation, with active elements as well as few failure elements. The study first analyzes the radiation performance of each configuration with all elements being active, then simulates array with failure elements. In order to meet the required directivity with failure element in the array, optimization is carried out. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is applied to reconfigure the amplitude and phase weights of the remaining elements to restore the achieve active array directivity with no failure elements. The directivity achieved for the fully active square array was 6.29 dB, dropping to 4.02 dB after failure, while the rectangular array decreased from 13.37 dB to 9.09 dB. With the optimization, the achieved directivity for square and rectangular array with failure elements and with the optimization is, square array was restored to 10.30 dB, and the rectangular array was restored to 9.89 dB. Further in this work the spacing between elements is also varied. For the square array, PSO achieves near-complete recovery of directivity. In contrast, for the rectangular array with closely spaced elements, recovery is limited due to increased mutual coupling and reduced aperture uniformity after failure. The study is highlighting the impact of element spacing on performance and resilience.

title: Video Super-Resolution with ESRGAN: A Frame-Wise Approach For Detail Restoration and Clarity
authors: Aksh Chaudhary, Anjali, Dev Garg, Guru Prasad M S
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This research paper aims to utilize ESRGAN to increase video resolution and produce visually enhanced videos. ESRGAN is typically applied to images due to its ability to generate high-resolution image outputs with a great deal of detail, making it a promising approach for video upscaling/enhancement. The proposed work is divided into three phases. Initially, the frame-by-frame approach is employed to deconstruct each video into individual frames. Next, ESRGAN is applied to enhance the quality of individual frames, and finally, the enhanced frames are combined to reconstruct a final enhanced video. Measures are taken to ensure that the enhanced video matches the original video frame rate and temporal structure. The outcome of the work demonstrated significant improvements in the video's visual quality. Future work will focus on optimizing speed for real-time application implementation, as well as enhancing temporal structure and visual coherence to achieve smoother video playback generation.

title: Sleep Disorder Classification Using Bidirectional LSTM Models on Polysomnographic Signal Streams
authors: Sivashanmugam Thangavel, R.Meenakshi, D Vetriselvi, M. Muthulekshmi, Indumathi L, Sathishkumar V E
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This study tackles the essential need for precise, automated classification of sleep disorders using continuous polysomnographic data. The aim is to create a comprehensive deep learning framework that can differentiate among various sleep disorders by examining temporal signal patterns. Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) models were used on the Sleep-EDF Expanded Dataset, including 197 complete polysomnographic recordings from entire nights, which include EEG, EOG, and EMG data, together with carefully annotated hypnograms. The proposed BiLSTM architecture effectively captures both forward and backward dependencies in sleep stages, hence improving the extraction of temporal context across successive sleep cycles. The model attained an accuracy of 97.57%, precision of 91.80%, recall of 91.16%, and F1-score of 91.48%, surpassing conventional machine learning methods used in similar tasks. The findings illustrate the efficacy of BiLSTM in acquiring profound temporal information from unprocessed sleep data streams. This research has practical applications in real-time clinical decision assistance, automated sleep monitoring, and home-based sleep diagnostics. The platform facilitates the integration of wearable sensor data for tailored therapy of sleep disorders.

title: Energy-Conscious Deep Learning Framework for Intrusion Detection in Edge Computing
authors: Mala K, Shashikala M, Jyothi B, Monika Rani H G
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This study proposes an energy-efficient Intrusion Detection System (IDS) tailored for resource-constrained edge computing environments. The framework integrates Separable One-Dimensional Convolutional Neural Networks with techniques such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling, structured pruning, and quantization-aware training to achieve high performance with minimal computational overhead. Evaluation on the NSL-KDD dataset demonstrates the model’s ability to deliver robust intrusion detection with a classification accuracy of 93%, while maintaining a lightweight architecture suitable for deployment on edge devices. The proposed system effectively mitigates class imbalance issues and supports real-time detection in constrained computing environments. These results highlight the framework’s potential for practical deployment in energy-sensitive and resource-limited edge computing scenarios, achieving both efficiency and reliability in cybersecurity applications.

title: Explainable AI for Compliance-Driven Security Incident Response
authors: Muhammad Ismaeel Khan, Aftab Arif, Ali Raza A Khan, Ihtasham Hafeez, Umer Farooq
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The growth of digital security incidents and their complexity and volume require quick and compliant response system. Conventional automated systems have typically focused on speed at the expense of transparency, which poses some problems to organizations facing tough regulatory and governance rules. In this paper, an Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) framework of compliance-based security incident response is proposed, in which the decision transparency is interwoven into the detection, triage, and mitigation workflow. The combination of interpretable machine learning models with rule-based compliance policies, not only speeds up incident resolution but also provides human-readable explanation as per legal and industry standards. The proposed solution takes advantage of the explainability methods including feature attribution and causal reasoning by justifying incident prioritization and response activities hence building the trust among security analysts, auditors, and regulators. Experimental tests of the system on real-world cybersecurity data show that the XAI-enabled system has competitive detection rates with low false positives and generates actionable and compliance-sensitive insights. This piece underscores the importance of explainability in mitigating the disconnect between AI-based automation and compliance-based security governance, which will eventually lead to a resilient and auditable incident response strategies.

title: Particle Swarm Optimized High-Sensitivity Rotating Multiple Concentric Complementary Split-Ring Resonator (RMCCSRR) Sensor for Dielectric Material Characterization
authors: Fei-Yu Qin, Jun-Ji Chen, Wen-Jing Wu, Da-Wei Wang, Wen-Sheng Zhao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Achieving both high accuracy and sensitivity in dielectric material characterization remains a persistent challenge for planar microwave sensors. This paper proposes a novel rotating multiple concentric complementary split-ring resonator (RMCCSRR). The design employs multiple concentrically nested complementary split-ring resonators (CSRR) integrated with a 50 Ω microstrip transmission line, achieving distinct resonant frequency at 2.85 GHz, 5.55 GHz and 7.53 GHz on a 0.508 mm thick Rogers 5880 substrate. The key innovation is the introduction of independent rotation angles for each concentric CSRR. This rotational degree of freedom enables fine-tuning of the EM field distribution and inter-resonator coupling, significantly enhancing interaction with the material under test (MUT). Particle swarm optimization (PSO) is employed to precisely determine the optimal rotation angles. An inverse regression model, utilizing Scikit-learn machine learning algorithms, is developed to predict the complex permittivity of the MUT based on the measured three resonant frequencies and the known sample thickness. Experimental validation demonstrates a maximum relative sensitivity of 9.04% and a maximum permittivity prediction error of 6.30% (average error:3.63%).
