title: Wideband Vivaldi Antenna with Metamaterial Guide to Enhance Gain
authors: Xinran Ji, Haijian Wang, Yue Zhao, Wei Wang, Qiannan Wu, Mengwei Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: The wideband antenna has become an ideal choice for modern wireless communication systems due to its excellent bandwidth advantage. However, the traditional wideband antenna has problems such as low frequency impedance mismatch, high frequency return loss difference, and insufficient full bandwidth gain. A novel high gain wideband Vivaldi antenna is proposed in this paper. The antenna is fed by substrate integrated waveguide (SIW), which effectively optimizes the high-frequency return loss by reducing electromagnetic leakage. Embedding slots in the dagger-shaped radiation patches symmetrically arranged in the antenna has improved the low-frequency impedance matching. Compared with the common metasurface structure, embedding 3D metamaterial guiding elements in the front end of the antenna substrate significantly increases the gain of the operating bandwidth and further reduces the return loss. In order to verify the feasibility of the proposed design, a prototype of the antenna is fabricated and measured. The bandwidth with return loss less than -10 dB ranges from 9 to 70 GHz, covering the X, Ku, K, Ka and V frequency bands. The maximum gain is 12.9 dBi. The proposed antenna has excellent wideband performance, improved impedance matching and enhanced radiation gain, which is suitable for centimeter-wave and millimeter-wave wireless high-speed communication systems.

title: Interference Mitigation Paradigm for Multi-Beam Satellites with Hybrid Analog and Digital Arrays: Phase-Only Beam Nulling
authors: Zhen Chen, Linling Kuang, Zuyao Ni, Bingkun Liu, Zhiyuan Lin
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the rapid development of satellite-enabled Internet of Things (IoT), malicious interference has become an inevitable issue, especially in the uplink due to the exposed satellite location. To mitigate uplink interference, this paper investigates the beam nulling technique for multi-beam satellite communication systems equipped with hybrid analog and digital (HAD) arrays. Unlike fully digital arrays, the beamforming for HAD architectures consists of a high-dimensional analog part implemented exclusively with phase shifters and a low-dimensional digital part with adjustable amplitude and phase, introducing the challenge of limited degrees of freedom. Despite extensive research on HAD beamforming, there is no consensus on the efficient paradigm to implement beam nulling against extremely strong malicious interference. In this paper, we first verify that phase-only beam nulling is an achievable paradigm that fully exploits the beam utilization for multi-beam satellites while effectively mitigating interference. Additionally, we design two normalized phase-only beam nulling approaches based on majorization minimization and per-antenna optimization respectively. Simulation results substantiate our analysis concerning the beam nulling paradigm for HAD arrays, and validate the superiority of our proposed approaches which require significantly lower computational complexity. Furthermore, a prototype is developed to demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed phase-only beam nulling approaches.

title: ReFEND: Leveraging Social Sentiment Resonances for Fake News Detection
authors: Mengya Guan, Jiaxing Shang, Rong Xu, Fei Hao, Ruiyuan Li, Geyong Min
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Fake news detection is a hot topic in the social media mining research community. Recent studies have shown that sentiment signals could significantly benefit the detection performance. However, most existing methods treat sentiment merely as auxiliary features, while the more sophisticated social sentiment interactions were rarely explored. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named ReFEND, which leverages the sentiment resonances among the social users (i.e., social sentiment resonances) and the sentiment relationship between news content and user comments to improve the detection performance. Specifically, we first utilize sentiment scorers to assess the sentiment of comments and identify users' emotional tendencies. Then we creatively construct a sentiment-aware multi-relational graph to capture social sentiment resonances evoked by the content and the interactions between comments and news. Next, we leverage the relational graph convolutional network (RGCN), which specializes in handling multi-relational graph data, to learn the interactions on sentiment-aware graph. To our best knowledge, this is the first effort to leverage social sentiment resonances for fake news detection. Experimental results on three datasets indicate that ReFEND significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art sentiment-based methods in terms of F1 and accuracy. Besides, ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of components designed in ReFEND.

title: Multi-Scale Reinforcement Disentangled Representation for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation Remote Sensing Detection
authors: Jiaqi Zhao, Peirong Guo, Wen-Liang Du, Xixi Li, Rui Yao, Yong Zhou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptation for object detection in remote sensing images is currently a research hotspot. The significant distribution differences between the source and target domain are the core problem of unsupervised domain adaptation for object detection. Remote sensing images are characterized by large variations in scale, complex backgrounds, and numerous small objects, which pose great difficulties to remote sensing object detection. To address these issues, we propose multi-scale reinforcement disentangled representation for unsupervised domain adaptation remote sensing detection. Firstly, the multi-scale disentangled representation module is proposed to reduce the data distribution difference between the source and target domain by disentangling features into domain-invariant and domain-related parts at multiple scales. Furthermore, the reinforcement filtering mechanism is proposed to capture high quality information from the target domain, thereby enhancing adaptability. Experiments conducted on two popular benchmarks show that the proposed method demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: The Importance of Geopolitics in AI Development

title: G -Hausdorff Kannan Mapping and Proximal G -Hausdorff Kannan Mapping in S b Metric Space
authors: P. Selvapriya, P. Dhivya
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: In this article, we introduced G-Hausdorff Kannan mapping and proximal G-Hausdorff Kannan mapping in Sb metric space, and it is used to generalize generalized Kannan mapping. The article demonstrates the application of the proposed mappings to solve an electric circuit equation formulated as a second-order differential equation. This is particularly relevant to circuit theory, where voltage–current relationships in RLC circuits can be transformed into equivalent fixed-point problems.

title: Finite-Time Resilient Output Feedback Control for Semi-Markov Jump Systems With Imprecise Matching and Jumping Information
authors: Haiyang Chen, Guangdeng Zong, Guangming Zhuang, Shiji Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: This paper addresses the finite-time dissipative resilient output feedback control for discrete-time semi-Markov jump systems (S-MJSs). Instead of the Lyapunov stability over an infinite-time horizon, transient dynamics in short-time are focused on. With imprecise mode matching, the controller may access partial information of the system mode, which is assumed time-varying here rather than fixed as studied in the literature. New constraints are developed to leverage the imprecise mode jumping for the S-MJSs. First, a resilient output feedback controller is established to cope with the unmeasurable system states and possible gain perturbations. Then, novel Lyapunov functions dependent on both system mode and elapsed time are proposed, based on which sufficient criteria ensuring dissipative finite-time boundedness are derived. Afterward, traceable design conditions are obtained via new decoupling technique. Further, elapsed time-dependent output feedback controller is devised to improve the results. Finally, the proposed algorithms are verified by both numerical examples and application-oriented examples including the boost converter circuit and electronic throttle systems.

title: Distributed Online Convex Optimization With Unknown Delays
authors: Ping Wu, Heyan Huang, Haolin Lu, Zhengyang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: In the field of distributed online convex optimization with delayed feedback, previous works have primarily focused on scenarios with fixed and predetermined delays. In this paper, we consider a more complex and realistic scenario with unknown delays. Here the delayed periods vary among agents and iterations, and even the time stamps could be unknown. To address the challenges arising from asynchronous delayed optimization and communication within the network, we propose a delayed distributed online gradient descent algorithm that incorporates an agent-specific and delay-tracking learning rate dependent on the amount of feedback received from each local agent. Specifically, we achieve regret bounds of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\sqrt{\bar{d}T})$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\bar{d}\log T)$</tex-math></inline-formula> for general convex and strongly convex loss functions, respectively, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bar{d}$</tex-math></inline-formula> denotes the average maximum delay among agents and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$T$</tex-math></inline-formula> denotes the total horizon. Furthermore, we extend the algorithm and theoretical guarantees to bandit setting by leveraging multi-point gradient estimators. We offer empirical experiments to substantiate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first step to address distributed online convex optimization under the universal delayed setting.

title: Exploiting Highly Inconsistent Image Regions for Active Semi-Supervised Crowd Counting
authors: Sixiang Xu, Huihui Dong, Chen Zhang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Automatically counting the number of pedestrians in an image is a challenging task, but is useful in many practical applications, including intelligent transport systems and congestion alerts. One of the challenges associated with this task is labeling, which is onerous as it requires annotating each pedestrian in the training images. To alleviate the labeling burden, one promising method is Active Learning (AL). Under a limited annotation budget, AL selects the most informative regions for annotation, based on the fact that visually similar regions often exist. Hence, the core of the AL strategy is the region-selection approach. Unlike in previous studies, where the approach aims to select the region representative of the entire image, we argue that the region with highly inconsistent predictions is more informative. To support this argument, we propose a new AL approach: Inter-Scale Bilateral Inconsistency (ISBI). The ISBI measures the prediction divergence between the original image and its scaled images, which allows identification of the regions with the highest inconsistencies. Furthermore, to fully leverage the abundant unlabeled regions, we implement a pseudo labeling strategy for semi-supervised learning. Extensive experiments on mainstream datasets demonstrate that our approach can outperform other state-of-the-art approaches in most cases.

title: 2025 Message from the Program Chairs
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Welcome to the 32nd annual IEEE International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC 2025). This is the eighth year HiPC has broadened the technical program to specifically include topics related to scalable data science and AI, and paper submissions were invited to either the four HPC tracks of Algorithms, Applications, Architecture and Systems Software or the two Scalable Data Science and “AI/ML for System and System for AI/ML” tracks. As the program chairs, we recruited the twelve track vice-chairs who built the program committee with a total of 270 technical program committee members who are experts in their respective track areas and who organized the review to address each area.

title: Optical-Flow-Guided Recursive Prediction-Refinement Neural Network for Particle Image Velocimetry
authors: Hao Lin, Chong Pan, Qingfu Zhang, Shaofei Wang, Yi Zhang, Shuai Qiao, Jinjun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate in-plane displacement field estimation is a key procedure in planar two-dimensional Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). In this paper, we propose an end-to-end deep learning-based model, termed Recursive Prediction-Refinement Neural Network (RPR-NN), for PIV post-processing. The proposed model estimates dense displacement fields from a single pair of PIV images using a lightweight recurrent unit that incorporates key principles of conventional optical flow solvers, including coarse-to-fine multi-resolution pyramids, iterative warping-based refinement, and gradient-based optical flow constraints derived from the brightness constancy assumption. Furthermore, a spatial attention mechanism is introduced to emphasize high-confidence features, effectively addressing the inherent sparsity of PIV images. RPR-NN is trained on the publicly available PIVDataset and achieves superior accuracy and computational running-time efficiency compared to representative PIV post-processing methods. Quantitative assessments on synthetic datasets show that the proposed model exhibits robust generalization across diverse out-of-distribution scenarios that span a wide spectrum of particle seeding density, diameter, image noise levels, and particle displacement. Its effectiveness is further validated through challenging experimental PIV measurements across a wide range of flow regimes, including low-speed and hypersonic flows, where non-uniform particle seeding and low signal-to-noise ratios create substantial difficulties for conventional PIV post-processing algorithms.

title: FWFR-SLAM: A Robust Monocular SLAM System for Flapping-Wing Flying Robots
authors: Zheng Zhong, Zeyang Yu, Lin Dong, Qiang Fu, Xiaoyang Wu, Wei He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) technology enables real-time and accurate positioning of flapping-wing flying robots (FWFRs) through sensor data such as vision, which is one of the key technologies for autonomous navigation of FWFRs. However, its robustness and accuracy in complex environments are still insufficient. In this paper, we propose a robust monocular visual SLAM system called FWFR-SLAM for FWFRs in complex environments. First, in the front-end feature matching, the two-stage feature matching method of global search and local matching in S-HFNET algorithm is used to improve the matching accuracy. Then, the uncertainty modeling of initialization map points is introduced in the monocular initialization process, and the reliable initialization map points are selected according to the evaluation results to reduce the front-end mismatches and redundant calculation. Furthermore, a keyframe selection strategy combining image information entropy and motion instability energy is proposed, which only retains representative frames. Finally, we conduct experimental verification through open source datasets and self-collected datasets. Quantitative results show that compared with ORB-SLAM3-S (ORB-SLAM3 after image stabilization), FWFR-SLAM improves positioning accuracy by more than 41% in challenging environments.

title: Multi-Energy Flow Coupled System Considering Integrated Demand Response Optimization Scheduling
authors: Yanfei Song, Guoxiang Sun, Dongao Li, Mingxu Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Facing the shortage of fossil fuels as well as the pressure related to environmental protection, electrical power systems is evolving towards multi-source synergy, with multienergy flow coupled systems emerging as a key to energy transformation. Integrated demand response technology, due to its excellent coordination and control capabilities, has become an important regulatory means for such systems. This paper set up a collaborative optimization model for integrated demand response and study on the optimal scheduling strategy for multienergy flow coupled systems. Firstly, the architecture of the multi-energy flow coupled system is established, and a refined mathematical model is formulated. Subsequently, relying on the theory of energy hubs, the system's energy flow topological network is constructed. Based on this foundation, optimal dispatch model for multi-energy flow coupled systems incorporating demand response was developed to thoroughly investigate the regulatory potential of demand-side resources. Numerical simulations of typical scenarios demonstrate that this model can achieve complementary coordination of multi-energy flows, fully leverage system flexibility, effectively narrow the load peak-valley difference, reduce system operating costs.

title: Efficient Fine-Grained Gpu Performance Modeling for Distributed Deep Learning of Llm
authors: Biyao Zhang, Mingkai Zheng, Debargha Ganguly, Xuecen Zhang, Vikash Singh, Vipin Chaudhary, Zhao Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: Training Large Language Models(LLMs) is one of the most compute-intensive tasks in high-performance computing. Predicting end-to-end training time for multi-billion parameter models distributed across hundreds of GPUs remains challenging due to complex interactions between transformer components, parallelism strategies(data, model, pipeline, tensor), and multi-tier communication. Learned models require costly sampling, while analytical models often struggle with real-world network and hardware complexities. We address this by decomposing LLMs into core computational primitives and modeling them with: (1) operator-level decomposition for fine-grained analysis; (2) lightweight sampling based hardware-aware prediction models for key operations; (3) an end-to-end prediction system integrating these components across complex parallelization strategies. Crucially, our methodology has been validated on two large-scale HPC systems. Our framework achieves low average prediction errors-4.98% on Perlmutter(A100) and 9.38% on Vista(GH200)-for models up to 20B parameters across 128 GPUs. Importantly, it runs entirely on CPUs, enabling rapid iteration over hardware configurations and training strategies without costly on-cluster experimentation.

title: Predicting Cardiovascular Mortality Risk from Subclinical Hypothyroidism Biomarkers Using Gradient Boosting schema
authors: M.L.S.N S Lakshmi, Punithavathi S, Sowmiya S, Bharathi C, Rajalakshmi S, Keerthana N V
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The hypothetical scientific study depicts a functional concept by which the Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) technique is capable of predicting the death risk related to the cardiovascular system of patients with hypothyroidism who have a lower threshold of the clinical condition. The proposed method allows for an efficacious amalgamation of data preprocessing, feature learning, and model parameters’ optimization with the goal of augmenting not only the model’s accuracy but also an envisaged consistency concerning computational power. GBM's iterative learning procedure significantly contributes to the unveiling of complex, non-linear relationships between biomarkers and cardiovascular endpoints in the research, which are often overlooked by regular statistical models. To garner the clinician's confidence and facilitate the interpretability of the system, the authors employ Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values along with feature importance mapping. The new methods allow medical practitioners being able to isolate the portion of a particular biomarker, which means the right path for calculating risks. As a result, doctors can check for implicit patterns of risk, comprehend the outcomes, and thus make a thorough decision. The core of the novel approach is that it is essentially the intelligent usage of modern, real-time, clinical data, depending on the type, regularization techniques for controlling overfitting, and the capacity for discovering new risk factors that are usually not revealed by traditional analyses. Henceforth, clinicians and researchers are both comprehensively equipped with intelligible and practically implementable observations for the initiation of proper treatment, thus achieving the right moment of medical decisions and tracking the patient's condition. The operability of this conceptual design is among its characteristics: it is scalable, it can be effortlessly customized for other datasets, while also having the potential to be integrated into clinical decision-support systems for non-intrusive and early cardiovascular risk management.

title: A Deep Learning Approach For Trust Based Task Scheduling For Big Data Pre Processing
authors: Katamaneni Vaishnavi, P.Pooja Reddy, P.Ashwick Sai, Chinta Anusha, M.A. Jabbar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Nowadays, it is more difficult to extract and utilize meaningful patterns from complex trust-related data. Existing methods fail to capture temporal and spatial features in real-time data. This interest in MapReduce increased the necessity to develop Big Data settings by combining autoencoders and MapReduce frameworks. Existing models can only identify a specific type of anomaly, and they fail to detect anomalies in real-time data. So, we are implementing a better framework that can work with reducing the Trust-based data. In this paper, rust-sensitive data by ensuring tasks are assigned based on both resource availability and the trustworthiness of nodes. Experimental evaluations have shown that our model improves scheduling accuracy, enhances system reliability, and reduces execution time compared to traditional scheduling methods. The blend of deep learning with the scalable MapReduce model makes our approach very suitable for real-world Big Data applications. Future work may look into integrating more advanced deep learning structures and reinforcement learning techniques to optimize trust-based scheduling in dynamic and uncertain settings. The trust-related data has an accuracy of 98.9% in our scheduling

title: A TabNet-Based Deep Learning Framework for Predicting Uric Acid Elevation and Kidney Health Risks
authors: Satyanarayana Nimmala, R Sahith, K Madan Mohan, Vikram Narayandas, Pallati Narsimhulu, S V Suryanarayana
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The rising popularity of high-protein diets, while advantageous in some situations, has been increasingly linked to heightened uric acid levels and renal impairment. These illnesses often go untreated until significant consequences develop, highlighting the need for predictive and preventive healthcare instruments. This paper introduces a TabNet-based deep learning framework for the early identification of hyperuricemia and renal health issues using structured health data. The model utilizes the NHANES dataset, a comprehensive repository of publicly accessible data including biochemical markers, nutritional consumption, demographic characteristics, and comorbidity indicators. Proposed Model Works in 3 stages. Initially, data preparation includes the cleansing, imputation, and modification of pertinent parameters like blood uric acid, creatinine, BUN, protein intake levels, age, and BMI. Secondly, the TabNet architecture is trained, which uses attentive feature selection to comprehend intricate, non-linear interactions while maintaining model interpretability. In the third phase, the model is assessed and contrasted with leading classifiers such as XGBoost, Random Forest, and MLP, while feature significance is shown using TabNet’s attention masks. The suggested model had an accuracy of 94.2%, exceeding baseline techniques across all principal performance criteria. Findings indicate that consumption of animal protein, salt, inadequate hydration, and compromised filtration are significant factors in increased uric acid levels.This approach exhibits exceptional prediction accuracy and provides valuable insights for personalized dietary planning. Our technique has potential in enhancing AI-driven preventive healthcare and facilitating scalable, personalized dietary risk assessment systems.

title: Cloud System Observability: Automating Infrastructure Insight for Service Level Adherence
authors: Shiva Kumar Bommakanti
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: This paper addresses the fundamental challenge of achieving transparent and automated performance management in evolving cloud environments. It proposes a novel framework for integrating dynamic service policies directly into cloud infrastructure, shifting the burden of performance oversight. By enabling granular visibility and automated verification of operational parameters, the described system facilitates proactive control over resource allocation and service delivery. This work details an approach that leverages real-time insights to ensure adherence to defined service levels and provides a foundation for next-generation, self-managing cloud operations where infrastructure status is continuously and reliably assessed.

title: Developing Knowledge-based Decision Support Systems (KBDSS) for Evidence-Driven Clinical Decision Making
authors: Vijai Anand Ramar, Yashwant Kumar Kolli, Karthik Kushala, Venkataramesh Induru, Priyadarshini Radhakrishnan, S K Pravin Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Knowledge-based Decision Support Systems (KBDSS) play a crucial role in healthcare, enabling doctors to make evidence-based decisions. Therefore, establishing a well- organized and comprehensive healthcare infrastructure is essential. This report explores how Bayesian inference, fuzzy logic, and clustering algorithms are assembled to form a KBDSS, whose benefits on diagnosis are explored. In conclusion, the diagnostic accuracy of expert-based methods is 85%, data-driven approaches achieve 90%, and combination methods yield 88% and 92%, respectively. Expert-based models were the slowest and had the longest time (120%) of the dataset, whereas data-driven (100%) and a combination of these two methods (90%) helped reduce the time for diagnosis support. The situation with system reliability and user satisfaction were different; hybrid models were the system reliability leader (96%) and user delighter (84%) while the combination model increased the clinical outcome by 85%. The paper stresses the necessity of hybridizing systems that find a sweet spot between expert knowledge and learning from big data to advance performance metrics in clinical settings. Nevertheless, problems such as keeping current evidence and integrating KBDSS with current health systems continue to be there. KBDSS might be greatly assisted in its performance by the new AI and machine learning developments. The research provides insights into the efficacy of current clinical decision support technologies and their promising future thus, it contributes to the improvement of their features.

title: A Survey on Deep Learning Approaches for Autism Spectrum Disorder Detection Using Facial Expression Diagnosis
authors: K. Sirisha, M. Lakshmi
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) has emerged as a globally acknowledged developmental condition, affecting individuals worldwide. Accurate and early diagnosis is essential for providing effective intervention and support. ASD encompasses a wide range of social, communicative, and behavioral difficulties, and delayed detection often results in unfavorable long-term outcomes. Although the exact cause of ASD remains uncertain, genetic and environmental factors are believed to contribute. Individuals with ASD often display abnormal facial expressions and emotional responses, which can be key indicators for early detection. Recognizing these facial cues aids in identifying potential signs, enabling timely intervention. ASD is commonly detected using various computational models, including Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), hybrid and other approaches. This survey evaluates papers published between 2017 and 2024, focusing on publication years, research methodologies, effectiveness of the proposed models, and performance evaluations. The findings highlight the frequent use of DL in many of the reviewed studies, presenting promising opportunities for early diagnosis and intervention, with Python being the primary tool for ASD detection. The evaluation provides insights that pave the way for the development of more innovative models in the future.

title: Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data Based on Dynamic Sparse-Dense Transformer and Mamba
authors: Zhe Meng, Zixin Yang, Feng Zhao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) provides rich spatial-spectral information, while light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data offers complementary elevation details. As a result, the fusion of HSI and LiDAR data to improve classification performance has emerged as a prominent research direction. Recently, Mamba has demonstrated significant advantages in long-range sequence modeling with linear computational complexity, whereas Transformer is particularly effective at capturing short-range dependencies. To fully harness the complementary strengths of both architectures, this paper introduces a joint classification model for HSI and LiDAR data named Dynamic Sparse-Dense Transformer and Mamba (DSDT-Mamba). Specifically, we design a Dynamic Sparse-Dense Transformer (DSDT) to extract intra-modal features from HSI and LiDAR data separately. The DSDT integrates Sparse Self-Attention (SSA) and Dense Self-Attention (DSA): SSA alleviates the negative impact of poor query-key matches on feature representation, while DSA preserves sufficient information flow throughout the network to promote discriminative feature learning. Furthermore, features from different modalities are concatenated and transformed into a long multimodal sequence, which is efficiently fused using Mamba to leverage its long-range dependency modeling capability. Experiments carried out on four datasets serve to confirm DSDT-Mamba, with results showing that DSDT-Mamba yields competitive or superior classification accuracy compared to other state-of-the-art approaches.

title: High Performance Long Wavelength InGaN Micro-LEDs Achieving 1.01 GHz Bandwidth
authors: Guanqi Li, Panpan Li, Chunyu Liu, Yang Su, Jinjian Yan, Jianhui Liu, Zihan Xia, Jinchai Li, Zhaoxia Bi, Hongjian Li, Kai Huang, Rong Zhang
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: The low modulation bandwidth of AlInGaP red micro light-emitting diodes (Micro-LEDs) limits full-color visible light communication (VLC) applications. Here, we demonstrate fast modulation speed long wavelength InGaN Micro-LEDs with red multiple quantum wells (MQWs) that exhibit sharp and uniform interfaces. The red Micro-LEDs show extremely low reverse leakage current of 1.77 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-11</sup> A at -5 V and an external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 4.07%. The -3 dB modulation bandwidth reaches 1.01 GHz for 20 μm device, which is the highest one among red Micro-LEDs. The dependence of modulation bandwidth on current densities revealed the transition of carrier lifetime mechanisms with current injection level. Furthermore, an optical communication system was established, achieving open eye diagrams up to 1 Gbps with measured quality factors. These results demonstrate the large potential of long wavelength InGaN Micro-LEDs for next-generation high-speed VLC systems and optical computing.

title: Urgent Computing beyond Low Earth Orbit: Enabling Earth-Independent Operations in Human Space
authors: Tina L. Panontin, Megan E. Parisi, Shu-Chieh Wu, John A. Karasinski, Alonso H. Vera
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: In human space exploration, complexity of vehicles compounded by hostile elements of the space environment can turn otherwise benign anomalies into outright emergencies. On missions in low Earth orbit such anomalies are routinely resolved by flight controllers at the mission control center on the ground. The rigorous data-driven decision-making process that mission control engages arguably epitomizes the essence of urgent science. With destinations of expeditions reaching beyond low Earth orbit, light time delayed communication will render Earth-based support more difficult and necessitate technological solutions backed by urgent computing onboard future vehicles. This paper presents case studies to illustrate the type of problems often encountered in human space exploration and how human-centered urgent computing will be needed help astronauts manage unanticipated safety critical situations that have so far been handled in real-time by the large team of experts on the ground.

title: PointDiff: Conditional Diffusion Model for Point-Supervised Video Moment Retrieval
authors: Zihui Guo, Zhenhai Yu, Yan Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Point-supervised Video Moment Retrieval (PS-VMR) aims to retrieve a video moment that is semantically aligned with a given natural language query through the use of single-point annotations. Existing approaches suffer from sub-optimal performance mainly due to two aspects: i) the generated proposals are centered on the annotation frames which are actually randomly selected, and ii) the usage of multiple Gaussian distributions leads to boundary overstepped. To address these issues, we propose a generative framework for the PS-VMR task with the spirit of diffusion model. Specifically, we introduce a Gaussian prior constraint module to explore salient regions and leverage Gaussian weights to achieve compact pseudo-segments. Subsequently, the multi-modal conditional diffusion module diffuses the pseudo-segment into random noise, which is further denoised back to the initial segment with the guidance of similarity between query and video. It boasts an enhanced ability to capture video-query relationships and alleviate reliance on the annotation frame. Moreover, a noise intensity prediction task is integrated to assist the denoising process in the multi-modal conditional diffusion module. We conduct extensive experiments on three benchmarks of Charades-STA, ActivityNet Captions and TACoS, where the proposed network outperforms state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating its effectiveness for this task. All codes will be available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZhenhaiYu/PointDiff</uri> <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZhenhaiYu/PointDiff</uri>.

title: StereoAnything: Advanced Zero-Shot Stereo Imaging for Robotic Grasp Detection With Transparent Objects_supp1-3670734.mp4
authors: Lei Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Grasping transparent objects remains challenging for robotic systems due to their reflective and refractive properties, which distort depth perception and introduce background noise. Unlike humans, who leverage life experience to perceive depth intuitively, robotic algorithms often fail to generalize across different object types. To address this, we propose a novel framework inspired by human perception for grasping transparent objects. Our approach extends features extracted by foundation models to implicitly learn reconstruction strategies for transparent objects without requiring segmentation priors. Crucially, our framework maintains strong performance across all types of objects and scenes, preventing catastrophic forgetting of opaque objects while learning to perceive transparent ones. By integrating affordance information, our method dynamically guides a five-finger dexterous hand to execute diverse grasping strategies based on human intent. To tackle the challenge of annotating transparent objects, we constructed a large-scale synthetic dataset with depth information, affordance data, and automated annotations. Our framework demonstrates strong generalization, achieving a 96% grasp success rate in real-world robotic experiments and proving its broad applicability across varied environments.

title: Mamba-MPSE: Multi-Pattern State Evolution Based on the Mamba Model for Intra-Class Heterogeneous Wetland Classification with UAV Hyperspectral Imagery
authors: Song Zhao, Qiaoyu Liu, Zhaoyu Liu, Yu Chen, Wei Li, Junjie Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In hyperspectral imagery, intra-class heterogeneity often arises from local environmental variations and spatial distribution non-uniformity, leading to significant discrepancies across spectral and spatial dimensions and limiting the capacity of unified modeling mechanisms. The Mamba model, built upon the Selective State Space Model (SSM), integrates local perception with long-range dependency modeling; however, its state evolution matrix adopts a single evolution pattern, lacking dynamic adaptability to heterogeneous features. To address this limitation, we propose a unified framework that integrates Multi-Pattern State Evolution (MPSE) and Dual-Path Spectral-Spatial (DP-SS) modeling. MPSE introduces multiple learnable evolution patterns for hidden states, enabling each sample to dynamically integrate diverse historical information and thereby capture intra-class spectral-spatial heterogeneity. Meanwhile, DP-SS models features along both spectral and spatial paths, enhances the representation of spectral trends and spatial structures, and fuses the two paths for adaptive global feature integration. The synergy of these modules strengthens the model’s ability to discriminate intraclass heterogeneous land covers. Experiments conducted on a self-constructed UAV hyperspectral dataset of the Yellow River wetland demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving an overall accuracy (OA) of 98.27%, an average accuracy (AA) of 98.84%, and a Kappa coefficient of 98.63%, offering a new perspective for efficient and fine-grained hyperspectral image classification.

title: A Fully-Portable and Highly-Sensitive All-Fiber Quantum Magnetometer
authors: Chaowen Peng, Kaiyan Liu, Feishu Cao, Piwei Tseng, Yue Wen, Ximian Yao, Yuandong Chen, Minghui Du, Lei Chen, Guishi Liu, Yunhan Luo, Yaofei Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Fiber-optic quantum magnetic sensors based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond offer unique advantages, such as flexible optical path design and high integration, significantly enhancing their potential for practical deployment. However, existing fiber-based NV magnetometer systems predominantly rely on bulky discrete components and relatively low sensitivity, imposing critical constraints on system miniaturization and field applicability. Here, we report a fully-portable all-fiber quantum magnetometer achieving high sensitivity through three compact modules: a sensor probe integrating a conical fiber tip with micron-scale diamond structures sandwiched between paired pyramidal magnetic flux concentrators; a control module combining optical and signal processing subsystems with a customized compact microwave lock-in composite module; a power supply unit. Magnetic and optical simulations reveal synergistic optimization of magnetic concentrators and optical interfaces, providing critical insights for optimizing quantum sensor performance parameters. The integrated system occupies 21×16×9 cm3 with 14.9 W total power consumption while demonstrating a sensitivity of 57.12±1.15 pT/Hz<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/2</sup> @1 Hz. The physical size of the sensing probe is 6.7×4.7×4.7 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Field validations in operational elevators and moving vehicles successfully detected sub-100 nT magnetic anomalies with temporal resolution matching target kinematics. This architecture establishes a paradigm for practical NV magnetometer miniaturization, enabling deployable quantum sensing in real-world scenarios.

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Chlorophyll-a Estimation in Coastal Waters from Top-of-Atmosphere VIIRS Satellite Data_supp1-3670718.pdf
authors: Richa Dutt
publish: N/A
abstract: Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) concentration is a key indicator of water quality and phytoplankton biomass, used to monitor algal blooms in coastal and offshore waters. Traditional Chl-a prediction methods involve a two-step process: atmospheric correction to convert top-of-the-atmosphere (TOA) data to water-leaving radiance, followed by a machine learning or band-ratio algorithm. However, atmospheric correction is challenging in optically complex waters, reducing prediction accuracy. This study introduces a novel single-step approach that directly predicts Chl-a from TOA data using machine learning, bypassing the need for atmospheric correction. An attention-based data-driven atmospheric compensation model is also applied, incorporating contextual features such as sea surface pressure and sun geometry to enhance predictions. The experiments used real-world satellite data collected over the Chesapeake Bay and the Florida coast. The experimental results in the Florida regions demonstrate that using a multilayer perceptron (MLP) with TOA data achieves a logarithmic mean absolute error (Log MAE) of 1.830, outperforming both the Log MAE of 1.843 obtained with Level-2 reflectance data and Log MAE of 4.087 from the Chlorophyll 3-band algorithm (OC3V). The data-driven atmospheric compensation model improves this further, achieving a Log MAE of 1.745. A similar trend is observed in the Chesapeake Bay region data experiments. A key advantage of TOA data is that the model can predict Chl-a in regions previously excluded in the Level-2 reflectance data due to Level-2 quality checks, thus providing broader coverage.

title: Shape2Mask: A Weakly Supervised Instance Segmentation Method for Aluminum Alloy Precipitations Based on Prior Shape Knowledge
authors: Mingchun Li, Naishi Feng, Qiang Wang, Dali Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The size, distribution, and shape of precipitations in aluminum alloys critically influence their mechanical properties. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) enables the analysis and measurement of these nanoscale precipitations. However, the inherent fuzziness and overlapping in TEM images necessitate annotation by domain experts. It not only increases the cost of pixel-level annotation but also poses significant challenges for segmentation algorithms. To this end, we present Shape2Mask, a novel instance segmentation framework that utilizes low-cost box supervisions to predict masks by parameterized shape modeling. First, it establishes a novel shape-based segmentation paradigm. Instead of pixel-wise prediction, it predicts shape parameters of precipitations, effectively handling fuzziness in TEM images. Second, we design a hierarchical module based on Graph Neural Network (GNN) and recent Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN). The former is employed to extract effective geometric shape representations, while the latter is used to fit nonlinear shape parameters. Furthermore, benefiting from the shape-based paradigm, we introduce a differentiable prior loss function, explicitly integrating domain knowledge into the deep model. Experiments demonstrate our method accurately segments three types of precipitations with an AP score of 29.5 under box supervision. Quantitative measurements show that Shape2Mask achieves errors as low as 1.2 nm and 9.8 nm² in precipitation length and area, respectively. It significantly outperforms existing weakly supervised methods for both segmentation and measurement metrics. These results prove the method's effectiveness not only in visual tasks but also in providing valuable quantitative measurements for potential materials science applications. Our source code and TEM image dataset are available at https://github.com/neulmc/Shape2Mask.

title: Application of the conversion research results for international cooperation Sibconvers'99
authors: N/A

title: Data-Driven Control of Large-Scale Networks with Formal Guarantees: A Small-Gain-Free Approach
authors: Behrad Samari, Amy Nejati, Abolfazl Lavaei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: This paper offers a data-driven divide-and-conquer strategy to analyze large-scale interconnected networks, characterized by both unknown mathematical models and interconnection topologies. Our data-driven scheme treats an unknown network as an interconnection of individual agents (a.k.a. subsystems) and aims at constructing their symbolic models, referred to as discrete-domain representations of unknown agents, by collecting data from their trajectories. The primary objective is to synthesize a control strategy that guarantees desired behaviors over an unknown network by employing local controllers, derived from symbolic models of individual agents. To achieve this, we leverage the concept of alternating sub-bisimulation function (ASBF) to establish a relation between the state trajectories of each unknown agent and its data-driven symbolic model. Under a newly developed data-driven compositional condition, we then establish an alternating bisimulation function (ABF) between an unknown network and its symbolic model based on ASBFs of individual agents while providing correctness guarantees. Despite the sample complexity in monolithic studies being exponential with respect to the network size, we demonstrate that our divide-and-conquer strategy reduces it to the subsystem level. We also showcase that our data-driven compositional condition does not necessitate the traditional small-gain condition, which demands precise knowledge of the interconnection topology for its fulfillment. We apply our data-driven findings to three benchmarks comprising unknown networks with an arbitrary, a priori undefined number of agents and unknown interconnection topologies.

title: False Data Injection Attack Detection Based on Fuzzy Reinforcement Learning for Smart Grids
authors: Zhao Peng, Qiuyu Lu, June Li, Qi Shang, Jing Wang, Kaipei Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: As smart grids integrate increasing communication technologies, they face sophisticated cybersecurity threats, particularly coordinated false data injection attacks (FDIA) that manipulate both power measurements and network topology. Existing detection methods are often limited in processing the non-Euclidean data of power grids or in handling dynamic multi-mode attacks. To address these challenges, we propose a novel detection framework that combines a fuzzy residualconnected graph convolutional network (FRC-GCN) with a multi-agent deep Q-network (MA-DQN). The FRC-GCN effectively extracts features from non-Euclidean grid data by quantifying measurement uncertainty via fuzzy membership functions. The MA-DQN models detection as a collaborative multi-agent process, enhanced by a dynamic dual reward mechanism to handle diverse attack types. On the IEEE 39-bus system, our method achieves an accuracy of 92.34 %, maintains a high F1-score of 0.736 even under 10 % data loss, and demonstrates superior training efficiency and real-time usability. These results demonstrate that our method provides an accurate, robust and scalable solution for real time FDIA detection in smart grids.

title: Analysis on Ground Wire Fracture and Treatment Measures of ± 500Kv Jinzhong DC Transmission Line
authors: Shizeng Liu, Chenkai Zhang, Yigang Ma, Tongbo He, Bingnan Liu, Shicheng Yan
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: The safe and stable operation of power systems is essential for supporting societal productivity and living standards. With the continual expansion of power grid infrastructure, the operation and maintenance of overhead transmission lines have become increasingly critical. Ground wire breakage has emerged as a significant factor affecting transmission line reliability. This study investigates strand breakage incidents on the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{5 0 0 ~ k V}$</tex> Jinzhong DC transmission line, in operation for many years, by integrating lightning activity records along the route with test results from ground wire and optical cable samples removed from fault sections. Analysis indicates that the primary cause of strand breakage is lightning-induced damage, which - under conditions of insufficient discharge capacity and absence of effective protective measures-leads to progressive failure of the ground wire. Based on the statistical and diagnostic findings, this paper proposes targeted preventive measures and operational recommendations to mitigate the risk of similar failures in the future.

title: Passive Damping Design of T-Type Filter Based on Full Frequency Domain Passivity
authors: Jiaxiang Zheng, Bingda Zhu, Rutian Wang, Chuang Liu, Xuejiao Li
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Aiming at the grid-connected instability of converters induced by the inherent resonant peaking in T-type filters, this paper presents a parameter optimization method based on a resistive-capacitive series passive damping branch (RC-PD). First, a dq-frame admittance model of the T-type grid-connected voltage-source converter (VSC) system with RC-PD is established. By deriving the system stability conditions using passivity theory and simplifying through admittance decomposition, the non-passive regions requiring compensation are identified via parameter impact comparison. Building on this, combined with the compensation capability of RC-PD, a PD parameter optimization method is constructed to enforce full-frequency passivity of the output admittance, thereby satisfying stability requirements. This method demonstrates universality for a class of grid-connected VSC systems equipped with T-type filters. Finally, a real-time simulation platform for grid-connected VSC is established to verify the effectiveness and correctness of the proposed method.

title: Two-Stage Deep Compression for Edge Load Forecasting Model Deployment
authors: Shuaiwei Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Edge load forecasting has become essential for the efficient management of distributed energy resources. However, deploying deep learning models on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge due to high memory and computational requirements. To address this issue, we propose a two-stage deep compression framework that integrates pruning and quantization techniques into deep neural networks for load forecasting tasks. Our method first prunes the network, effectively reducing redundant parameters by retaining only the most important connections, thus maintaining high predictive performance. Subsequently, further compression is achieved by quantizing the weights and activations to lower-precision formats, substantially decreasing computational cost without significant loss in accuracy. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves substantial reductions in model size and computational complexity, with negligible loss in forecasting accuracy. This enables the practical deployment of load forecasting models on edge devices, facilitating real-time and energy-efficient analytics.

title: IMBPS - Iterative MLP Blocks with Parameter Splits for Improving LLM Inference
authors: Ganesh Prasad Nagaraja, Arun Coimbatore Ramachandran, Shubhendu Sharma, R. Govindarajan, Prakash Sathyanath Raghavendra
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data, and Analytics (HiPC)
abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) is increasingly challenged by their substantial computational and memory demands, straining the capabilities of current hardware. A significant, yet often less explored contributor to this inefficiency lies within the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) blocks of transformer architectures, where sequences of tensor operations introduce considerable overhead. Although attention mechanisms have been the focus of extensive optimization, the MLP component has remained a comparatively underexplored frontier for performance enhancement. Our paper aims to address this critical gap in LLM inference by introducing Iterative MLP Blocks with Parameter Splits (IMBPS). IMBPS introduces a lossless blocking strategy designed to enhance LLM inference efficiency through two principal mechanisms. We present a concise analytical model that facilitates effective mitigation of memory-related overhead, specifically targeting data-packing and peak activation footprints in MLP computations. This approach leads to a significant reduction in cache misses. Our empirical evaluations provide compelling evidence of these benefits, with results indicating a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1. 5 - 1. 6 x}$</tex> speedup for standalone MLP blocks and end-to-end (E2E) performance improvements of up to 1.37 x.

title: Enhancing Accessibility of Sacred Texts Using NLP for Translation and Summarization
authors: Aravind Chandran, Hanumanagutti Venkata Nikhil, J. Sastic, Raj Pandi M, Prathicshaa R, Karthikeyini. M
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The auto-generation of summaries and translations of sacred texts raises good challenges about the comprehensibility, cultural interpretation, and the spiritual significance of the texts. In this paper, a BERT attention model is proposed that is able to read and comprehend passages of the sacred texts and produce both translations and short notices on these texts. The model uses a combination of bi- activated long short-termed memory, convolutional neural networks, and the attention mechanism built over an encode- decode architecture that will work on capturing the deep linguistic and semantic structures of sacred languages. It presents how training on sacred literature enables the model to produce an appropriate, relevant, and precise translation along with a summary. Their evaluation is based on ROUGE and BLEU metrics with significant improvements to the productivity of traditional capital models. Other issues include limitations and risks of biases alongside prospects for improvement in accuracy, as well as the integration of culture in the processing of sacred texts the paper explores.

title: Behavioural Pattern Recognition for Anomaly Detection in IoT Using Random Forest and K-Means Hybrid Model
authors: Shanmuganathan V, J. Shanmugapriya, Sarangam Kodati, Thaya Madhavi, S. Mohanasundaram, Dinesh S
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Detecting irregularities in Internet of Things (IoT) systems is essential due to the increasing dangers associated with cyber threats and irregular device behaviour. Current models frequently inadequately detect complex behavioural anomalies and struggle to adjust to evolving IoT contexts. The suggested study introduces an innovative hybrid model that integrates K-Means clustering with Random Forest classification for efficient behavioural pattern recognition and anomaly detection. K-Means facilitates the unsupervised clustering of device activity profiles derived from extracted characteristics, while Random Forest offers a strong and interpretable classification method for identifying abnormalities within clustered patterns. This hybrid method, compared to standard models, harmonises interpretability, accuracy, and scalability. The system was analysed utilising publicly available IoT datasets, demonstrating exceptional performance across all principal metrics: Accuracy (0.981), Precision (0.978), Recall (0.984), F1-Score (0.981), and AUC-ROC (0.992). The comparative investigation explains that the model outperforms baseline techniques including SVM, Isolation Forest, and Logistic Regression. The results confirm the efficiency of integrating behavioural pattern clustering with ensemble learning in anomaly detection tasks. This method provides practical applications for safeguarding real-time IoT implementations in smart homes, healthcare systems, and industrial control settings, while also establishing a foundation for future integration with deep learning and adaptive anomaly response strategies.

title: Prostate Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning with Clinical-Structural Data Integration in an EfficientNetV2B3 Framework
authors: Ragini Y P, Mohammad Omar Sabri, Rajkumar Bhookya, Swathi B
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Prostate cancer continues to be a cause of cancer-related deaths among men and the creation of better diagnostic instruments is needed in this case. This paper lays out a deep learning model called EfficientNetV2B3 architecture to fuse clinical and structural patient data and improve the prediction of prostate cancer. The degree of training and validation occurred in a labeled dataset by placing clinical variables and structural imaging features into the model. Confusion matrix analysis, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and loss/accuracy tracking of 50 epoch were used to measure the performance of the model. The outcomes indicated that the overall ROC AUC was 0.92 with great discriminatory capacity between malignant and benign. The confusion matrix showed accurate prediction of 8 benign and 4 malignant cases with 8 malignant cases being predicted as benign and 0 benign cases as malignant. During the course of training validation accuracy was always above 70 percent and reached well above 90 percent during training. The results make EfficientNetV2B3 promising as deep learning to use multimodal data in prostate cancer diagnostics.

title: Evaluation of Hybrid and GPU-Optimized Explainability Methods for Tree-Based Model
authors: Rethika K Jegan, Suprajha Baskaran, Gurupriya M, Gayathri R
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The interpretability of the model is hindered by several barriers that restrict the adoption and use of AI systems in high-stakes decision-making. Conventional explainability algorithms like SHAP and LIME provide useful information, but can be compromised by trade-offs in runtime performance, stability, and local fidelity and cannot be used in practice on real world and large scale problems. In response to these issues, this paper will propose a Hybrid Explainer Framework based on GPU acceleration, which consists of SHAP and LIME, having a tradeoff between global consistency and local interpretability. The model was both trained on the Adult Income data with a GPU- optimized XGBoost classifier and it showed a high predictive accuracy of 87.6, F1-score of 0.71 and ROC-AUC of 0.93. Comparative benchmarking of SHAP, GPU-SHAP, LIME, and the Hybrid method by runtime, stability, consistency, faithfulness and comprehensiveness revealed that SHAP was the most reliable with GPU-SHAP the fastest, whereas the Hybrid method was the most balanced with a stability of 0.71 and comprehensiveness of 0.176. The findings demonstrate the feasibility of scalable and reliable AI with a practical approach to explainability in critical tasks with the help of a GPU.

title: Real-Time Water Quality Classification for Aquaculture Using Machine Learning and Cloud Integration
authors: Vivek Raj Yadav, Saurav Suman, Vishnu Raj, Anand R
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Maintaining proper water quality is essential for the health and productivity of aquaculture systems. Relying on manual measurement of crucial parameters is generally time-consuming, inconsistent, and reactive rather than preventive. This study presents a machine learning-based framework for real-time classification of water quality in prawn farms, using key physicochemical indicators such as pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen (DO), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), turbidity, carbon dioxide (CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>), and ammonia. The proposed system employs LightGBM, a high-performance gradient boosting algorithm, trained on labeled historical data to classify water conditions into three categories: Good, Moderate, and Bad. The trained model is integrated with sensor input and deployed to perform real-time predictions, with results logged to Google Sheets for live monitoring via mobile or web interfaces. Comparative analysis with models including XGBoost, Random Forest, SVM, and Logistic Regression indicates that LightGBM delivers superior performance, achieving a test accuracy of 99.67%, an F1-score of 0.9977, an AUC-ROC of 0.9992, and a log loss of 0.0288. Unlike prior studies on generic aquaculture, this work uniquely integrates LightGBM with real-time sensor data and cloud deployment, specifically tailored to the sensitivity of prawn farming condition.

title: Leveraging Masked Autoencoders and Multi-Scale Feature Fusion for Predicting Remaining Useful Life in Rolling Bearings
authors: Maiyue Kang, Hui Yi, Ming Lu, Qingao Peng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: To overcome the drawbacks of current data-driven approaches—such as insufficient degradation feature extraction, inadequate utilization of spatiotemporal information, and poor generalization capability—this paper proposes a Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction method for rolling bearings based on a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) and Multi-Scale Feature Fusion (MSFF). To improve the model's capacity to adapt to noise and missing information, the suggested methodology first uses the MAE for self-supervised pretraining on raw vibration signals. This allows the model to learn robust global feature representations from partially masked data. Then, using parallel convolutional branches, a Multi-Scale Convolutional Attention Encoder (MSCAE) is created to record both local details and global patterns over a variety of temporal scales. In order to adaptively fuse multi-scale features and highlight important degradation characteristics, a channel attention technique is devised. Lastly, for accurate RUL estimation, a fully connected regression layer is used. The suggested approach performs noticeably better than a number of cutting-edge models in terms of Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE), according to experimental results on the PHM2012 bearing dataset, confirming its superiority in terms of both predicted accuracy and robustness.

title: Untangling the Low Accuracy of the Entangling Instruction Prefetcher
authors: Vedant Kalbande, Biswabandan Panda, Alexandra Jimborean, Alberto Ros
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: The Entangling Instruction Prefetcher (EIP) is a state-of-the-art mechanism designed to improve instruction-fetch timeliness. While earlier studies report an average prefetch accuracy of 72% and as high as 91%, more recent evaluations using different methodologies—most notably the gem5 simulator— report lower accuracies of 44% on average and even below 30%. This work aims to shed light on this controversy by providing a robust and faithful open-source implementation within the gem5 simulation framework. In addition, we analyze several design choices that influence EIP's accuracy and performance highlighting common pitfalls when porting EIP across simulators. In our experiments, using the same benchmarks as prior studies, our gem5 version of EIP achieves an average prefetch accuracy of 64.35%, demonstrating that its accuracy remains stable across the simulators.
