title: Explainable AI Framework for Retinal Images Collected by Consumer Electronics: Research on Early Screening of Uveitis and Adherence Improvement in Connected Healthcare Patient Follow-Up
authors: Lili Hu, Yuxuan Peng, Hang Zhang, Jing Wang, Yue Bin, Rui Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: This paper presents an explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) framework to assist with the detection of posterior segment inflammatory signs associated with uveitis through retinal images obtained via consumer electronics. We investigate the integration of deep learning architectures with interpretability mechanisms to enhance diagnostic transparency while assessing the framework’s impact on patient appointment adherence within connected healthcare ecosystems. Our proposed Uveitis-XAI-Net architecture employs a multi-scale convolutional neural network backbone integrated with dual explainability modules utilizing Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) and Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP). The framework was validated using a multi-center dataset comprising 4,826 fundus images captured via smartphone-based imaging devices from 1,543 participants across diverse clinical settings. The explainable AI system was deployed within a connected healthcare platform delivering personalized follow-up appointment reminders coupled with AI-generated diagnostic reports. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed framework achieves an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) of 0.924, sensitivity of 87.3%, and specificity of 89.6% for detecting uveitis-related posterior segment findings, with explainable outputs exhibiting strong concordance with expert ophthalmologist annotations (Cohen’s Kappa κ = 0.81). A prospective 12-month clinical evaluation involving 798 patients revealed that XAI-enhanced digital health intervention significantly improved follow-up appointment adherence rates compared to standard care (78.5% vs. 52.4%, p < 0.001) and disease knowledge scores (mean improvement 24.7 percentage points, p < 0.001). These findings substantiate that incorporating explainable AI into consumer-grade imaging equipment supports detection of uveitisassociated retinal manifestations while enhancing patient engagement and long-term follow-up maintenance through transparent diagnostic assessments.

title: Decoupling Target Semantics via Text-anchored Visual Contrast for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Qingjie Zeng, Huan Luo, Xinke Ma, Zilin Lu, Yang Hu, Mengkang Lu, Yanning Zhang, Yong Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) provides an effective means of reducing reliance on large-scale annotated datasets by leveraging unlabeled data. However, existing SSL methods often struggle with semantic ambiguity, especially under limited supervision. Recent studies have incorporated textual information to provide contextual guidance, yet most focus on feature fusion rather than emphasizing target semantics critical for segmentation. In this paper, we proposed a novel Text-anchored Visual Decoupling (TeViD) framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation. TeViD is built upon a teacher-student architecture with a dual-decoder design that explicitly disentangles target and background representations using both labeled and unlabeled data. For unlabeled data, a reversed cross-supervision mechanism is introduced to enhance decoder diversity and semantic separation. Furthermore, two contrastive learning objectives are proposed: a teacher-guided visual contrastive loss and a text-anchored contrastive loss, both designed to reinforce semantic disentanglement from visual and textual perspectives. Extensive experiments on five public datasets (covering X-ray, pathology, ultrasound, MRI, and CT) demonstrate that TeViD consistently outperforms both standard SSL and text-enhanced SSL methods, achieving average improvements of 5.72% in Dice and 8.15% in mIoU over the second-best competitor. The code is available at: https://github.com/jgfiuuuu/TeViD.

title: An Advanced Quadratic Boost Converter with Low Voltage Stress Devices for Renewable Energy Sources
authors: Seshagiri Rao Vemparala, Mahmoud F. Elmorshedy, M. S. Bhaskar, Dhafer Almakhles, Md Rabiul Islam, Rakibuzzaman Shah
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Quadratic gain-based converters achieve substantial gains when the duty ratio is greater than 0.7. Furthermore, most of the converters require switch and load diode ratings that match their output voltage, resulting in higher switching losses and reduced efficiency. To address these issues, this work introduces an advanced quadratic boost converter (AQBC). The AQBC has a wide voltage gain range, allowing for operation at a reduced duty ratio and lowering voltage stress on the components. This enables the selection of components with lower ratings, which improves the AQBC's overall performance. Furthermore, AQBC exhibits continuous source current and common ground. This work describes the steady-state operation of both continuous and discontinuous conduction modes (CCM and DCM) and investigates the impact of parasitic elements on voltage gain and efficiency. Small-signal model design is used to analyse the dynamic performance of the AQBC converter. The performance of the AQBC is compared to that of traditional quadratic gain-based converters. An experimental model with a 400 V output voltage and 270 W power was created to evaluate the functioning and back up the theoretical analysis with experiments.

title: Breaking the Bottleneck of Heterogeneity in Federated Learning Through GBG: Grouping, Block Training, and Global Distillation_supp1-3673419.pdf
authors: Lu Dong
publish: N/A
abstract: Federated learning (FL) collaboratively trains a global model across multiple clients without sharing local data, effectively utilizing data while preserving privacy. However, real-world data are often non-independently and identically distributed ( non-IID) and heterogeneous across clients, making standard FL less suitable for practical deployment. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel framework named GBG which includes grouping, block training and global distillation. First, it introduces a novel grouping mechanism that clusters heterogeneous clients into different groups. Clients in these groups perform different training tasks. Second, it employs Symmetric Balanced Incomplete Block Design (SBIBD) to construct intra-group blocks and establishes a new training paradigm called block training. Finally, by incorporating mutual learning, the GBG framework enables the effective development of both personalized block-level models and a global model. Moreover, we demonstrate the convergence of block training in combination with existing works. Additional experiments further demonstrate that the GBG framework achieves favorable results in both testing accuracy and training error.

title: Performance Analysis of Multi-UAV-Aided NB-IoT Communication System_supp1-3673845.pdf
authors: Naveen Mysore
publish: N/A
abstract: Non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) for Internet of things (IoT) are one of the prominent scenarios envisioned in the fifth generation (5G) of wireless communication and beyond. The third generation partnership project (3GPP) has identified the narrowband IoT (NB-IoT) standard as a candidate technology for NTN-based IoT. In this work, an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based NTN using NB-IoT for communication is considered, where multiple UAVs act as relays between user equipment (UEs) and the terrestrial base station (BS). The communication procedure for the proposed system is designed by building upon the uplink and downlink communication procedures of a single UAV-aided system. To facilitate efficient data transmission between UAVs and the BS, scheduling mechanisms using orthogonal multiple access (OMA) and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) are explored. For the NOMA scheduling mechanism, two grouping strategies are proposed for UAVs - static grouping and dynamic grouping, where the latter leverages the longest transmission time first (LTTF) algorithm for optimal grouping. Closed-form expressions for average transmission time are derived under both strategies, accounting for UE distribution modeled by discrete uniform, Poisson, and Beta distributions. Finally, we perform end-to-end physical layer simulations using 3GPP-compliant channel models and evaluate the latency and energy consumption of NB-IoT UEs. It is observed from simulations that NOMA, along with dynamic grouping, offers lower latency and reduced energy consumption when compared to OMA and static grouping, validating its suitability for multi-UAV-based NTN applications.

title: Wideband Circularly-Polarized Phased Array with Multi-Bit Phase Encoding for Wide-Angle Scanning and RCS Reduction
authors: Xiaoyu Li, Xiaoyi Wang, Xiaojie Lu, Bo Wang, Mei Song Tong
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a compact circularly polarized (CP) phased array that integrates wide-angle scanning with wideband in-band radar cross-section (RCS) reduction. A multi-bit CP radiating element is proposed based on the Pancharatnam-Berry (PB) phase principle, achieving flexible phase encoding within a compact sub-wavelength periodicity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.4\lambda _{0}$</tex-math></inline-formula> (at 10 GHz). This work employs a 2-bit <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\times 8$</tex-math></inline-formula> heterogeneous fusion array configuration-combining linear gradients, subarray rotation, and a destructive interference core-to co-optimize radiation and scattering. Measurements demonstrate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm 60^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> scanning with axial ratios (ARs) below 3 dB, scan loss under 3.4 dB, and 21.7 dBic peak gain. For RCS reduction, the array achieves over 10 dB reduction across 20.7% (8.83–10.9 GHz) and 26.4% (9.08–11.72 GHz) bandwidths for X- and Y-polarizations under normal incidence, respectively. Furthermore, under <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-60^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> oblique incidence, it maintains effective 10 dB reduction bandwidths of 12.4% (9.56–11.8 GHz) and 17.6% (8.94–10.7 GHz). This work offers a novel solution for co-optimizing multi-bit phase encoding for scattering control and high-performance radiation in compact arrays.

title: Pseudorandom Time-Modulated Metasurface Cloaked Target Detection based on Encoded Modulation Sequence Reconstruction
authors: Guonan Cui, Zhaolin Zhang, Jia Su, Chong He, Mingliang Tao, Shiyuan Li, Ling Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: Time-modulated metasurfaces (TMMs) encoded by pseudorandom sequences (PS) present emerging challenges to radar detection by spreading the echo spectrum and defocusing target energy after matched filtering. As a result, the radar receiver fails to reconstruct the correct range profile, severely degrading detection performance. To address this issue, this paper proposes a single-channel phase-domain reconstruction framework for non-cooperative 1-bit PS-TMM cloaked targets, where the metasurface elements switch between two phase states governed by a pseudorandom binary sequence. Unlike multi-channel or high-dimensional solutions, the proposed method requires only one receiving channel, offering low computational complexity, low hardware cost, and ease of implementation, while maintaining high accuracy. For 1-bit PS-TMM cloaked targets with unknown range and velocity, the algorithm compensates for range–velocity coupling terms and reconstructs the encoded modulation sequence directly in the time domain. The effectiveness of the method is validated through theoretical analysis, simulations, and experimental measurements under various conditions. Results demonstrate that the proposed single-channel algorithm can effectively recover the spectral distribution of 1-bit PS-TMM echoes and restore high-resolution range profiles, showing robust performance across different modulation rates, non-ideal conditions, and multi-target scenarios.

title: A Direction-Dependent Mechanotransduction Model to Convert Fingertip Forces into Neural Spike Trains for Tactile Feedback
authors: Hope O. Shaw, John McBride, Liudi Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Haptics
abstract: Microneurography studies have shown that human mechanoreceptor (MR) activity is directionally sensitive to shear forces, enabling fine tactile perception and object manipulation. However, existing computational mechanotransduction models largely neglect this directional tuning, limiting their biological realism and effectiveness for tactile feedback systems such as prosthetic hands. This paper presents a Direction-Dependent Mechanotransduction Model (DDMM) that replicates the direction-specific encoding behavior observed in human tactile afferents. The model integrates multidirectional pressure and shear forces to modulate neural spiking according to the alignment between resultant shear vectors and neuron-specific attenuation profiles. Force inputs are first transformed into afferent-specific currents (SAI, RAI, RAII), which are then converted into spike trains using an Izhikevich neuron model. Simulated fingertip interactions produced directionally selective spiking frequencies ranging from 0 to 47.5 pulses per second, consistent with biological firing ranges. Directional tuning, quantified using the profile-resolved sensitivity index (PRSI), yielded values of 0.31–0.45 for selective and broad profiles, comparable with those experimentally measured directional sensitivity indices (DSI; 0.23 ± 0.18) as reported in the literature. Further experimental validation using triaxial force measurements from human fingertip press–push–lift actions confirm the model's directional sensitivity, with aligned neural attenuation profiles and shear force direction yielding a mean spiking frequency increase of approximately 350% relative to misaligned conditions. These findings establish the DDMM as a biologically inspired and computationally efficient framework for encoding tactile force direction, with potential applications in neuroprosthetics, robotic manipulation, and somatosensory modeling.

title: Importance-Aware Dynamic Compression for Efficient Diffusion Models Inference
authors: Cheng Gu, Gang Li, Jiayao Ling, Xiaolong Lin, Zhuoran Song, Jian Cheng, Xiaoyao Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Diffusion models (DMs) exhibit remarkable generative performance within AI. Despite their outstanding generative capability, DMs still face two major limitations in fast and energy-efficient edge deployment: 1) the large number of parameters in DMs introduces substantial computational overhead during a single inference; 2) The iterative denoising property of DMs requires multiple inference steps, which further amplifies both computational and memory overhead. To alleviate the bottlenecks of DMs described above, we propose an importance-aware dynamic compression framework for hardware-efficient inference of DMs. We design importance metrics for steps, blocks, and layers in DMs based on the observation that not all denoising modules are equally important for a specific input. Guided by importance metrics, we propose a dynamic pruning method to eliminate redundant blocks during iterative denoising, while simultaneously applying binary quantization to the weights and mixed-precision quantization to the activations. To address the accuracy degradation introduced by the compression framework, we further propose tailored supervision losses and novel connection modes to improve the semantic fidelity and perceptual quality of the generated images. Given the binary and mixed-precision operations, along with the dynamic changes in model topology and computation flow due to pruning, we design a dedicated accelerator to more effectively supports our compression framework. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a maximum pruning acceleration of 1.21× and a quantization bit-width of W2.05A4.59. The hardware implementation achieves 2.3× speedup and 76% energy savings compared to the baseline.

title: GrainMIL: Automated Grain Damage Classification from Weak Labels via Deep Multiple Instance Learning
authors: Bradley Ezard, Ling Li, Senjian An
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Visual analysis is essential for ensuring the quality and safety of cereal grains. However, the traditional manual process is subjective and inconsistent which motivates the need for automated systems offering better repeatability and throughput. Deep learning methods have shown strong results in many classification tasks in other domains, however they often require extensive instance-level annotations. The numerous causes of grain damage, rarity of many damage types, and variety of contaminants makes such methods difficult to apply to grain quality analysis. The high cost of obtaining annotations at scale, creates a major barrier to deploying automation in the grain industry. Standard industry visual assessment practices produce a report of damages present in each grain sample. This paper proposes a novel deep multiple instance learning pipeline, named GrainMIL, that leverages the damage report information as a weak annotation to train assessment models without further manual annotation. A neural network is trained with weak annotations derived from damage reports using Multiple Instance Learning. A recursive propagation algorithm is then applied to transfer annotations to the instance level. Another neural network is then trained using the propagated labels and a robust loss to further increase accuracy. The multiple instance learning phase is evaluated on a dataset constructed from public, supervised benchmark data across four major cereal grains (Wheat, Rice, Sorghum, and Maize). Experimental results show strong performance at both the sample and instance level across a wide range of sample sizes, indicating a viable approach to exploit the more easily acquired weak annotations.

title: Not All Data are What You Need: A Data-Efficient Training Method Using Heterogeneous Hardware
authors: Zulong Diao, Mingyu Qiao, Xin Wang, Guangxing Zhang, Wei Liang, Jianguo Chen, Changhua Pei, Yanbiao Li, Zhenyu Li, Gaogang Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Deep learning is applied to various tasks, such as image recognition and self-driving. Training acceleration is crucial for the further development of deep learning, as efficient training algorithms can help greatly reduce the time consumption and hardware usage while making real-time updates of large-scale deep learning models possible. The mainstream methods realize it through distributed training or network pruning. The former relies on abundant hardware resources and the latter may suffer from a non-negligible performance drop. In this paper, we propose CORESTR, a data-efficient training framework that asynchronously utilizes heterogeneous hardware resources. The framework consists of two major procedures. We first characterize the training status of each instance and propose a representative instance selection algorithm for reducing the total number of instances participating in each epoch of training. In the second procedure, we design a lightweight sample weighting mechanism based on meta-learning to closely approximate the convergence quality using a representative instance set selected from the full training dataset. We present the theoretical rationale for our approach and evaluate its training performance with several classical models and datasets. Experiment results demonstrate that our training method can achieve an average speedup of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.8\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> and reach a higher final accuracy compared with state-of-the-art methods by only relying on a small part of the training data.

title: Special Issue on Quantum Networks
authors: Qun Li, Marcello Caleffi, Yu Wang
publish: IEEE Internet Computing
abstract: Quantum networking exploits superposition and entanglement to enable secure communication, distributed computing, and advanced sensing. As the field moves toward practical realization, it requires interdisciplinary advances spanning physics, computer science, and network engineering, along with system-level innovations in architecture, protocols, and resource management. This special issue of IEEE Internet Computing highlights recent progress in the design, implementation, and application of quantum networks.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Industrial Electronics Magazine

title: Detection and profiling of dataset bias in CNN analysis of images
authors: Eder Martinez, Michael Okonoda, Abhilekha Dalal, Lior Shamir
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have become the most common approach for automatic image classification. However, their complex and unintuitive nature makes them vulnerable to biases that are very difficult to pinpoint, and can mislead even experienced researchers. Here we study and profile possible reasons leading to such CNN bias. Experiments are performed to mimic the generation of image datasets, leading to high classification accuracy even when no information about the objects is present in these images. The analysis shows that even when using the exact same conditions for all images, CNN architectures can still provide classification accuracy far higher than mere chance. In fact, these biases are common in many of the commonly used benchmark datasets. The bias depends on the order of imaging, the use of camera settings, the room temperature, and the CNN architecture. The datasets created for the experiments are publicly available.

title: Adaptive Multimodal Semantic Balancing Framework for Sentiment Analysis
authors: Jiajia Tang, Feiwei Zhou, Xiping Wang, Jianhai Zhang, Qibin Zhao, Yu Ding, Wanzeng Kong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Multimodal sentiment analysis is crucial in the existing research area. However, existing methods leverage the same static multimodal fusion order to deal with all samples, which ignores the impact of the sample variability issue. This fails to dynamically balance the sentiment semantics among multiple modalities for distinct samples. That is, the fixed modality fusion order may not be optimal for all types of samples, resulting in the suboptimal sentiment analysis. In this paper, we proposed an adaptive multimodal semantic balancing framework (MSBF) for sentiment analysis task. The above architecture consists of two essential part, the gated residual attention mechanism and mini-batch design. Specifically, the gated residual self-attention mechanism is proposed to investigate the significance and contribution of each modality, leading to the specific modality fusion order. Then, the gated residual cross-modality attention mechanism is utilized to integrate multiple modalities in a hierarchical manner. This allows us to effectively eliminate the semantic bias to some extend and adaptively balance the sentiment semantics according to the corresponding fusion order. Moreover, the mini-batch based strategy is leveraged to further handle the sample variability and facilitate the robustness of the multimodal semantic balancing procedure. The proposed adaptive multimodal semantic balancing design may provide the novel analysis paradigm for the multimodal learning and sentiment analysis. To evaluate the performance of the proposed model, the public multimodal sentiment analysis benchmark was introduced to perform the corresponding experiments. The experiments demonstrated that our model can obtain state-of-the-art performance.

title: A Hybrid Data-Augmented Deep Learning Pipeline for Breast Tumor Diagnosis
authors: Vemula Mounika, Martha Sheshikala
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Breast cancer one of the life-threatening diseases in women worldwide. Advancements in screening programs and treatment strategies, mortality rates remain high, particularly in resource-constrained environments, due to delayed diagnosis and limited access to accurate screening methods. This research proposed a deep learning framework for automated breast tumor classification using histopathological images. The framework employed EfficientNetB4 as the backbone, enhanced with extensive data augmentation and a class-weighted training strategy to effectively manage imbalanced datasets. The dataset Grayscale images are preprocessed, normalized, and replicated to match the RGB input format required by the pretrained model. A customized classification integrating dropout, batch normalization, and L2 regularization is designed to reduce overfitting and improve generalization. The proposed model achieved training accuracy of 98%, validation accuracy of 66– 70%, an AUC of 0.77, and an optimal F1-score of 0.67.

title: SecureMind-Net: A Context-Aware Adaptive Agent Framework for Personalized Cybersecurity Threat Mitigation Using Neuro-Symbolic AI and Federated Learning
authors: Blaise Joseph, B. Lavaraju, S.Thangamayan, R Roshini
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Cybersecurity threats are evolving rapidly, necessitating intelligent defense systems that adapt to user-specific behavior and environmental context. This paper presents SecureMind-Net, a novel context-aware adaptive agent framework that integrates neuro-symbolic reasoning with federated learning for personalized cybersecurity threat mitigation. Unlike traditional static or rule-based systems, SecureMind-Net continuously profiles user behavior and system interactions using GRU-based context encoders and semantic reasoning ontologies to dynamically detect threats. A Transformer-based encoder extracts latent threat signatures, while an OWL-based symbolic module provides explainable reasoning over contextual metadata and policy violations. The hybrid neuro-symbolic threat detection achieves superior accuracy by fusing neural and symbolic predictions through a confidence-weighted decision gate. Personalized risk mitigation is performed using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), which adapts mitigation strategies like quarantine, alerting, or process isolation to user-specific risk profiles. Federated learning ensures decentralized, privacy-preserving model updates using secure aggregation and differential privacy, eliminating the need to share raw data across clients. Experimental results on the CICIDS2017 and TON_IoT datasets show that SecureMind-Net achieves an overall detection accuracy of 97.6%, outperforming conventional models including CNN (89.7%), Bi-LSTM (91.5%), and Transformer-only models (93.6%). The system also demonstrates low false-positive rates (1.8%) and fast inference times (28.3 ms), making it suitable for real-time deployment. The framework's ability to learn personalized behavior profiles significantly reduces alert fatigue and improves precision across diverse user roles. SecureMind-Net represents a significant advancement in intelligent, adaptive, and privacy-preserving cybersecurity systems.

title: Real-Time Ransomware Detection Using ML-Based Behavioral Profiling
authors: Abdul Hadi, Krishna Chaganti, Viraj Soni, Mahmood A Hussain, Prayas Lohalekar
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Ransomware has become one of the most widespread and harmful types of cyberattacks, causing serious risks in the integrity of data, the availability of services, and financial sustainability. The use of standard signature-based and static analysis methods does not provide the ability to detect new or polymorphic ransomware variants in real time, highlighting the importance of adaptive and intelligent defence mechanisms. The ransomware detection framework proposed in the paper is based on machine learning behavioural profiling in real-time. The proposed system records the patterns of fine-grained process level and file-system activities such as unusual encryption rates, unusual I/O operations and unusual access sequences and assemble them into dynamic behavioural signatures. With the help of supervised and ensemble learning algorithms, including Random Forests and Gradient Boosted Models, the framework will be able to identify benign applications and ransomware with high precision and small false-positive rates. Besides, a lightweight streaming pipeline allows a real-time monitor with a small latency, so the strategy is appropriate to implement in a cloud and enterprise environment. Benchmark ransomware datasets are experimentally tested to show that the proposed model has a high level of detection when compared to the baseline static and heuristic approaches. This paper demonstrates the effectiveness of behavioural machine learning models in proactive ransomware defence as an effective and scalable and adaptive solution against the changing cyber threats.

title: A Computational Model for Drug-Target Interaction
authors: Dhanalakshmi R, N. Vijayaraghavan, R. Rajesh
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: Drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction is one of the important aspects in drug discovery. This allows researchers to find potential therapeutic drugs and permits screening large libraries of compounds in an efficient manner. Neural network-based approaches have advanced DTI prediction by capturing complex patterns within molecular data, but such models are often suffering from a lack of interpretability. This study proposes a DTI model with that adds model transparency. The model uses BPE to decompose molecular sequences into substructures that can be interpreted so that one may understand which molecular features contribute to the interaction prediction. We pair ESPF with a hybrid model combining transformer-based encoders and CNNs for the ability to capture the sequential dependencies and spatial relationships of drug-protein interaction data. Experimental results on established datasets demonstrate that this model not only achieves competitive predictive performance but also sheds light on the substructural basis of drug-target interactions. This paper offers a sound foundation for further research in more interpretable DTI models that could significantly benefit drug repurposing and precision medicine.

title: Hybrid K-Means–Fuzzy Logic Model for Automated Brain Tumour Segmentation and Classification from MRI Images
authors: S. Rajalakshmi, Suman Vashist, Manikandan S, Rvs Praveen, Rashi Saraswat, Saloni Martolia
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: The detection and classification of brain tumours continue to pose significant issues in medical imaging, since accurate segmentation of tumour regions directly influences clinical decision-making and patient survival rates. Conventional clustering methods like K-Means and Fuzzy C-Means, despite their computing efficiency, sometimes encounter difficulties with intensity variations and uncertain borders. The proposed paper presents a Hybrid K-Means–Fuzzy Logic model to automate brain tumour segmentation and classification from MRI images, addressing current challenges. The suggested method initially uses K-Means clustering for segmentation, thereafter utilising fuzzy logic refinement to enhance edge detection and minimise misclassification errors. A fuzzy inference method is utilised for the classification of tumours into benign, low-grade, and high-grade gliomas. Experimental evaluation of the publicly accessible Kaggle MRI datasets reveals enhanced performance relative to baseline models, attaining 97.8% accuracy, 97.5% precision, 98.1% recall, 97.8% F1-score, and 96.9% Dice similarity factor. The proposed framework exceeds the performance of K-Means, FCM, and CNN-based U-Net models while improving interpretability through fuzzy membership values, providing a clinically reliable and computationally efficient solution. The study underscores the promise of hybrid intelligent systems in medical imaging and facilitates future integration with multimodal MRI and deep learning to improve diagnostic accuracy.

title: Evaluating Continuous-Time Recurrent Neural Networks for State-Dependent EEG Forecasting
authors: Rakesh Sengupta
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: We investigate the capacity of a lightweight continuous-time recurrent neural network (CTRNN) to forecast short-term EEG dynamics and benchmark its performance against classical and deep-learning baselines. Using publicly available Motor Movement/Imagery EEG data, we extracted three central channels (C3, Cz, C4), applied common-average referencing, band-pass filtering (8–15 Hz), and downsampling to 100 Hz. Sliding windows of 500 ms history were normalized per channel and used to predict the subsequent 200 ms of multivariate EEG. The CTRNN, featuring 64 hidden units with heterogeneous time constants and Euler integration, was trained via backpropagation through time. We compared it to an autoregressive VAR(5) model, simple last-value and moving-average predictors, a feed-forward neural network (FFNN), and an LSTM. On a held-out 20% validation set, the FFNN achieved the lowest mean-squared error (24.7 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), followed by the LSTM (33.6 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) and CTRNN (41.0 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>). All neural models outperformed classical baselines (VAR: 45.6 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>; moving-average: 46.3 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>; last-value: 94.4 μV<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>). Errors were consistently higher during eyes-closed versus eyes-open segments, reflecting more pronounced alpha oscillations. Our findings demonstrate that even a small CTRNN can capture essential temporal structure in continuous EEG and, with further tuning, offers a competitive, interpretable alternative to black-box sequence models for real-time neurotechnology applications.

title: A Comprehensive Study on Sentimental Social Media Analysis of Vaccine Discourse
authors: Teena A James, Shreenath Acharya
publish: 2025 2nd Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT)
abstract: In the 21st century, immunization is coined to be one among the prominent achievements in public health domain, as it could demonstrate tremendous efficacy in controlling outbreaks, reducing mortality and immunizing generations at population level. This was clear in recent 2017 outbreak which led to the introduction of the measles-rubella vaccines. However, even with clear evidence of its benefits, many communities still struggle with challenges like vaccine hesitancy and the spread of misinformation. WHO emphasize digital platform like Twitter, Facebook as a powerful driver to analyze public vaccine discourse, enabling both constructive dialogue and rapid dissemination of misleading narratives. In this review paper we tried to put light on recent studies that used advanced language models to understand emotions and sentiments especially in regions with linguistic diversity like India, where one-size-fits-all messaging often fails. This review effectively analyzes vaccine sentiment trends over time by identifying problems earlier, respond to concerns and make efforts to build trust through healthy campaigns or other engagement approaches. It aims to gain deeper insights into the nature of humongous data source and understand how to process it effectively. Moreover, it recommends more adaptive NLP pipelines designed to interpret diverse languages and growing misinformation patterns, build opinion tailoring mechanism to improve public health messaging and build vaccine trust across communities.

title: Research on Impact Response and Protection Technologies of Transmission Line Breakage
authors: Wei Zhang, Meipeng Chen, Zhen Chen, Haijun Du
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Control Engineering (ICPECE)
abstract: Transmission lines are essential components of modern power systems, and their mechanical and electrical stability are directly related to the safety and reliability of grid operation. When a conductor breakage occurs, the resulting impact response may cause severe vibration, equipment damage, and even cascading failures. To address this issue, this paper conducts a systematic study on the impact response characteristics of transmission line breakage and explores relevant key technologies. Through theoretical modeling and finite element simulation, the dynamic behavior of conductors under sudden breakage conditions is analyzed, and a fitting formula for calculating the maximum falling height of conductors under different spans is derived, providing a theoretical basis and technical support for the safe and stable operation of modern power grids.

title: Data Center Prosumers in Wholesale Power Markets: SCUC-SCED Integrated Clearing and Settlement Design
authors: Zhanhua Pan, Huan Quan, Zhichuan Ma, Zhong Fan, Zhaoxia Jing, Dawei Qiu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: This paper presents a near-deployable market framework that integrates data-center prosumers into two-settlement wholesale electricity markets. The design embeds bidirectional data-center offers within a day-ahead (DA) SCUC/SCED and a real-time (RT) rolling SCED, and proposes a settlement scheme that combines neutral DA-RT deviations with penalised RT-metered deviations. All components rely on constructs widely used by independent system operators (ISOs)—piecewise energy blocks, three-part generator costs, nodal locational marginal prices (LMPs), and 15-minute inter-vals—requiring only modest extensions to current offer formats and settlement rules. Case studies on a modified IEEE 24-bus system with four data centers demonstrate coordinated prosumer behaviour (import at low prices, export around mid-day), reduced peak price pressure, and accurate delivery enforced by deviation penalties. The results indicate operational feasibility and provide a practical pathway toward pilot implementation in actual markets.

title: Black-Start Strategy for Grid-Connected Microgrid Based on ESS-PV-Load Power Balance Control
authors: Xinjie Zeng, Ying Xue, Yun Li, Shuang Huang, Ilkhomjon Kholiddinov, Shoirbek Olimov
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the high penetration of renewable energy, the vulnerability of DS (Distribution Station) under extreme disasters has become increasingly prominent. Although equipping ESS (Energy Storage System) can provide regulation capacity for DS integrated with renewable energy, significant challenges remain during black start, such as limited ESS capacity and the dynamic matching between response characteristics for PV (Photovoltaic) and load. Furthermore, the coordination mechanism among PV, ESS, and load is still underdeveloped, and the lack of effective coordinated control strategies results in low renewable energy utilization, slow power supply recovery, and insufficient operational resilience. Based on this, this paper proposes a microgrid structure with coordinated control of PV, ESS, and load. After the DS is disconnected from the distribution transformer, ESS supports voltage and frequency during the initial black start phase. PV adjusts its active power operation mode based on the SOC (State of Charge) of the ESS and the load demand. This prioritizes the consumption of renewable energy while preventing power backflow, which could lead to overcharging of the ESS. This approach can achieve efficient coordination among the ESS, PV, and load. Simulation and experimental results show that the proposed method can effectively enhance the self-recovery capability of the DS and the utilization rate of renewable energy, providing a feasible solution for the resilience improvement of DS.

title: Multi-Objective Volt/Var Control of Distribution Network Incorporating Inverter Lifetime Enhancement
authors: Yuxia Hua, Cuo Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: With the growing penetration of photovoltaic (PV) systems in distribution networks, inverter based Volt/Var control (VVC) can enhance system operational efficiency while keeping bus voltage constraints. Conventional methods mainly minimize network power losses or voltage deviations, and neglect long-term thermal degradation and impaired lifetime of inverters. This paper develops a multi-objective inverter-based VVC optimization model jointly minimizing thermal stress impacts on inverter lifetime and network power losses. Temperature of capacitor core junction is modeled and used as a “thermal cost” which has negative impacts on ensuring inverter lifetime. The multi-objective optimization model is converted into a single-objective equivalent model and solved efficiently. Case studies on a 33-bus test system verify the effectiveness of the proposed model in balancing VVC performance and inverter lifetime enhancement with analysis on a Pareto front. Comprehensive studies provide valuable insights to the design of inverter-based VVC considering the inverter lifetime enhancement.

title: A Self-Variable Voltage Fault Ride-Through Method for Virtual Synchronous Generators
authors: Yulin Xia, Leming Zhou, Zhiqiang Xiao, Jiansheng Hu, Jianxin Meng, Jin Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of VSGs (virtual synchronous generators) in dealing with grid voltage sags under the new distribution network, such as poor short-term current limiting capability, difficulty in returning from faults and difficulty in coping with deep voltage sags, this paper proposes a self-variable voltage fault ride-through method for virtual synchronous generators. Through the introduction of the current judgement detection link to indirectly identify the grid three-phase grounded short-circuit fault information, and through the introduction of the variable voltage adjustment link to adaptively switch between the fault prediction voltage command and the outer power loop voltage command to achieve the fault prediction voltage command close to the fault steady state output voltage, thus eliminating the adverse impact of the grid fault detection triggered by the fault current impact of virtual synchronous generators, and effectively suppressing the fault current spike amplitude, achieving highly reliable fault ride-through of the VSG during faults. The simulation results verify the high reliability of self-variable voltage fault ride-through method over the full voltage transient range.

title: An Improved UNet++ Approach in Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring
authors: Jiangliang Jin, Ming Tang, Gui Gu, Xue-Qin Jiang, Shubo Zhou, Genlong Chen, Kailian Deng
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Non-intrusive load monitoring aims to identify individual appliance operational states and energy consumption from aggregated power signals, serving as a critical technique for enhancing energy management efficiency. To address decomposition requirements in low-frequency power data scenarios, this study develops a deep learning model based on an improved UNet++ architecture. In the encoder design, a lightweight spatial and channel reconstruction convolution module is introduced to strengthen discriminative capabilities for key load features. Additionally, bidirectional long short-term memory network is also used to capture the temporal dynamics characteristics and combined with efficient channel attention network to realize the adaptive calibration of the feature channel weights. The decoder employs a parallel structure with dense skip connections to integrate multi-scale features, effectively improving disaggregation accuracy. The experimental results on the UK-DALE dataset show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of mean absolute error.

title: Coordinated Design of Frequency Control Parameters of VSCs via Analytical Description of AC Fault Currents
authors: Sijie Wu, Xiuhuan Wang, Liwen Zheng, Yuexi Yang, Shan Liu, Dajiang Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies - Asia (ISGT Asia)
abstract: Frequency supportive control from DC/AC voltage source converters (VSCs) is vital for the frequency stability of local AC power grids with a high penetration rate of VSCs. The active power balance and rating of active power output are the primary factors considered in the design of traditional frequency coordinated control system. However, increasing engineering practice has observed that the frequency stability of the system is impacted by the fault transient process of the VSC, which would result in cascaded tripping of VSCs and threatens the active power balance in the system. To fill this research gap, this paper proposes to optimize the frequency supportive control loop of VSCs based on analytical analysis of the fault currents considering the impact of frequency control loops. The analytical expressions of VSC operated in gridfollowing mode is derived, and numerical results are obtained to show the impact of controller parameters on fault current dynamics. Results presented in this paper can provide guidance for the optimal frequency control parameter design in multiple VSCs integrated system, including both grid-forming and gridfollowing converters, for optimal frequency support performance.
